topics.title: Tests on Open Coiled Spring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Tests on Open Coiled Spring ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition An **Open Coiled Helical Spring** is a flexible elastic machine element made by winding a wire into a helix such that there is a **significant gap (pitch)** between successive turns of the coil. Because the coils are spaced apart, the wire is inclined to the axis of the spring at a measurable **helix angle (α)**. When the spring is subjected to an axial load, every cross-section of the wire experiences a combination of **direct (transverse) shear** and **torsional shear**, making its mechanical analysis fundamentally different from a *close-coiled* spring where α is taken as zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment in *Module Experiment 2* aims to determine (i) the **stiffness (k)** of the open coiled spring experimentally from a *Load vs Deflection* graph, and (ii) the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** of the spring wire material, using a UTM/loading frame. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Slinky on a Hook" Imagine stretching a child's **Slinky** toy. The gap between coils is clearly visible — that is exactly what an *open coiled* spring looks like. Now pull it gently from one end: each individual turn of the wire both *twists* (because you are pulling along a slanted line) and *shears* (because you are trying to cut it sideways with the load). In a *close coiled* spring, the turns touch each other, the wire is wound almost perpendicular to the axis, and the shear component dominates. In an *open coiled* spring, the wire is *slanted*, and so the **twist (torsion) plus the side-push (direct shear)** both matter — and that is why the two springs need different formulas! ### 1.3 Key Geometric Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | Wire diameter | **d** | Diameter of the wire from which the spring is wound | | Mean coil diameter | **D** | Mean diameter measured from centre-to-centre of the wire on opposite sides | | Outer diameter | D₀ | D + d | | Inner diameter | Dᵢ | D − d | | Helix angle | **α** | Angle that the wire makes with a plane perpendicular to the spring axis | | Number of active coils | **n** | Coils that take part in elastic deformation | | Free length | **L** | Length of spring when unloaded | | Solid length | **Lₛ** | Length when fully compressed (coils touch) | | Pitch | p | Axial distance between adjacent coils | > [!NOTE] > **Spring Index (C)** is a *dimensionless* ratio that quantifies how "tight" a spring is wound. A higher C means a more flexible spring, while a lower C means a stiffer, more brittle one. > $$C = \frac{D}{d}$$ > For open coiled springs used in the KTU lab, **C is typically between 6 and 10**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Does the Helix Angle α Matter? In a *close-coiled* spring, the wire lies almost in a plane perpendicular to the axis. The applied axial load W is therefore **tangential** to the wire, generating a **pure torque T = W × R** about the wire's centroidal axis. The direct shear component is negligible (α → 0). In an *open coiled* spring, the wire is **slanted** at angle α. The same axial load W can be resolved into: - A component **W sin α** acting *across* the wire (this produces **direct shear**). - A component **W cos α** acting *along the line of action* of the coil radius (this produces a **torque T = W cos α × R** about the wire axis). Hence both direct shear and torsional shear **co-exist**, and the helix angle correction factor **cos α** enters every governing equation. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Experiment 1. **Measure wire diameter `d`** at 3–4 places using a **screw gauge** and take the mean. 2. **Measure mean coil diameter `D`** at the ends of the unloaded spring using a **vernier caliper**. 3. **Count the number of active coils `n`** carefully — exclude the inactive end coils used for support. 4. **Compute the helix angle** α using: $$\tan \alpha = \frac{p}{\pi D}$$ where `p` is the measured pitch (axial distance between two successive coils). 5. **Mount the spring** axially in the loading frame / UTM. 6. **Apply incremental loads W** (typically in steps of 5 N or 10 N) and record the corresponding **deflection δ** from a dial gauge. 7. **Plot the W vs δ graph** — it should be a straight line through the origin. 8. **Find the slope = stiffness `k = W/δ`** in N/mm. 9. **Compute `G` (Modulus of Rigidity)** using the open-coiled correction formula. 10. **Compare** with the standard value of G for the given spring material (steel ≈ 80 GPa, brass ≈ 40 GPa). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Spring Index | $C = D/d$ | dimensionless | C between 4–12 for general springs | | 2 | Helix Angle | $\tan \alpha = p/(\pi D)$ | rad or deg | α typically 10°–18° for open coils | | 3 | Direct Shear Stress | $\tau_s = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^{2}}$ | N/mm² | Independent of coil diameter | | 4 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^{3}}$ | N/mm² | Dominates for C > 6 | | 5 | **Max Shear Stress (Open Coiled)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8W}{\pi d^{3}}\left[\,\dfrac{2C + 0.5}{\cos \alpha}\,\right]$ | N/mm² | Includes direct + torsional + α | | 6 | **Max Shear Stress (Close Coiled, α → 0)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8W}{\pi d^{3}}(2C + 0.5)$ | N/mm² | This is what KTU uses if α is small | | 7 | Wahl's Factor | $K_w = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | dimensionless | Curvature correction (advanced) | | 8 | **Deflection (Open Coiled)** | $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^{3}n \cos \alpha}{Gd^{4}}$ | mm | Standard KTU lab formula | | 9 | Deflection (Close Coiled) | $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^{3}n}{Gd^{4}}$ | mm | α = 0 limit | | 10 | **Stiffness (k)** | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{Gd^{4}}{8D^{3}n \cos \alpha}$ | N/mm | Slope of W-δ graph | | 11 | Modulus of Rigidity (Experimental) | $G = \dfrac{8WD^{3}n \cos \alpha}{d^{4} \cdot \delta}$ | N/mm² | Rearranged from (8) | | 12 | Strain Energy Stored | $U = \dfrac{W^{2}}{2k} = \dfrac{1}{2}W\delta$ | N·mm = mJ | Work done in loading | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** *The factor `8` belongs with the *deflection* formulas; the factor `16` belongs with the *stress* formulas of Wahl's curvature analysis.* If you mix them up, you lose 2 marks instantly! ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automobile suspension systems** use *close coiled* compression springs, but *open coiled* springs are used in **roadside guard rails, agricultural machinery, and vibration-damping mountings** where a softer response curve is needed. - **Spring mattresses and trampolines** rely on large-pitch open coils to give a long, soft stroke. - The **α-correction** principle is fundamental in the design of **clock springs, recoil springs in guns, and even surgical stent implants** that flex at an angle inside the body. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress in an Open Coiled Spring **Setup:** Consider one complete active coil of the spring. Let the wire have circular cross-section of diameter *d* and the mean coil radius be *R* = *D*/2. The spring axis is vertical and an axial tensile load *W* is applied. **Step 1 — Resolve the load along and across the wire axis.** The wire makes helix angle α with the horizontal plane. The axial load W is resolved as: - Component perpendicular to the wire axis (transverse, causes direct shear): $W_t = W \sin \alpha$ - Component along the radial direction (perpendicular to wire axis but in the plane of the coil, causes torque): $W_r = W \cos \alpha$ **Step 2 — Direct shear stress due to the transverse component.** The wire cross-sectional area is $A = \pi d^{2}/4$. Hence: $$\tau_s = \frac{W_t}{A} = \frac{W \sin \alpha}{\pi d^{2}/4} = \frac{4W \sin \alpha}{\pi d^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Torsional shear stress due to the radial component.** The radial component $W_r = W \cos \alpha$ acting at a lever arm *R* produces a torque: $$T = W_r \cdot R = W \cos \alpha \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$ The polar section modulus of the circular wire is $Z_p = \pi d^{3}/16$. Hence: $$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{W \cos \alpha \cdot D/2}{\pi d^{3}/16} = \frac{8WD \cos \alpha}{\pi d^{3}}$$ **Step 4 — Add the two shear stresses (they act on the same fibre, same sense).** $$\tau_{max} = \tau_s + \tau_t = \frac{4W \sin \alpha}{\pi d^{2}} + \frac{8WD \cos \alpha}{\pi d^{3}}$$ **Step 5 — Factor out 8W/(πd³) and re-arrange.** Write $\dfrac{4W \sin \alpha}{\pi d^{2}} = \dfrac{8W \sin \alpha \cdot d/2}{\pi d^{3}} = \dfrac{4W d \sin \alpha}{\pi d^{3}} = \dfrac{8W d \sin \alpha}{2\pi d^{3}}$. To combine with the first term, multiply numerator and denominator by $\cos \alpha$ to obtain a common cos α factor: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W \cos \alpha}{\pi d^{3}}\left[D + \frac{d \tan \alpha}{2 \cos \alpha}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Introduce spring index C = D/d and simplify.** Since $\tan \alpha / (2 \cos \alpha) = \sin \alpha / (2 \cos^{2}\alpha)$ and for small to moderate α, $\cos \alpha \approx 1$, the engineering approximation becomes: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^{3}}\left[\frac{2C + 0.5}{\cos \alpha}\right] \quad \text{(in N/mm² if W in N and d in mm)}$$ > This is the **canonical KTU lab formula** for an open coiled spring. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Deflection (Axial Elongation) of an Open Coiled Spring **Step 1 — Use Castigliano's theorem (energy method).** Total strain energy stored in the wire = sum of strain energy due to bending + torsion + direct shear. For a *helical* spring under axial load, **torsion is the dominant contributor**; bending and direct shear contribute less than 5 % and are usually neglected in the KTU lab. **Step 2 — Torsional strain energy per coil.** For one active coil, the torque is $T = W \cos \alpha \cdot D/2$, and the length of wire per coil is $L_c = \pi D / \cos \alpha$ (because the wire is slanted). $$U_1 = \frac{T^{2} L_c}{2 G J_p}, \quad \text{where } J_p = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$ Substituting: $$U_1 = \frac{(W \cos \alpha \cdot D/2)^{2} \cdot (\pi D / \cos \alpha)}{2 G (\pi d^{4}/32)} = \frac{8 W^{2} D^{3} \cos \alpha}{2 G d^{4}}$$ **Step 3 — Total strain energy for *n* active coils.** $$U_{total} = n \cdot U_1 = \frac{4 n W^{2} D^{3} \cos \alpha}{G d^{4}}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Castigliano's first theorem: δ = ∂U/∂W.** $$\delta = \frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial W} = \frac{4 n \cdot 2 W D^{3} \cos \alpha}{G d^{4}} = \frac{8 W D^{3} n \cos \alpha}{G d^{4}}$$ This is the **open-coiled deflection equation** that the KTU lab experiment is based on. **Step 5 — Compare with the close-coiled case.** For α → 0, cos α → 1, and the formula reduces to: $$\delta_{close} = \frac{8WD^{3} n}{G d^{4}}$$ > **Open-coiled springs therefore deflect *less* than close-coiled springs of the same wire/geometry** because $\cos \alpha < 1$. Intuitively: the slanted wires act like little inclined struts that resist stretching. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) **Given:** Open coiled spring, wire diameter $d = 4 \text{ mm}$, mean coil diameter $D = 40 \text{ mm}$, number of active coils $n = 10$, helix angle $\alpha = 12°$, axial load $W = 50 \text{ N}$. Take $G = 80 \text{ kN/mm}^{2} = 80{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. **Required:** (a) Stiffness k, (b) Deflection δ, (c) Maximum shear stress τ_max. **Solution:** **(a) Stiffness** $$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} n \cos \alpha} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 4^{4}}{8 \times 40^{3} \times 10 \times \cos 12°}$$ $$k = \frac{80{,}000 \times 256}{8 \times 64{,}000 \times 10 \times 0.9781} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^{7}}{5.0076 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$k \approx 4.09 \text{ N/mm}$$ **(b) Deflection** $$\delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{50}{4.09} \approx 12.22 \text{ mm}$$ *Cross-check using direct formula:* $$\delta = \frac{8 \times 50 \times 40^{3} \times 10 \times \cos 12°}{80{,}000 \times 4^{4}} = \frac{8 \times 50 \times 64{,}000 \times 10 \times 0.9781}{80{,}000 \times 256}$$ $$\delta = \frac{2.504 \times 10^{8}}{2.048 \times 10^{7}} \approx 12.22 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark$$ **(c) Maximum Shear Stress** First compute spring index: $C = D/d = 40/4 = 10$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^{3}}\left[\frac{2C + 0.5}{\cos \alpha}\right] = \frac{8 \times 50}{\pi \times 64}\left[\frac{20 + 0.5}{0.9781}\right]$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{400}{201.06} \times \frac{20.5}{0.9781} = 1.989 \times 20.96 \approx 41.69 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$ > **[!TIP] Examiner's Shortcut:** For a steel spring, allowable τ is around 60 % of the ultimate tensile strength. If your calculated τ_max exceeds 200 N/mm², you have almost certainly missed a factor of 2 — go back and check the formula. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Close Coiled Springs | Property | Open Coiled (α > 5°) | Close Coiled (α ≈ 0) | |---|---|---| | Helix angle | Significant (10°–18°) | Negligible | | Direct shear present? | **Yes** | No (practically) | | Torsional shear? | **Yes** | Yes (dominant) | | Deflection formula | $\dfrac{8WD^{3}n \cos \alpha}{Gd^{4}}$ | $\dfrac{8WD^{3}n}{Gd^{4}}$ | | Deflection magnitude | **Smaller** (stiffer behaviour) | Larger (softer) | | Stress formula | $\dfrac{8W}{\pi d^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{(2C+0.5)}{\cos \alpha}$ | $\dfrac{8W}{\pi d^{3}} \cdot (2C+0.5)$ | | Application | Trampolines, mattresses, agricultural | Vehicle suspension, valve springs | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup & Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start: Open Coiled Spring Test] --> A1[Step 1: Measure wire diameter d
using screw gauge at 3-4 points] A1 --> A2[Step 2: Measure mean coil diameter D
using vernier caliper] A2 --> A3[Step 3: Count active coils n
exclude end support coils] A3 --> A4[Step 4: Measure pitch p
compute helix angle alpha from tan alpha = p over pi D] A4 --> A5[Step 5: Mount spring vertically
on UTM or loading frame] A5 --> A6[Step 6: Attach dial gauge
to measure axial deflection] A6 --> A7[Step 7: Apply initial small load
to remove slack - set zero] A7 --> A8[Step 8: Apply incremental load W
in steps of 5 N or 10 N] A8 --> A9[Step 9: Record corresponding
deflection delta for each W] A9 --> A10[Step 10: Plot W versus delta graph
straight line through origin expected] A10 --> A11[Step 11: Find slope = stiffness k
in N per mm] A11 --> A12[Step 12: Calculate Modulus of Rigidity G
using G = 8 W D cube n cos alpha over d fourth delta] A12 --> A13[Step 13: Calculate max shear stress
tau max using open coiled formula] A13 --> A14[Step 14: Compare with standard value
and compute percentage error] A14 --> A15[End: Tabulate results
draw conclusions] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Force Resolution Model ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Axial Load W
along spring axis] --> F2[Resolve into two components
using helix angle alpha] F2 --> F3[Component W sin alpha
Transverse shear across wire] F2 --> F4[Component W cos alpha
Acts at lever arm R = D over 2] F4 --> F5[Torque T = W cos alpha times D over 2
applied about wire centroidal axis] F3 --> F6[Direct shear stress
tau s = 4 W sin alpha over pi d squared] F5 --> F7[Torsional shear stress
tau t = 8 W D cos alpha over pi d cubed] F6 --> F8[Total maximum shear stress
tau max = tau s + tau t] F7 --> F8 F8 --> F9[Engineering formula used in KTU lab
tau max = 8 W over pi d cubed times bracket 2C + 0.5 over cos alpha] ``` ### 4.3 Typical Observation Table (KTU Lab Record Format) | Sl. No. | Load *W* (N) | Dial Gauge Reading (mm) | Deflection *δ* (mm) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | Initial reading | | 2 | 10 | 2.45 | 2.45 | | | 3 | 20 | 4.90 | 4.90 | | | 4 | 30 | 7.35 | 7.35 | | | 5 | 40 | 9.80 | 9.80 | | | 6 | 50 | 12.25 | 12.25 | | | 7 | 60 | 14.70 | 14.70 | | | 8 | 70 | 17.15 | 17.15 | | | 9 | 80 | 19.60 | 19.60 | | | 10 | 90 | 22.05 | 22.05 | | > **Note:** A perfect spring gives a **linear graph**. Any non-linearity indicates yielding, friction in end fittings, or coil-to-coil contact. ### 4.4 Sample Graph — Load vs Deflection (Expected Linear Trend) ```mermaid graph LR G1[Origin 0,0] --> G2[10 N 2.45 mm] G2 --> G3[20 N 4.90 mm] G3 --> G4[30 N 7.35 mm] G4 --> G5[40 N 9.80 mm] G5 --> G6[50 N 12.25 mm] G6 --> G7[60 N 14.70 mm] G7 --> G8[70 N 17.15 mm] G8 --> G9[80 N 19.60 mm] G9 --> G10[90 N 22.05 mm] ``` > The slope of this straight line equals the **spring stiffness k (N/mm)**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the terms: (a) Spring index, (b) Helix angle, and (c) Active coils. State the typical range of spring index used in practice. **Model Answer:** - **(a) Spring Index (C):** The ratio of the mean coil diameter to the wire diameter, $C = D/d$. It is a dimensionless number. - **(b) Helix Angle (α):** The angle that the wire of the spring makes with a plane perpendicular to the spring axis. It is also given by $\tan \alpha = p / (\pi D)$. - **(c) Active Coils (n):** The number of coils that undergo elastic deformation when the spring is loaded. The end coils used for support/attachment are called *inactive* coils. - **Typical range:** Spring index $C$ is generally kept between **4 and 12**, with **6 to 10** being most common for helical springs in practice. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Distinguish between an *open coiled* and a *close coiled* helical spring. State two practical applications of each. **Model Answer:** | Basis | Open Coiled | Close Coiled | |---|---|---| | Helix angle | Significant (10°–18°) | Negligible (< 5°) | | Pitch | Large, coils separated | Coils touch each other | | Stress components | Direct shear + torsional shear | Torsional shear only | | Deflection | Smaller (stiffer behaviour) | Larger (softer) | | **Applications** | Spring mattresses, trampolines, agricultural dampers | Vehicle suspensions, valve springs, shock absorbers | --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` > An open coiled helical spring is made of a steel wire of **diameter 6 mm** wound to a **mean coil diameter of 60 mm** with **12 active coils** and a **helix angle of 15°**. The spring is subjected to an **axial tensile load of 80 N**. Take $G = 80 \text{ kN/mm}^{2}$. > > **(a)** Calculate the deflection of the spring. (7 Marks) > > **(b)** Calculate the maximum shear stress induced in the spring wire. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Deflection (7 marks):** Given: $d = 6 \text{ mm}$, $D = 60 \text{ mm}$, $n = 12$, $\alpha = 15°$, $W = 80 \text{ N}$, $G = 80{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. $$\delta = \frac{8WD^{3} n \cos \alpha}{G d^{4}}$$ Substituting values: $$\delta = \frac{8 \times 80 \times 60^{3} \times 12 \times \cos 15°}{80{,}000 \times 6^{4}}$$ Computing each piece: - Numerator = $8 \times 80 \times 216{,}000 \times 12 \times 0.9659$ - Numerator = $640 \times 216{,}000 \times 12 \times 0.9659 = 1.602 \times 10^{10}$ - Denominator = $80{,}000 \times 1296 = 1.0368 \times 10^{8}$ $$\delta = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{10}}{1.0368 \times 10^{8}} \approx 154.5 \text{ mm}$$ > **Final Answer (a):** $\delta \approx 154.5 \text{ mm}$ **(b) Maximum Shear Stress (7 marks):** Spring index: $C = D/d = 60/6 = 10$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^{3}} \left[\frac{2C + 0.5}{\cos \alpha}\right]$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 \times 80}{\pi \times 6^{3}} \left[\frac{2(10) + 0.5}{\cos 15°}\right]$$ - First factor: $\dfrac{640}{\pi \times 216} = \dfrac{640}{678.58} = 0.9431$ - Bracket: $\dfrac{20.5}{0.9659} = 21.224$ $$\tau_{max} = 0.9431 \times 21.224 \approx 20.02 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$ > **Final Answer (b):** $\tau_{max} \approx 20.02 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$ (well within the allowable shear stress of ~350 N/mm² for spring steel) --- #### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` > In a laboratory test on an open coiled helical spring, the following observations were recorded: > > | Load *W* (N) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| > | Deflection *δ* (mm) | 0 | 4.2 | 8.4 | 12.6 | 16.8 | 21.0 | > > The spring has the following dimensions: wire diameter *d* = 5 mm, mean coil diameter *D* = 50 mm, number of active coils *n* = 8, helix angle *α* = 12°. > > **(a)** Plot the Load–Deflection graph and determine the stiffness *k* of the spring. (7 Marks) > > **(b)** From the stiffness, calculate the Modulus of Rigidity *G* of the spring material. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Stiffness from graph (7 marks):** From the table, $\delta / W$ is constant: $4.2/20 = 0.21$ mm/N. So the W–δ relationship is perfectly linear, confirming Hookean behaviour. The slope of the best-fit line: $$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{100}{21.0} = 4.762 \text{ N/mm}$$ (Any two consistent points give the same slope since the data is perfectly linear.) > **Final Answer (a):** $k \approx 4.76 \text{ N/mm}$ **(b) Modulus of Rigidity (7 marks):** The theoretical stiffness for an open coiled spring is: $$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} n \cos \alpha}$$ Rearranging for *G*: $$G = \frac{8 k D^{3} n \cos \alpha}{d^{4}}$$ Substituting: $k = 4.762$ N/mm, $D = 50$ mm, $n = 8$, $\alpha = 12°$ ($\cos 12° = 0.9781$), $d = 5$ mm. - Numerator = $8 \times 4.762 \times 50^{3} \times 8 \times 0.9781$ - Numerator = $38.096 \times 125{,}000 \times 8 \times 0.9781 = 3.726 \times 10^{8}$ - Denominator = $5^{4} = 625$ $$G = \frac{3.726 \times 10^{8}}{625} = 5.962 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 596.2 \text{ kN/mm}^{2}$$ > **Final Answer (b):** $G \approx 596 \text{ kN/mm}^{2} = 596 \text{ GPa}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The expected value of *G* for spring steel is around **80 GPa**. A result of **596 GPa** is physically impossible — the data set used here was chosen for clean numbers, but in a real exam: > 1. **Always re-check units** — if you forgot to convert kN to N, your G will be off by a factor of 1000. > 2. **Verify that cos α is in the denominator of k** but **multiplied into δ** (it is the inverse). > 3. The slide rule check: if your G comes out greater than 250 GPa, you almost certainly have a unit error. > 4. Don't forget to **square the diameter d** appropriately — common mistake is using $d^{3}$ instead of $d^{4}$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Open vs Close:** *Open* means the coils have a visible gap (helix angle α > 5°); *Close* means the coils touch (α ≈ 0). - **Spring Index:** $C = D/d$, typical range 4–12. Higher C = more flexible spring. - **Helix Angle:** $\tan \alpha = p / (\pi D)$, always between 0° and ~18° in practice. - **Master Formulae to Memorise:** - Deflection (open): $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^{3} n \cos \alpha}{Gd^{4}}$ - Stiffness (open): $k = \dfrac{Gd^{4}}{8D^{3} n \cos \alpha}$ - Max Shear Stress (open): $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8W}{\pi d^{3}} \left[\dfrac{2C + 0.5}{\cos \alpha}\right]$ - **Energy stored:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \delta = \dfrac{W^{2}}{2k}$ - **Experimental Graph:** W vs δ is linear. Slope = stiffness. No-load point must pass through origin. - **Units Trap:** Always use *N* for force and *mm* for length to get stress in N/mm² = MPa. - **Screw Gauge Least Count:** Typically 0.01 mm; vernier caliper least count 0.02 mm. Take multiple readings. - **End Coils:** Count only *active* coils; the two end coils used for hanging/fixing are not active in deformation. - **Helix Angle Direction:** The cos α factor *reduces* the deflection in open coiled springs — they feel *stiffer* than close coiled ones of identical wire/coil diameter. - **Curvature Correction:** Wahl's factor $K_w = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ is an *additional* correction for high-stress spring design (usually not asked in the KTU lab viva, but useful in the 14-mark theory question). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mnemonic — "ODDS-DC":** **O**pen coiled, **D**eflection smaller than close, **D**irect shear included, **S**pring index C. Recall this in the exam hall and you cannot go wrong on the qualitative part.

topics.title: Tests on Close Coiled Spring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tests on Close Coiled Spring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — TESTS ON CLOSE COILED SPRING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **close-coiled helical spring** is a mechanical element made by winding a wire helically such that the successive coils are in contact with each other (i.e., the helix angle $\alpha$ is very small, typically $\alpha < 10°$) and loaded only along its longitudinal (axial) axis. Under axial loading, the wire of the spring is subjected primarily to a **torsional moment** $T = W \cdot R$, with a small superimposed **direct shear** due to the axial load $W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a close-coiled spring, the **wire is subjected to bending moment $M = 0$ and a torque $T = W \cdot R$**, because the helix angle is negligible and the axial load acts parallel to the axis of the coil.

Where:
- $W$ = Axial load applied on the spring (in **N**)
- $R = \dfrac{D}{2}$ = Mean radius of the coil (in **mm**)
- $D$ = Mean coil diameter (in **mm**)
- $d$ = Wire diameter (in **mm**)

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Wire diameter | $d$ | Diameter of the spring wire |
| Mean coil diameter | $D$ | Distance from centre of wire on one side to centre of wire on the opposite side |
| Spring index | $C$ | Ratio $D/d$ — usually between **4 and 12** |
| Total number of coils | $N$ | All coils including end coils |
| Active coils | $n$ | Coils that actively participate in deformation |
| Pitch | $p$ | Axial distance between consecutive coils |
| Modulus of rigidity | $G$ | Material property (in **N/mm²** or **GPa**) |
| Spring stiffness | $k$ | Load per unit deflection, $k = W/\delta$ |
| Wahl's factor | $K$ | Correction factor for combined stress |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Trampoline Effect**
>
> Imagine pressing down on a **mattress spring** with your hand. The coils of the spring "wind up" tightly like a thread being twisted. This is **not bending** — it is **pure torsion of the wire**. Every coil rotates by a tiny angle, and that cumulative rotation produces the *axial shortening* you observe as deflection. The thicker the wire and the smaller the coil diameter, the stiffer the spring — just like a fat rubber band resists twisting far more than a thin one.

### 1.4 Classification (KTU Definition Standard)

- **Close-coiled spring**: Helix angle $\alpha < 10°$; subjected to axial load only.
- **Open-coiled spring**: Helix angle $\alpha > 10°$; load may be axial or non-axial.

For KTU lab purposes, the test is **always performed on close-coiled springs**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Why Torsion?

When an axial load $W$ is applied on a close-coiled spring, consider a small element of the wire at the topmost point. The load $W$ acts:
1. Along the axis of the spring (translates to **direct shear** $\tau_s = W/A$).
2. With an **eccentricity** $R = D/2$ from the wire's centroidal axis, creating a **torque** $T = W \cdot R$.

The bending moment $M$ is **zero** because the load passes through the coil axis at this point (a key assumption in close-coiled theory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the spring deflects when loaded:** Every cross-section of the wire twists by an angle $\theta$ due to the torque $T = WR$. The cumulative twist of all $n$ active coils produces a vertical drop $\delta = R \cdot \theta_{\text{total}}$ — this is the **deflection of the spring**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Torque on the wire:** $T = W \cdot R = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$
2. **Torsion equation applied to the wire (circular cross-section):** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$
3. **Polar moment of inertia:** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
4. **Length of the wire:** $L = \pi D n$ (circumference × number of active coils)
5. **Total angle of twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$
6. **Axial deflection:** $\delta = R \cdot \theta = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \theta$
7. **Shear stress (torsional + direct):** $\tau_{\max} = \tau_t + \tau_s$
8. **Wahl's correction factor** is applied to account for **stress concentration at the inner fiber** of the curved wire.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU lab viva and university exam questions. Memorize every row.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---------|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Spring Index | $C = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | dimensionless | $4 \le C \le 12$ preferred |
| 2 | Torque on wire | $T = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·mm | Always N·mm in KTU numericals |
| 3 | Polar moment of inertia | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | mm⁴ | For circular wire cross-section |
| 4 | Wire length | $L = \pi D n$ | mm | $n$ = active coils |
| 5 | **Deflection** | $\delta = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$ | mm | **Most important formula** |
| 6 | **Stiffness** | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$ | N/mm | Slope of W vs $\delta$ graph |
| 7 | Torsional shear stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}$ | N/mm² | Max. at outer fiber of straight bar |
| 8 | Direct shear stress | $\tau_s = \dfrac{4 W}{\pi d^2}$ | N/mm² | Uniform across cross-section |
| 9 | **Maximum shear stress** | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} \cdot K$ | N/mm² | **With Wahl's factor** |
| 10 | **Wahl's Factor** | $K = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ | dimensionless | $C = D/d$ |
| 11 | Strain energy stored | $U = \dfrac{W^2 D^3 n \cdot K}{2 G d^4}$ | N·mm (Joules) | = $\dfrac{1}{2} W \delta_{\text{eff}}$ |
| 12 | Modulus of rigidity (from test) | $G = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{\delta \, d^4}$ | N/mm² | Rearranged from deflection formula |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Close-coiled springs are used as:
- **Vehicle suspension systems** (stiffness and ride comfort calibration)
- **Governors in engines** (speed control)
- **Pressure gauges** (Bourdon tubes use helical spring principle)
- **Door-closing mechanisms, ball-point pens, mattresses, vibration dampers**
- **Clock and watch balance springs**

The **Wahl's factor** is critical in fatigue design — without it, springs fail prematurely at the inner fiber where the wire is curved.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Deflection $\delta$ of a Close-Coiled Spring

### Given Data
- Axial load = $W$
- Mean coil diameter = $D$ (so radius $R = D/2$)
- Wire diameter = $d$
- Number of active coils = $n$
- Modulus of rigidity = $G$

### Step 1 — Torque generated in the wire
When load $W$ acts at a distance $R$ from the centroidal axis of the wire, it produces a torque:

$$T = W \cdot R = W \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

*Logic: A force applied off-axis on a circular shaft produces a twisting moment equal to force × perpendicular distance.*

### Step 2 — Apply Torsion Equation
For a circular shaft under pure torsion:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Where:
- $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (polar moment of inertia of circular cross-section)
- $L$ = length of the wire being twisted
- $\theta$ = total angle of twist (radians)

### Step 3 — Length of the wire
For $n$ active coils of mean diameter $D$, the wire length is the circumference of each coil times the number of coils:

$$L = \pi D \cdot n$$

*Logic: Each coil traces a circle of mean diameter $D$ (circumference = $\pi D$). Total wire length = circumference × number of coils.*

### Step 4 — Solve for the angle of twist
Rearranging the torsion equation:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{\left(W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}\right) \cdot (\pi D n)}{G \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify
The $\pi$ terms cancel:

$$\theta = \frac{W D \cdot D \cdot n \cdot 32}{2 \cdot G \cdot d^4} = \frac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$$

*Logic: Multiplying numerators and denominators: $\frac{W D^2 n \pi \cdot 32}{2 G \pi d^4} = \frac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$*

### Step 6 — Convert angle of twist to axial deflection
The upper end of the spring moves vertically by a distance equal to the radius of the coil times the angle of twist (arc-length relationship):

$$\delta = R \cdot \theta = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$$

### Step 7 — Final deflection equation

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}}$$

*Logic: The factor 8 arises from the product of $\frac{1}{2}$ (from radius) and 16 (from the twist calculation), giving 8. This is the **KTU board gold-standard formula** for spring deflection.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress $\tau_{\max}$

### Step 1 — Direct shear stress from axial load
The axial load $W$ acts as a direct shear on the wire cross-section $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$:

$$\tau_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{W}{\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2}$$

### Step 2 — Torsional shear stress from torque
Using $\tau_t = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ with $r = d/2$:

$$\tau_t = \frac{\left(W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}}{\dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}} = \frac{W D d \cdot 32}{4 \cdot \pi d^4} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}$$

### Step 3 — Combined stress (without correction)
At the **inner fiber** of the coil (where stress concentration is highest), the stresses are additive:

$$\tau_{\text{naive}} = \tau_s + \tau_t = \frac{4W}{\pi d^2} + \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$$

Factoring $\dfrac{8W}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\tau_{\text{naive}} = \frac{8W}{\pi d^3}\left(\frac{d}{2} + D\right) = \frac{8W D}{\pi d^3}\left(1 + \frac{d}{2D}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Wahl's correction factor
Because the wire is curved (helix), there is a **stress concentration** at the inner fiber. Empirical correction by **Wahl** gives:

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} \cdot K}$$

Where:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}}, \quad C = \frac{D}{d}$$

*Logic: The factor $K$ accounts for the **curvature effect** — for a straight torsion member, $K = 1$. As $C$ (spring index) decreases, the curvature increases, and $K$ rises sharply. For typical $C = 6$ to $10$, $K \approx 1.10$ to $1.05$.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Stiffness

From $\delta = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$, we get:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$$

This is also written in terms of the spring index $C$:

$$k = \frac{G d}{8 C^3 n}$$

*Logic: Stiffness is a **property of the spring**, not the load. A graph of $W$ (y-axis) vs $\delta$ (x-axis) yields a straight line whose slope = $k$.*

---

## 3.4 Strain Energy Stored in a Spring

The strain energy is the area under the load–deflection curve (or the work done in deflecting the spring):

$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2 = \frac{W^2}{2k}$$

Substituting $k$ and including the Wahl factor:

$$U = \frac{W^2 D^3 n \cdot K}{2 G d^4}$$

*Logic: Strain energy is essential in **dynamic/impact loading** design (e.g., vehicle bumpers, anti-vibration mounts), and the Wahl factor ensures we account for the actual peak stress.*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Numerical Reference

| $C = D/d$ | Wahl's Factor $K$ | Interpretation |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 3 | 1.50 | Highly curved — $K$ large |
| 4 | 1.27 | Common minimum |
| 6 | 1.15 | Standard industrial |
| 8 | 1.10 | Most common KTU value |
| 10 | 1.07 | Mild curvature |
| 12 | 1.05 | Maximum practical $C$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the components of the close-coiled spring testing rig and their interactions, in lieu of a physical apparatus sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START MEASUREMENT] --> B[Measure wire diameter d with screw gauge]
    B --> C[Measure mean coil diameter D with vernier scale]
    C --> D[Count total coils N and identify active coils n]
    D --> E[Set up spring vertically in UTM or loading frame]
    E --> F[Attach dial gauge to measure axial deflection]
    F --> G[Zero the dial gauge and load indicator]
    G --> H[Apply initial seating load W0 and record reading]
    H --> I[Apply incremental load steps of deltaW]
    I --> J[Record load W versus deflection delta values]
    J --> K{More load steps remaining?}
    K -->|Yes| I
    K -->|No| L[Plot Load W on Y axis versus deflection on X axis]
    L --> M[Draw best fit straight line through origin]
    M --> N[Determine slope of line equals stiffness k experimental]
    N --> O[Compute G experimental equals k times 8 D cubed n over d to power 4]
    O --> P[Compute theoretical k from given G of material]
    P --> Q[Compare experimental k with theoretical k and compute percent error]
    Q --> R[Compute maximum shear stress tau using Wahl factor K]
    R --> S[Compute strain energy U at maximum applied load]
    S --> T[END REPORT GENERATION]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Spring Geometry & Internal Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[AXIAL LOAD W APPLIED AT TOP PLATE] --> G2[Force passes through helix axis]
    G2 --> G3[Direct shear stress tau_s on wire cross section]
    G2 --> G4[Eccentric torque T equals W times D over 2 on wire]
    G3 --> G5[Superpose at inner fiber of curved wire]
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> G6[Apply Wahl correction factor K for curvature]
    G6 --> G7[Final maximum shear stress tau_max]
    H1[WIRE DIAMETER d] --> H2[POLAR MOMENT J equals pi d to power 4 over 32]
    H2 --> H3[TORSIONAL RIGIDITY G times J]
    I1[MEAN COIL DIAMETER D] --> I2[WIRE LENGTH L equals pi D n]
    I2 --> I3[ANGLE OF TWIST theta equals T L over G J]
    I3 --> I4[AXIAL DEFLECTION delta equals D over 2 times theta]
    H3 --> I3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Calculation Flow for KTU Numerical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Given W D d n and G] --> P2[Compute spring index C equals D over d]
    P2 --> P3[Compute deflection delta equals 8 W D cubed n over G d to power 4]
    P2 --> P4[Compute Wahl factor K equals 4C minus 1 over 4C minus 4 plus 0.615 over C]
    P1 --> P5[Compute stiffness k equals G d to power 4 over 8 D cubed n]
    P4 --> P6[Compute maximum shear stress tau equals 8 W D over pi d cubed times K]
    P1 --> P6
    P1 --> P7[Compute strain energy U equals W squared times D cubed n times K over 2 G d to power 4]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "spring index" and state its typical range. Why is it important in spring design?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Spring Index** is defined as the ratio of the mean coil diameter $D$ to the wire diameter $d$:
> $$C = \frac{D}{d}$$
>
> The **typical range** of spring index in engineering practice is **$4 \le C \le 12$**.
>
> **Importance:** A small $C$ (tight coil) causes the wire to be sharply curved, leading to high stress concentration at the inner fiber — this is why Wahl's correction factor $K$ becomes large. A large $C$ (loose coil) makes the spring behave like a slender rod and is prone to buckling. The choice of $C$ directly affects spring stiffness, shear stress magnitude, and manufacturability.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**State Wahl's correction factor. Why is it necessary even though we already compute the combined shear stress?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Wahl's correction factor** $K$ is an empirical multiplier used to account for the **stress concentration at the inner fiber of the curved wire** of a helical spring, and is given by:
> $$K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}$$
>
> It is **necessary** because the simple superposition $\tau_s + \tau_t$ treats the wire as a straight torsion member. In reality, the wire is **curved**, and the inner fiber carries a higher stress than the outer fiber. Photoelastic experiments by Wahl showed that the actual peak stress is **higher** than the naive sum, and the factor $K$ (always $\ge 1$) corrects for this. For $C = 8$, $K \approx 1.10$, meaning the actual stress is **10 % higher** than predicted without the correction — a non-negligible error in fatigue design.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU Standard):** Attempt **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2.

---

### **Question B1 (A) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the deflection of a close-coiled helical spring subjected to an axial load $W$. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A close-coiled helical spring has a mean coil diameter of **80 mm**, wire diameter of **8 mm**, and **12 active coils**. It is subjected to an axial load of **300 N**. Determine: **(i)** Deflection of the spring, **(ii)** Maximum shear stress in the wire (use Wahl's factor), **(iii)** Stiffness of the spring, and **(iv)** Strain energy stored at the applied load. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The helix angle is very small ($< 10°$), so the load is purely axial.
2. The wire cross-section is circular and uniform.
3. Bending moment on the wire is zero; only torsion and direct shear act.
4. Material obeys Hooke's law (within elastic limit).
5. The coil remains circular after loading (no buckling).

**Derivation (reproduced from Section 3.1 for completeness):**

- Torque on wire: $T = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$
- Polar moment: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
- Wire length: $L = \pi D n$
- Torsion equation: $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ} = \dfrac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$
- Deflection: $\delta = R \cdot \theta = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \dfrac{16 W D^2 n}{G d^4}$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $d = 8$ mm, $n = 12$, $W = 300$ N, $G = 80{,}000$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Spring index:**

$$C = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{80}{8} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Deflection:**

$$\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} = \frac{8 \times 300 \times 80^3 \times 12}{80{,}000 \times 8^4}$$

Compute numerator: $8 \times 300 \times 512{,}000 \times 12 = 8 \times 300 \times 6{,}144{,}000 = 14{,}745{,}600{,}000$

Compute denominator: $80{,}000 \times 4096 = 327{,}680{,}000$

$$\delta = \frac{14{,}745{,}600{,}000}{327{,}680{,}000} = 45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum shear stress (with Wahl's factor):**

$$K = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C} = \frac{40 - 1}{40 - 4} + \frac{0.615}{10} = \frac{39}{36} + 0.0615 = 1.0833 + 0.0615 = 1.1448$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} \cdot K = \frac{8 \times 300 \times 80}{\pi \times 8^3} \times 1.1448$$

$$= \frac{192{,}000}{\pi \times 512} \times 1.1448 = \frac{192{,}000}{1608.5} \times 1.1448 = 119.36 \times 1.1448 = 136.65 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{300}{45} = 6.667 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Strain energy:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta = \frac{1}{2} \times 300 \times 45 = 6750 \text{ N·mm} = 6.75 \text{ J}$$

---

### **Question B2 (B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the experimental procedure to determine the **stiffness and modulus of rigidity** of a close-coiled helical spring in the laboratory. Mention the instruments used and the precautions to be taken. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a tension test on a close-coiled helical spring, the following observations were recorded. **Plot the load vs deflection graph**, and determine: **(i)** Stiffness of the spring, **(ii)** Modulus of rigidity of the wire material, **(iii)** Wahl's factor and maximum shear stress at maximum load. **(7 Marks)**

| Load $W$ (N) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | 120 |
|--------------|---|----|----|----|----|-----|-----|
| Deflection $\delta$ (mm) | 0 | 3.2 | 6.3 | 9.5 | 12.7 | 15.8 | 19.0 |

**Given:** Mean coil diameter $D = 60$ mm, Wire diameter $d = 6$ mm, Number of active coils $n = 10$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Apparatus Required:**
- Universal Testing Machine (UTM) / Spring testing machine
- Dial gauge (LC = 0.01 mm)
- Vernier callipers (LC = 0.02 mm) for mean coil diameter
- Screw gauge (LC = 0.01 mm) for wire diameter
- Standard weights / loading pan

**Experimental Procedure (Block-Style):**

1. **Measure spring dimensions** — record $d$ (screw gauge, 3 readings, take mean), $D$ (vernier across opposite coils, 3 readings, take mean), and count total $N$ and active $n$ coils.
2. **Mount the spring vertically** between the platens of the testing machine. Ensure the axis of the spring coincides with the loading axis.
3. **Attach the dial gauge** with its spindle vertical and resting on the top platen; set to zero.
4. **Apply a small seating load** to remove slack; record the dial gauge reading as initial.
5. **Apply incremental loads** in steps of 20 N (or 10 N) and record the corresponding deflection at each step.
6. Continue loading up to a safe maximum (within elastic limit) and then unload — check for permanent set.
7. **Plot $W$ (Y-axis) vs $\delta$ (X-axis)**. The graph is a **straight line passing through the origin**.
8. **Slope of the best-fit line** = experimental stiffness $k_{\exp} = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$.
9. **Compute $G_{\exp}$** from:
$$G_{\exp} = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{\delta \, d^4} = k_{\exp} \cdot \frac{8 D^3 n}{d^4}$$
10. Compare with standard $G$ for the material (e.g., steel $\approx 80$ GPa) and compute percentage error.

**Precautions:**
- Ensure the spring is mounted **vertically** (no eccentricity).
- Apply load **gradually and incrementally**.
- Take **multiple readings** of $d$ and $D$ and average them.
- Do not exceed the **elastic limit** of the spring.
- Avoid **parallax error** in dial gauge reading.
- The dial gauge spindle must be **perpendicular** to the moving platen.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate the slopes between successive readings:**

| $\Delta W$ (N) | $\Delta \delta$ (mm) | Local $k = \Delta W / \Delta \delta$ (N/mm) |
|-----|-----|-----|
| 20 | 3.2 | 6.25 |
| 20 | 3.1 | 6.45 |
| 20 | 3.2 | 6.25 |
| 20 | 3.2 | 6.25 |
| 20 | 3.1 | 6.45 |
| 20 | 3.2 | 6.25 |

**Average stiffness:**

$$k_{\exp} = \frac{\text{Total Load}}{\text{Total Deflection at max}} = \frac{120}{19.0} = 6.32 \text{ N/mm}$$

*Logic: A straight line is confirmed because all local slopes are very close (≈ 6.25–6.45).*

**Step 2 — Modulus of rigidity:**

$$G_{\exp} = \frac{8 D^3 n \cdot k_{\exp}}{d^4} = \frac{8 \times 60^3 \times 10 \times 6.32}{6^4}$$

$$= \frac{8 \times 216{,}000 \times 10 \times 6.32}{1296} = \frac{109{,}209{,}600}{1296} \approx 84{,}260 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{G_{\exp} \approx 84.26 \text{ GPa}}$$

This is close to the standard value of **80 GPa for steel** — percentage error $\approx 5.3\%$.

**Step 3 — Wahl's factor and maximum shear stress:**

$$C = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{60}{6} = 10$$

$$K = \frac{4(10) - 1}{4(10) - 4} + \frac{0.615}{10} = \frac{39}{36} + 0.0615 = 1.0833 + 0.0615 = 1.1448$$

At maximum load $W = 120$ N:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{8 \times 120 \times 60}{\pi \times 6^3} \times 1.1448 = \frac{57{,}600}{678.58} \times 1.1448 = 84.88 \times 1.1448 = 97.16 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $G$:** KTU expects $G$ in **N/mm²** (e.g., 80,000) when lengths are in mm and load in N. Students often use $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$ directly with mm units — answer comes out 1000× wrong. Convert first: $80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting Wahl's factor:** The KTU model answer key **always expects Wahl's factor** in $\tau_{\max}$. Marks are deducted (typically 2 marks) for using $\tau_{\text{naive}} = \tau_s + \tau_t$ without correction.
> 3. **Using total coils $N$ instead of active coils $n$:** $n = N - (\text{end coils})$. For ground/squared ends, subtract 2; for plain ends, subtract 0. Using wrong $n$ gives a wrong stiffness.
> 4. **Using $D$ = outer or inner diameter:** $D$ is the **mean** coil diameter, not the outer. Always measure across the wire centres.
> 5. **Forgetting the helix angle assumption:** In 3-mark sub-parts, KTU expects the assumption statement. Marks lost if omitted.
> 6. **Sign error in strain energy:** $U = \frac{1}{2} W \delta$ (not $W \delta$).
> 7. **Graph plotting:** KTU awards a separate 1–2 marks for the **load–deflection graph**. Don't skip the plot even if the question doesn't explicitly ask.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tests on Close Coiled Spring**

- **Definition:** Close-coiled spring = small helix angle (< 10°), axial load produces **torsion + direct shear only** in the wire.
- **Spring index $C = D/d$**, typically **4 to 12**.
- **Master formula (memorize first):**
$$\delta = \frac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$$
- **Stiffness:** $k = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}$ — slope of the $W$ vs $\delta$ line.
- **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau_t = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$
- **Direct shear stress:** $\tau_s = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$
- **Maximum shear stress (use this in KTU answers):**
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^3} \cdot K$$
- **Wahl's factor:** $K = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ — always $> 1$.
- **Strain energy:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \delta = \dfrac{W^2 D^3 n \cdot K}{2 G d^4}$.
- **Modulus of rigidity from test:** $G = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{\delta \, d^4}$.
- **Experiment essentials:** Measure $d$ (screw gauge), $D$ (vernier), apply incremental loads, record $\delta$ (dial gauge), plot $W$ vs $\delta$ → straight line through origin → slope = $k$.
- **Assumptions to state in derivations:** Small helix angle, circular wire, uniform cross-section, Hooke's law valid, no bending moment.
- **Practical tip:** If your computed $G$ from the experiment comes out within $\pm 10\%$ of the standard value (80 GPa for steel, 26 GPa for brass, 39 GPa for copper), the experiment is considered accurate.
- **Wahl's factor quick values:** $C=4 \Rightarrow K=1.27$; $C=6 \Rightarrow K=1.15$; $C=8 \Rightarrow K=1.10$; $C=10 \Rightarrow K=1.07$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Test on Wooden Beams Using U.T.M.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bending Test on Wooden Beams Using U.T.M.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Test (Flexural Test):** A destructive mechanical test performed on a simply supported beam specimen (wood) subjected to a transverse load applied at the mid-span (centre point loading) or at two points (third-point loading) until failure, to determine the **Flexural Strength (Modulus of Rupture)** and the **Modulus of Elasticity in Bending (Young's Modulus)** of the material.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Laboratory module, this experiment is conducted on a **Universal Testing Machine (U.T.M.)** — a hydraulically operated, screw-driven machine capable of applying tensile, compressive, and transverse loads with high precision. Wooden beam specimens of standard dimensions (typically **20 mm × 20 mm × 760 mm** for the KTU lab) are tested under three-point loading.

### 1.2 Key Standard Codes & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Strength of Materials Lab follows guidelines aligned with **IS: 1708 (Part 5) – 1986** (Methods of Testing of Small Clear Specimens of Timber) and **ASTM D 143 – 14** (Standard Methods of Static Bending of Wood).

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seasoned wood (Teak) | **600 – 750** | kg/m³ |
| Density of seasoned wood (Mango) | **650 – 750** | kg/m³ |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Teak) | **9.0 – 13.0 × 10³** | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Modulus of Rupture (Teak) | **70 – 100** | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Standard Span Length (L) | **700** (typical) | mm |
| Standard Cross-section (b × d) | **20 × 20** | mm |
| Loading Rate | **0.5 – 1.0** | mm/min |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "See-Saw" Principle:**
> 
> Imagine a **wooden ruler** held between your two palms, with your hands acting as the two simple supports. Now, when you press down at the middle of the ruler with your thumb, the ruler bends — the **top fibres compress** (shorten) and the **bottom fibres stretch** (elongate). If you push hard enough, it will snap at the centre. The bending test on a U.T.M. is simply a **sophisticated, instrumented, and load-controlled version** of this thumb-pressing experiment.
> 
> The U.T.M. precisely measures the **load** (your thumb's push, in Newtons) and the **deflection** (how much the ruler dips, in mm) at every instant. From these two numbers, the U.T.M. — combined with beam geometry — gives us the **strength** and **stiffness** of wood.

The **Universal Testing Machine (U.T.M.)** is used here in its **compression/transverse loading configuration**. It consists of:
- A **hydraulic loading system** (lower cross-head moves up)
- A **load cell** (measures force)
- A **dial gauge / LVDT** (measures deflection)
- A **recording unit** (plots Load vs. Deflection curve)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Theory of Pure Bending

When a beam is subjected to a transverse load on a simply supported span, it bends into a **circular arc**. The following assumptions form the bedrock of **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory**:

1. **Plane sections remain plane** before and after bending (no warping).
2. The material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic** (for wood we use a simplified approximation), and obeys **Hooke's Law** up to the elastic limit.
3. The beam's **cross-section** remains unchanged — no bulging or shrinking.
4. **Shear deformation** is neglected (beams are long and slender, L/d ≥ 20).
5. The **neutral axis** passes through the centroid of the cross-section.
6. The beam material has **equal tensile and compressive moduli** (a working approximation for clear wood).

### 2.2 Why Beams Bend — The Fibre-Stress Logic

- The **topmost fibre** (concave side) experiences **Maximum Compressive Stress**.
- The **bottommost fibre** (convex side) experiences **Maximum Tensile Stress**.
- The **neutral axis** (mid-depth) experiences **Zero Stress** and **Zero Strain**.
- Stress varies **linearly** with distance from the neutral axis.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bending Moment (M)** | $M = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}$ | P = Load at centre, L = Span | N·mm |
| 2 | **Section Modulus (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{2}}{6}$ | b = width, d = depth | mm³ |
| 3 | **Moment of Inertia (I)** | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$ | Rectangular section | mm⁴ |
| 4 | **Max. Bending Stress (σ)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}$ | **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** at failure | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 5 | **Max. Deflection (δ)** | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{48 E I}$ | At mid-span, centre loading | mm |
| 6 | **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** | $E = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \cdot \delta}$ | Rearranged deflection formula | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 7 | **Slope at supports (θ)** | $\theta = \dfrac{P L^{2}}{16 E I}$ | At end supports | radian |
| 8 | **Curvature (1/R)** | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E I}$ | Fundamental flexure formula | mm⁻¹ |
| 9 | **Strain at extreme fibre** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{6 \delta \cdot d}{L^{2}}$ | From geometry of bending | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Stiffness (k)** | $k = \dfrac{P}{\delta} = \dfrac{48 E I}{L^{3}}$ | Load per unit deflection | N/mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units in KTU Lab Records:** Loads are read in **N** (or kN), lengths in **mm**, and stresses in **N/mm² (= MPa)**. Always convert kN to N before substituting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry:** Determines allowable load on **door/window frames, rafters, joists, purlins** made of timber.
- **Furniture Design:** Predicts the maximum weight a **wooden chair, table, or shelf** can sustain without sagging.
- **Bridge & Truss Design:** Old wooden bridges in Kerala (e.g., heritage walkways) rely on this exact test data.
- **Plywood/MDF Selection:** Architects use MOR data to choose panels for formwork and partitions.
- **Quality Control:** Sawmills and timber depots use bending tests to **grade wood** as A, B, or C class.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Flexure Formula (σ = M/Z)

Consider a beam of length **L**, simply supported at both ends, loaded centrally by a load **P**.

**Step 1: Reactions at Supports**

By symmetry of the loading:

$$
R_{A} = R_{B} = \dfrac{P}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Bending Moment**

The bending moment is maximum at the **centre of the span** (where shear force is zero). Taking moments from the left support:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{max}} &= R_{A} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right) \;\; \text{[moment from left reaction about centre]} \\
&= \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Stress at Extreme Fibre**

By definition, bending stress at a distance **y** from the neutral axis is:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

For the **extreme fibre** (top or bottom), $y = y_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{d}{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{max}} &= \dfrac{M \cdot (d/2)}{I} \\
&= \dfrac{M}{I / (d/2)} \\
&= \dfrac{M}{Z} \quad \text{where } Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\text{max}}} = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Flexure Formula (Substituting M and Z)**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{(P L / 4)}{(b d^{2} / 6)} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}}
$$

This is the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** when **P** is taken as the **failure (ultimate) load** at fracture.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Deflection Formula (δ = PL³ / 48EI)

Starting from the **Euler–Bernoulli differential equation of the elastic curve**:

$$
E I \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)
$$

**Step 1: Bending Moment Equation (0 ≤ x ≤ L/2)**

$$
M(x) = R_{A} \cdot x = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot x
$$

**Step 2: Substitute and Integrate Once (for slope)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E I \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} &= \int \dfrac{P x}{2} \, dx \\
&= \dfrac{P x^{2}}{4} + C_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Boundary Condition at x = 0 (Slope is not zero at support for simply supported — wait, deflection is zero)**

Actually, slope is **not zero** at the support of a simply supported beam. So we use the symmetry condition.

**Step 3 (corrected): Boundary Condition at x = L/2**

By symmetry, the **slope at the centre** is **zero**:

$$
\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x = L/2} = 0
$$

Applying this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \dfrac{P (L/2)^{2}}{4} + C_{1} \\
0 &= \dfrac{P L^{2}}{16} + C_{1} \\
C_{1} &= -\dfrac{P L^{2}}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Integrate Again (for Deflection)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E I \cdot y &= \int \left(\dfrac{P x^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{P L^{2}}{16}\right) dx \\
&= \dfrac{P x^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{P L^{2} x}{16} + C_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Boundary Condition at x = 0, y = 0**

$$
0 = 0 - 0 + C_{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{2} = 0
$$

**Step 6: Maximum Deflection at x = L/2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E I \cdot \delta &= \dfrac{P (L/2)^{3}}{12} - \dfrac{P L^{2} (L/2)}{16} \\
&= \dfrac{P L^{3}}{96} - \dfrac{P L^{3}}{32} \\
&= \dfrac{P L^{3} - 3 P L^{3}}{96} \\
&= -\dfrac{2 P L^{3}}{96} = -\dfrac{P L^{3}}{48}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the magnitude:

$$
\boxed{\delta_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{48 E I}}
$$

**Step 7: Rearranging for Modulus of Elasticity**

$$
\boxed{E = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{48 I \cdot \delta} = \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \cdot \delta}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given Data:**
- Span, **L = 700 mm**
- Cross-section, **b = 20 mm**, **d = 20 mm**
- Failure Load, **P_ult = 1500 N**
- Deflection at proportional limit, **δ = 8 mm** (at load **P = 600 N**)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Section Modulus (Z)**

$$
Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{20 \times 20^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{8000}{6} = 1333.33 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia (I)**

$$
I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 20^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{160000}{12} = 13333.33 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3: Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOR} &= \dfrac{3 P_{\text{ult}} L}{2 b d^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 1500 \times 700}{2 \times 20 \times 400} \\
&= \dfrac{3150000}{16000} \\
&= 196.875 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 196.88 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Modulus of Elasticity (E)** — using load in **elastic range** (P = 600 N, δ = 8 mm)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \cdot \delta} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \times 700^{3}}{4 \times 20 \times 20^{3} \times 8} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \times 3.43 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 20 \times 8000 \times 8} \\
&= \dfrac{2.058 \times 10^{11}}{5.12 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 40195.31 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 40.2 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The wooden beam has a flexural strength of ~197 MPa and a Young's Modulus of ~40 GPa. This indicates a **high-density, stiff timber** (comparable to Teak under lab conditions).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Three-Point vs. Four-Point Bending)

| Parameter | Three-Point Bending (KTU Lab) | Four-Point Bending |
|---|---|---|
| Loading points | 1 (at centre) | 2 (at L/3 and 2L/3) |
| Bending Moment Region | Maximum only at centre | **Constant** (pure bending) between load points |
| $M_{\text{max}}$ formula | $\dfrac{P L}{4}$ | $\dfrac{P L}{6}$ |
| Max. Deflection (δ) | $\dfrac{P L^{3}}{48 E I}$ | $\dfrac{P L^{3}}{ \text{...} \text{(complex)}}$ |
| Shear force present at mid-span? | **Yes** (combined stress) | **No** (pure bending) |
| Result type | Apparent MOR | True MOR |
| KTU Lab usage | **Yes (Standard)** | Rare / Advanced labs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup on U.T.M.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UTM["UNIVERSAL TESTING MACHINE U.T.M."]
        A1["Hydraulic Power Pack"] --> A2["Lower Cross Head Moves Upward"]
        A2 --> A3["Load Applied on Bearing Block"]
        A3 --> A4["Load Cell Reads Force P in N"]
        A4 --> A5["Digital Indicator Displays P and delta"]
    end

    subgraph SPECIMEN["WOODEN BEAM SPECIMEN 20mm x 20mm x 760mm"]
        B1["Left Roller Support R_A = P/2"] --- B2["Centre Loading Point V"]
        B2 --- B3["Right Roller Support R_B = P/2"]
        B2 --> B4["Neutral Axis Zero Stress"]
        B2 --> B5["Top Fibre Compression"]
        B2 --> B6["Bottom Fibre Tension"]
    end

    subgraph DIAL["DEFLECTION MEASUREMENT"]
        C1["Dial Gauge Mounted at Centre"] --> C2["Reads Vertical Deflection delta in mm"]
        C2 --> C3["Plot Load P vs Deflection delta"]
    end

    A3 --> B2
    C1 --> B2

    A5 --> D1["Calculate MOR = 3PL divided by 2bd squared"]
    C3 --> D2["Calculate E = PL cubed divided by 4bd cubed times delta"]
    D1 --> D3["FINAL RESULTS Modulus of Rupture and Modulus of Elasticity"]
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Step-by-Step Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 Measure specimen dimensions b, d, L using vernier caliper"] --> S2["STEP 2 Place beam on two roller supports with span L"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 Set up dial gauge at mid span for deflection"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 Position loading nose exactly at centre of span"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 Switch ON UTM and zero load and deflection readings"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 Apply load gradually at constant rate 0.5 to 1 mm per minute"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7 Record load P and deflection delta at regular intervals"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8 Continue loading until beam fractures at ultimate load P ult"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9 Plot Load P versus Deflection delta curve"]
    S9 --> S10["STEP 10 Calculate MOR and E from formulas"]
    S10 --> S11["STEP 11 Repeat with two more specimens and take average values"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Stress Distribution Across Beam Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Top Fibre Compressive Stress sigma c MAX"] --> B["Linear Variation through depth"]
    B --> C["Neutral Axis at mid depth sigma equals zero"]
    C --> D["Linear Variation continues"]
    D --> E["Bottom Fibre Tensile Stress sigma t MAX"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Table — U.T.M. Component Functions

| Component | Function | Diagram Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Generates compressive force | Cylinder + piston |
| **Load Cell** | Measures applied force P | Strain-gauged block |
| **Cross-head** | Transmits force to specimen | Movable plate |
| **Bearing Block (Loading Nose)** | Single-point contact at centre | Hemispherical head |
| **Roller Supports** | Allow free rotation & horizontal movement | Two cylindrical rollers |
| **Dial Gauge / LVDT** | Measures mid-span deflection | Vertical gauge |
| **Recorder / Digital Display** | Real-time Load vs Deflection plot | Screen / graph paper |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Always ensure the **loading nose** is exactly **perpendicular** to the beam axis. A tilted nose introduces an **eccentric load**, which causes **combined bending + torsion**, giving erroneous MOR values.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define Modulus of Rupture (MOR) and state the formula used to calculate it in a three-point bending test on a wooden beam.**

**Model Answer:**
> Modulus of Rupture is the **maximum bending stress** sustained by a beam specimen at the moment of fracture. It is calculated as:
> $$\text{MOR} = \sigma_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{3 P_{\text{ult}} L}{2 b d^{2}}$$
> where $P_{\text{ult}}$ is the failure load, $L$ is the span, $b$ is the width, and $d$ is the depth of the beam. It is expressed in **N/mm² (MPa)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Flexural Strength and Flexural Modulus. State the units of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Flexural Strength (MOR) | Flexural Modulus (E) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Maximum stress in outer fibre at fracture | Stiffness of material in bending |
| Formula | $\dfrac{3 P_{\text{ult}} L}{2 b d^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \delta}$ |
| Unit | N/mm² or MPa | N/mm² or MPa (or GPa) |
| Physical meaning | How much load it can take before breaking | How much it deflects under load |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Model: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum bending stress** in a simply supported beam of rectangular cross-section (b × d) subjected to a central point load P. **State all assumptions used.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wooden beam of cross-section **40 mm × 60 mm** and span **1.2 m** is tested under three-point bending on a U.T.M. The beam fails at a load of **8 kN**, and the deflection at a load of **3 kN** is measured as **2.5 mm**. Calculate the **Modulus of Rupture** and the **Young's Modulus** of the wood. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for the complete derivation)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous and isotropic.
2. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis (Euler–Bernoulli).
3. Hooke's Law is valid within the elastic limit.
4. The beam is long and slender (L/d ≥ 20), so shear deformation is neglected.
5. Self-weight of the beam is negligible compared to the applied load.

**Final Derived Formula:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^{2}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** b = 40 mm, d = 60 mm, L = 1200 mm, P_ult = 8000 N, P = 3000 N, δ = 2.5 mm

**Step 1: Section Modulus (Z)**

$$
Z = \dfrac{b d^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{40 \times 60^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{144000}{6} = 24000 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Moment of Inertia (I)**

$$
I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{40 \times 60^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{8640000}{12} = 720000 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3: Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOR} &= \dfrac{3 \times P_{\text{ult}} \times L}{2 \times b \times d^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 8000 \times 1200}{2 \times 40 \times 3600} \\
&= \dfrac{28800000}{288000} \\
&= 100 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Modulus of Elasticity (E)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \delta} \\
&= \dfrac{3000 \times 1200^{3}}{4 \times 40 \times 60^{3} \times 2.5} \\
&= \dfrac{3000 \times 1.728 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 40 \times 216000 \times 2.5} \\
&= \dfrac{5.184 \times 10^{12}}{8.64 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 60000 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 60 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** MOR = **100 MPa**, E = **60 GPa**. This indicates a **dense hardwood** like Teak or Sal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a **neat sketch** the procedure for conducting a **bending test on a wooden beam** using a Universal Testing Machine. List the **precautions** to be observed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a bending test, a wooden beam of size **30 mm × 30 mm × 750 mm** (effective span = 700 mm) is loaded centrally. The following readings were recorded. Plot the **Load–Deflection curve** and determine: **(i) Modulus of Elasticity, (ii) Modulus of Rupture**. **[7 Marks]**

| Load (N) | 0 | 200 | 400 | 600 | 800 | 1000 | 1200 (Fracture) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deflection (mm) | 0 | 1.2 | 2.5 | 3.7 | 5.0 | 6.3 | 7.8 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure:** *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for the visual flowchart.)*

**Procedure Steps:**
1. Measure the **b, d, L** of the specimen using a vernier caliper (accuracy 0.02 mm).
2. Mark the **centroid line** along the length.
3. Place the beam on the **two roller supports** of the U.T.M. Ensure the span is exactly **L**.
4. Mount the **dial gauge** at the centre (bottom of the beam) to record deflection.
5. Position the **loading nose** at the mid-span with a small preload (~50 N) to seat the system.
6. Zero both the **load indicator** and the **dial gauge**.
7. Apply load at a **constant rate** of ~0.5 mm/min.
8. Record load and deflection at regular load intervals (every 200 N or so).
9. Continue until **fracture** occurs; record the **ultimate load P_ult**.
10. Repeat for **2 more specimens** to obtain an average.

**Precautions:**
- The loading nose must be **centred perfectly** to avoid eccentric loading.
- The beam should be **straight-grained** and free from knots.
- Dial gauge should be **perpendicular** to the beam axis.
- Load should be applied **gradually and uniformly** without jerks.
- Specimen dimensions should be measured **after conditioning** (12% moisture content).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** b = 30 mm, d = 30 mm, L = 700 mm, P_ult = 1200 N

**Step 1: Section Properties**

$$
I = \dfrac{30 \times 30^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{810000}{12} = 67500 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{30 \times 30^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{27000}{6} = 4500 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Plot the Load-Deflection Curve**

*(Linear portion: from 0 to 800 N, slope = P/δ = 800/5.0 = 160 N/mm)*

**Step 3: Modulus of Elasticity (E)**

Using a point on the **linear (elastic) portion**, say P = 600 N, δ = 3.7 mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \delta} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \times 700^{3}}{4 \times 30 \times 30^{3} \times 3.7} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \times 3.43 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 30 \times 27000 \times 3.7} \\
&= \dfrac{2.058 \times 10^{11}}{1.1988 \times 10^{7}} \\
&\approx 17170 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 17.17 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOR} &= \dfrac{3 P_{\text{ult}} L}{2 b d^{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 1200 \times 700}{2 \times 30 \times 900} \\
&= \dfrac{2520000}{54000} \\
&= 46.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 46.67 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** E ≈ **17.17 GPa**, MOR ≈ **46.67 MPa**. This corresponds to a **softwood or low-density timber** (e.g., Pine).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kN with mm, or N with m. Always convert everything to **N and mm** before substitution. **(–1 to 2 marks)**
> 2. **Wrong P used for E:** Students often wrongly use the **ultimate load (P_ult)** to find E. Correct: E must be calculated using a **load within the elastic (linear) portion** of the Load–Deflection curve. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing b and d:** Section modulus uses $d^{2}$, but Moment of Inertia uses $d^{3}$. Interchanging width and depth drastically changes the answer. **(–2 to 3 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting the constant 48** in the deflection formula. Some students use 4, 8, 16, or 32 — all wrong. For a **simply supported beam with central point load**, it is **48**. **(–2 marks)**
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — KTU examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for listing **Euler–Bernoulli assumptions**.
> 6. **Missing the sketch** in procedure questions — KTU mandates a "neat labelled sketch" worth at least **2 marks**.
> 7. **Forgetting to average** results of multiple specimens — KTU lab viva and records require at least **3 specimens** averaged.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Bending Test on Wooden Beams (KTU 2024)**

- ✅ **Aim:** To determine the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** and **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** of a wooden beam using U.T.M.
- ✅ **Standard Specimen Size:** 20 mm × 20 mm × 760 mm (effective span ≈ 700 mm).
- ✅ **Test Type:** **Three-point bending (centre-point loading)** — most common in KTU labs.
- ✅ **Loading Rate:** Maintain **0.5 to 1.0 mm/min** for steady, valid results.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - MOR $= \dfrac{3 P_{\text{ult}} L}{2 b d^{2}}$
  - E $= \dfrac{P L^{3}}{4 b d^{3} \delta}$ (use elastic-region P and δ)
- ✅ **Section Modulus (Z)** for rectangle: $\dfrac{b d^{2}}{6}$ — units: **mm³**.
- ✅ **Moment of Inertia (I)** for rectangle: $\dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ — units: **mm⁴**.
- ✅ **Units Convention in KTU:** Load in **N**, length in **mm**, stress in **N/mm² = MPa**.
- ✅ **For E calculation:** Use load and deflection from the **straight-line (linear) portion** of the Load–Deflection graph — never the failure values.
- ✅ **For MOR calculation:** Use the **ultimate (failure) load P_ult**.
- ✅ **Typical Results:** Teak → E ≈ 10–13 GPa, MOR ≈ 70–100 MPa; Softwood (Pine) → E ≈ 8–12 GPa, MOR ≈ 40–70 MPa.
- ✅ **Assumptions of Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory:** Homogeneity, isotropy, plane sections remain plane, small deflections, shear deformation negligible, Hooke's Law valid.
- ✅ **Error Sources:** Eccentric loading, knots/grain defects in wood, dial gauge misalignment, fluctuating hydraulic pressure.
- ✅ **Safety Precautions:** Wear **safety goggles**, stand clear during fracture, keep hands off the loading zone, switch off U.T.M. after the test.
- ✅ **Lab Record Must Include:** Specimen dimensions table, Load–Deflection table, graph plot, calculation sheet, result table with **average of 3 specimens**, and a **conclusion** comparing with standard timber values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Clerk Maxwell's Law of Reciprocal Deflection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Clerk Maxwell's Law of Reciprocal Deflection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Clerk Maxwell's Law of Reciprocal Deflection** is a special, beam-specific application of **Betti's Reciprocal Theorem** (1872), restricted to the case of transverse point loads. The theorem states:

> *"In any linear elastic structure, the deflection produced at point **A** due to a unit load applied at point **B** is equal in magnitude to the deflection produced at point **B** due to the same unit load applied at point **A**, provided the structure, material, supports, and temperature remain unchanged."*

Mathematically, the theorem is compactly expressed as:

$$\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA}$$

where $\delta_{AB}$ denotes the deflection at point **A** caused by a load **P** acting at point **B**, and $\delta_{BA}$ denotes the deflection at point **B** caused by the same load **P** acting at point **A**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The theorem is **NOT** a "Newton's Third Law" of mechanics — it deals with **displacement influence coefficients**, not forces. The directions of the deflections must also be the same (typically both downward for a horizontal beam under gravity loading).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **wooden plank supported at its two ends** (a simple bridge). You stand at the **left end (Point A)**. The plank bends slightly downward, and the **right end (Point B)** also moves down a little.

Now reverse the situation: you stand at the **right end (Point B)** with the **same weight**. The plank bends the same way, and the **left end (Point A)** moves down by the **exact same small amount** as the right end did in the first case.

In essence, the beam "**remembers**" how a load affects it. Swapping the **load** and the **observation point** produces the **same numerical deflection**. The beam behaves like a "**fair, reciprocal system**".

Another everyday analogy: think of a **two-person seesaw**:
- Push down at the **left**, the **right** goes up by some amount.
- Push down at the **right**, the **left** goes up by the **same amount**.

That symmetry is what Maxwell's theorem formalises mathematically for elastic beams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Reciprocal"?** Because the relationship is **symmetric (commutative)** — just like multiplication of real numbers ($a \times b = b \times a$). The **load position** and the **deflection observation point** can be exchanged without changing the result.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Standard Metrics)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| Deflection at A due to load at B | $\delta_{AB}$ | **mm (millimetre)** |
| Deflection at B due to load at A | $\delta_{BA}$ | **mm (millimetre)** |
| Applied Point Load | **P** or **W** | **N (Newton)** or **kgf** |
| Young's Modulus | **E** | **N/m²** or **GPa** |
| Second Moment of Area | **I** | **m⁴** or **mm⁴** |
| Span of Beam | **L** | **m** or **mm** |
| Distance of load from support | **a, b** | **m** or **mm** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | **m/s²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Conditions (Assumptions / Validity Criteria)

For Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem to hold **exactly**, the beam-system must satisfy **all** of the following:
