1. **Linear Elasticity** — The material obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$). No yielding, no plastic deformation.
2. **Small Deflections** — Slope $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is very small; the beam geometry does not change appreciably under load (linearised Euler-Bernoulli theory applies).
3. **Constant Flexural Rigidity (EI)** — The product of Young's Modulus (**E**) and Second Moment of Area (**I**) must remain **constant** along the span (uniform cross-section, homogeneous material).
4. **Constant Temperature** — No thermal gradients, which would otherwise induce internal stresses.
5. **Same Loading Magnitude** — The same load **P** is used in both the direct and the reciprocal cases.
6. **Statically Determinate or Indeterminate (both valid)** — Maxwell's theorem holds for **both** determinate and indeterminate beams.
7. **Superposition Valid** — Deflections from multiple loads may be added algebraically (direct consequence of linearity).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often quote Maxwell's theorem only for simply supported beams. The theorem is **GENERAL** — it applies to cantilevers, fixed beams, propped cantilevers, trusses, and frames alike, as long as the linearity conditions are satisfied.

### 2.2 Underlying Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Define Influence Coefficient.**
The **flexibility influence coefficient** $a_{ij}$ is defined as the deflection at point **i** produced by a **unit** load applied at point **j**. Maxwell's theorem states that the flexibility matrix is **symmetric**:

$$a_{ij} \;=\; a_{ji}$$

**Step 2 — Apply a Load P at A.**
The work done by the external load **P** acting through the deflection $\delta_A$ at **A** equals the strain energy stored **U** in the beam:

$$W_{ext} \;=\; P \cdot \delta_A \;=\; U_{strain}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a Load P at B (Reciprocal Case).**
The work done by the external load **P** acting through the deflection $\delta_B$ at **B** equals the same strain energy (by conservation of energy):

$$W_{ext}' \;=\; P \cdot \delta_B \;=\; U_{strain}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two.**
Since the strain energy stored depends only on the **final state** of deformation (and is path-independent for linear elastic systems), and because in both cases the load is the **same magnitude P** and the beam is the **same**:

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Generalisation to Multiple Loads (Betti's Theorem).**
For a system with two independent load systems $(P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n)$ and $(Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n)$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot \delta_i^{(Q)} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_j \cdot \delta_j^{(P)}$$

Maxwell's theorem is the **special case** where all loads are zero except for a single unit load in each system.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **every formula** a KTU 2024 student needs for the Strength of Materials Lab viva, record, and university exam on this experiment. Memorise these — they appear almost every year.

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|------:|------------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem (Core) | $\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA}$ | mm or m |
| 2 | Flexibility Influence Coefficient | $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$ | m/N |
| 3 | Betti's Theorem (General) | $\sum P_i \delta_i^{(Q)} = \sum Q_j \delta_j^{(P)}$ | Work-energy equivalence |
| 4 | Deflection at point **x** for SSB with load P at distance **a** (for $x \leq a$) | $y = \dfrac{Pbx}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)$ | m |
| 5 | Deflection at point **x** for SSB with load P at distance **a** (for $x \geq a$) | $y = \dfrac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2Lx - a^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)$ | m |
| 6 | Maximum deflection under the load (at $x = a$) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{Pa^{2}b^{2}}{3LEI}$ | m (occurs under load) |
| 7 | Slope at left support | $\theta_A = \dfrac{Pb\,(L^{2}-b^{2})}{6LEI}$ | rad |
| 8 | Slope at right support | $\theta_B = \dfrac{Pa\,(L^{2}-a^{2})}{6LEI}$ | rad |
| 9 | Strain Energy stored in beam | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L}\dfrac{M^{2}}{EI}\,dx$ | J (Joules) |
| 10 | Strain Energy from bending | $U = \dfrac{P^{2}a^{2}b^{2}}{6LEI}$ | J (for SSB, point load) |
| 11 | Verification Condition (Experimental) | $\dfrac{\vert\delta_{AB} - \delta_{BA}\vert}{\delta_{AB}} \times 100 \leq 5\%$ | Percentage Error |
| 12 | Symmetric point positions (typical) | $a + b = L$ and $a = b = L/2$ for symmetric loading | m |
| 13 | Modulus of Rigidity (Flexural Rigidity) | $EI$ | N·m² |
| 14 | Weight to Force conversion | $W = mg$ | N (where $m$ in kg) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maxwell's theorem is **not merely an academic curiosity** — it is the backbone of several modern structural-analysis techniques:

- **Matrix Stiffness Method (Finite Element Analysis):** The global stiffness matrix is symmetric **only** because of Maxwell's reciprocal theorem. This symmetry halves the computational effort in solvers.
- **Influence Line Diagrams:** Reciprocity allows engineers to find the influence line for a reaction or moment by simply loading the structure at the point where the effect is desired.
- **Müller-Breslau Principle:** This principle (used to construct influence lines for indeterminate structures) is a direct corollary of Maxwell's theorem.
- **Bridge & Crane Design:** Verifies symmetry in load-transfer behaviour, simplifying the design of symmetric girders and trusses.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Reciprocity-based diagnostic methods use the theorem to localise damage in beams from vibration measurements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Symmetry of the Flexibility Matrix (Betti / Maxwell Proof)

**Starting Setup.** Consider a linearly elastic beam of span **L** with flexural rigidity **EI** (constant). Apply a load $P_1$ at point **1** (i.e., point A in the lab). Let the deflections at points **1** and **2** be $\delta_{11}$ and $\delta_{21}$ respectively.

**Now apply a load $P_2$ at point 2.** Let the deflections at points **1** and **2** be $\delta_{12}$ and $\delta_{22}$.

By the principle of superposition (valid for linear elastic systems), the **total deflection at point i** under both loads simultaneously is:

$$\Delta_i \;=\; P_1\,\delta_{i1} \;+\; P_2\,\delta_{i2} \qquad (i = 1, 2)$$

**Strain Energy in the beam (two-load case):**

$$U \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_1\,\Delta_1 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_2\,\Delta_2$$

Substituting $\Delta_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \;&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_1\bigl(P_1\delta_{11} + P_2\delta_{12}\bigr) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_2\bigl(P_1\delta_{21} + P_2\delta_{22}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_1^{2}\delta_{11} \;+\; P_1 P_2 \delta_{12} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,P_2^{2}\delta_{22}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic note:** In the mixed term, the coefficient could equally have been $\delta_{21}$; both must lead to the same stored energy. Since $U$ is a unique scalar, the symmetry of the cross term is **mandated**.

**Path-Independence Argument (Clapeyron's Theorem Alternative).**
Strain energy $U$ is a **state function** of the final deflected shape. It does not depend on the **order** in which the loads were applied. Therefore, the expression for $U$ in terms of $(P_1, P_2)$ must be **unique**, requiring the cross-coefficients to be equal:

$$P_1 P_2\,\delta_{12} \;=\; P_1 P_2\,\delta_{21}$$

Dividing through by the non-zero product $P_1 P_2$:

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{12} \;=\; \delta_{21}\;} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\;\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA}\;}$$

**Conclusion.** This is the rigorous energy-based proof of Maxwell's reciprocal theorem. The flexibility influence coefficients form a **symmetric matrix** — the mathematical heart of the stiffness method in FEM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form Deflection Expressions Used in the Lab

For the standard **simply supported beam** used in this KTU lab experiment, we need explicit expressions for $\delta_{AB}$ and $\delta_{BA}$ to **predict** the experimental readings.

**Setup:** Simply supported beam of span **L**. Point load **P** applied at distance **a** from the **left support** (and therefore $b = L - a$ from the right support). We want the deflection at a general point **x**.

**Bending Moment Expressions (by Macaulay's Method):**

For the left portion ($0 \leq x \leq a$):

$$M_1(x) \;=\; R_A \cdot x \;=\; \frac{Pb}{L}\,x$$

For the right portion ($a \leq x \leq L$):

$$M_2(x) \;=\; R_A \cdot x \;-\; P(x-a) \;=\; \frac{Pb}{L}\,x \;-\; P(x-a)$$

**Governing Differential Equation (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory):**

$$EI\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; M(x)$$

**Integrate twice for the left portion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI\,\frac{dy_1}{dx} &= \int M_1\,dx = \frac{Pb}{L}\cdot\frac{x^{2}}{2} + C_1 \\[4pt]
EI\,y_1 &= \frac{Pb}{L}\cdot\frac{x^{3}}{6} + C_1 x + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Integrate twice for the right portion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI\,\frac{dy_2}{dx} &= \int M_2\,dx = \frac{Pb}{L}\cdot\frac{x^{2}}{2} - P\cdot\frac{(x-a)^{2}}{2} + C_3 \\[4pt]
EI\,y_2 &= \frac{Pb}{L}\cdot\frac{x^{3}}{6} - P\cdot\frac{(x-a)^{3}}{6} + C_3 x + C_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Conditions (4 equations for 4 constants $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$):**

1. $y_1(0) = 0$  →  $C_2 = 0$
2. $y_2(L) = 0$  →  $\dfrac{PbL^{2}}{6} - \dfrac{Pb^{3}}{6} + C_3 L + C_4 = 0$
3. Continuity of slope at $x = a$: $\dfrac{dy_1}{dx}\bigg|_{x=a} = \dfrac{dy_2}{dx}\bigg|_{x=a}$  →  $C_1 = C_3$
4. Continuity of deflection at $x = a$: $y_1(a) = y_2(a)$  →  $C_1 a = C_3 a + C_4$  →  $C_4 = 0$

Substituting $C_4 = 0$ into BC 2:

$$\frac{PbL^{2}}{6} - \frac{Pb^{3}}{6} + C_3 L = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_3 = -\frac{Pb(L^{2}-b^{2})}{6L}$$

**Deflection at any point in the left portion ($0 \leq x \leq a$):**

$$
EI\,y_1 \;=\; \frac{Pb\,x^{3}}{6L} \;-\; \frac{Pb(L^{2}-b^{2})}{6L}\,x
$$

Factorising:

$$\boxed{\;y_1 \;=\; \frac{Pbx}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)\;}$$

This is the **standard formula** for deflection in the left segment of a simply supported beam with an eccentric point load.

**Deflection at the point of loading itself ($x = a$):**

Substituting $x = a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1(a) &= \frac{Pba}{6LEI}\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - a^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{Pba}{6LEI}\bigl((a+b)^{2} - b^{2} - a^{2}\bigr) \quad\text{(since }L = a+b\text{)}\\[4pt]
&= \frac{Pba}{6LEI}\bigl(2ab\bigr) \;=\; \frac{Pa^{2}b^{2}}{3LEI}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{\text{under load}} \;=\; \frac{Pa^{2}b^{2}}{3LEI}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Explicit Maxwell Reciprocity for a SSB

To **verify** Maxwell's law in the lab, the deflections at **two interior points A and B** are measured. Let the beam have span $L$ with supports at $x=0$ and $x=L$.

**Case 1: Load P at Point A (distance $a$ from left support, $b = L-a$ from right).**

Deflection at Point B (which lies in the left segment, $x = x_B \leq a$):

$$\delta_{BA,\text{case1}} \;=\; \frac{P\cdot b \cdot x_B}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - x_B^{2}\bigr)$$

**Case 2: Same Load P at Point B (distance $x_B$ from left support, $L - x_B$ from right).**

By symmetry of the formula, the deflection at Point A (now in the left segment of the new load position, $x = a \leq x_B$):

$$\delta_{AB,\text{case2}} \;=\; \frac{P\cdot(L - x_B)\cdot a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - (L-x_B)^{2} - a^{2}\bigr)$$

Setting $b = L - a$, expand $(L - x_B)^{2} = L^{2} - 2Lx_B + x_B^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{AB,\text{case2}} &= \frac{P(L-x_B)\,a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - L^{2} + 2Lx_B - x_B^{2} - a^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P(L-x_B)\,a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2Lx_B - x_B^{2} - a^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P(L-x_B)\,a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(x_B(2L - x_B) - a^{2}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now apply the relation $2L - x_B = L + (L - x_B) = L + b'$ where $b' = L - x_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{AB,\text{case2}} &= \frac{P\,b'\,a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(x_B(L + b') - a^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P\,b'\,a}{6LEI}\,\bigl(x_B L + x_B b' - a^{2}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $x_B = L - b' = a + b - b' = a + (b - b')$ and $a^{2} = (x_B - b + b')^{2}$ — after careful algebraic expansion, one obtains:

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{AB,\text{case1}} \;=\; \delta_{BA,\text{case2}}\;}$$

This algebraic identity confirms that the **closed-form Euler-Bernoulli deflection formulas are intrinsically symmetric** under exchange of load-point and observation-point — a direct mathematical embodiment of Maxwell's reciprocal theorem.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Calculation Style)

**Given:**
- Span $L = 1000$ mm
- Point A at $a = 300$ mm from left support; Point B at $x_B = 700$ mm
- Load $P = 50$ N (i.e., ~5 kg weight)
- $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² (steel)
- $I = 8.5 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴ (rectangular section, 25 mm × 68 mm, neutral axis bending)

**Step 1 — Case 1: Load P at A.**

Here load distance from left = $a_1 = 300$ mm, so $b_1 = L - a_1 = 700$ mm.
Point B is at $x_B = 700$ mm, which lies in the **right segment** ($x \geq a_1$), so use the right-segment formula.

$$M_2(x) = \frac{P b_1}{L}x - P(x - a_1)$$

Deflection at B (by symmetry of formula):

$$\delta_{BA} \;=\; \frac{P\,a_1\,x_B}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2L x_B - a_1^{2} - x_B^{2}\bigr)$$

Wait — for $x \geq a$ the formula is:

$$y_2 \;=\; \frac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2Lx - a^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)$$

So at $x = x_B = 700$ mm, $a = a_1 = 300$ mm, $L - x_B = 300$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{BA} &= \frac{50 \times 300 \times 300}{6 \times 1000 \times 2\times10^{5} \times 8.5\times10^{4}}\,\bigl(2\times1000\times700 - 300^{2} - 700^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.5\times10^{6}}{1.02\times10^{14}}\,\bigl(1{,}400{,}000 - 90{,}000 - 490{,}000\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= (4.41\times10^{-8})\,\bigl(820{,}000\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= 0.0362 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Case 2: Load P at B.**

Here load distance from left = $a_2 = 700$ mm, so $b_2 = 300$ mm.
Point A is at $x = 300$ mm, which lies in the **left segment** ($x \leq a_2$), so use the left-segment formula.

$$y_1 \;=\; \frac{P\,b_2\,x_A}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b_2^{2} - x_A^{2}\bigr)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{AB} &= \frac{50 \times 300 \times 300}{6 \times 1000 \times 2\times10^{5} \times 8.5\times10^{4}}\,\bigl(1{,}000^{2} - 300^{2} - 300^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= (4.41\times10^{-8})\,\bigl(1{,}000{,}000 - 90{,}000 - 90{,}000\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= (4.41\times10^{-8})\,(820{,}000) \\[4pt]
&= 0.0362 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

$$\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA} \;=\; 0.0362 \text{ mm} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \textbf{Maxwell's theorem verified exactly!}$$

**Step 4 — Error Percentage.**

$$\%\text{Error} \;=\; \frac{\vert\delta_{AB} - \delta_{BA}\vert}{\delta_{AB}} \times 100 \;=\; 0\% \quad\text{(theoretical)}$$

In actual lab, a small error (1–5%) is observed due to friction in dial gauges, supports settling, and human observation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete laboratory test rig, mapped as an **interconnected functional architecture** (each block represents a real component and its measurement/data role):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph beam["Simply Supported Beam Rig"]
        supL["Left Support\nKnife Edge"]
        beamS["Test Beam\nRectangular Section"]
        supR["Right Support\nKnife Edge"]
        supL --- beamS
        beamS --- supR
    end

    loadA["Load Hanger P\napplied at Point A"]
    loadB["Load Hanger P\napplied at Point B"]
    pointA["Observation Point A\nx = 300 mm"]
    pointB["Observation Point B\nx = 700 mm"]
    beamS --- pointA
    beamS --- pointB

    dialA["Dial Gauge 1\nLC = 0.01 mm\nAt Point B reading"]
    dialB["Dial Gauge 2\nLC = 0.01 mm\nAt Point A reading"]
    pointA --- dialB
    pointB --- dialA

    weig["Standard Weights\n2 kg to 10 kg"]
    weig --> loadA
    weig --> loadB

    obs["Observer reads\nboth gauges"]
    dialA --> obs
    dialB --> obs
```

> **Read this diagram as:** Weights are placed on a hanger, the hanger is positioned at either A or B, and the two dial gauges at the **opposite** points record the deflection. The observer notes both readings simultaneously.

---

### 4.2 Procedural Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1: Set up simply supported beam\nCheck level, knife edges aligned"]
    s2["Step 2: Mark points A and B\nat distances a and b from left support"]
    s3["Step 3: Mount two dial gauges\nzero them at A and B"]
    s4["Step 4: Apply Load P at A\nWait 2 minutes for steady state"]
    s5["Step 5: Record deflection at B\nThis is delta BA case 1"]
    s6["Step 6: Remove load, note\nany residual deflection"]
    s7["Step 7: Apply same Load P at B\nWait 2 minutes"]
    s8["Step 8: Record deflection at A\nThis is delta AB case 2"]
    s9["Step 9: Remove load"]
    s10["Step 10: Compare delta AB vs delta BA\nCalculate percentage error"]
    s11["Step 11: If error within 5%\nMaxwell theorem is verified"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 --> s11
```

---

### 4.3 Beam Cross-Section & Loading Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph section["Beam Cross-Section b x d"]
        bdir["Width b = 25 mm"]
        ddir["Depth d = 68 mm"]
        ineut["Neutral Axis\npasses through centroid"]
        imoment["I = bd cubed / 12\n= 8.5 x 10 to power 4 mm to power 4"]
        imat["Material: Mild Steel\nE = 2 x 10 to power 5 N per mm squared"]
        ineut --- imoment
        bdir --- ineut
        ddir --- ineut
        imat --- imoment
    end

    subgraph loading["Loading Configuration"]
        Ldir["Span L = 1000 mm"]
        aPos["Load at A\na = 300 mm from left"]
        bPos["Load at B\nb = 300 mm from right"]
        sym["Symmetric about midspan\nL by 2 = 500 mm"]
        Ldir --- aPos
        Ldir --- bPos
        aPos --- sym
        bPos --- sym
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The beam is a mild-steel rectangular bar. The flexural rigidity $EI$ is the **physical property** that determines how much it deflects under load. The symmetry of the loading positions (A and B equidistant from the midspan) **enhances** the equality of the two deflections and makes the experimental result more convincing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**State Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem. What are the essential conditions for its validity?**

**Model Answer:**

Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem states that **"the deflection at point A due to a unit load applied at point B is equal to the deflection at point B due to the same unit load applied at point A"** in a linear elastic structure, i.e., $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$.

**Essential conditions:**

1. The material must be **perfectly elastic** and obey Hooke's Law.
2. The deflections must be **small** (linearised beam theory applies).
3. The **flexural rigidity $EI$** must be constant along the span.
4. The **temperature** must remain constant (no thermal stresses).
5. The same **load magnitude** is used in both the direct and reciprocal cases.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for statement + 2 marks for conditions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem and Betti's Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Maxwell's Theorem** | **Betti's Theorem** |
|------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Scope | Special case (single load) | General case (multiple loads) |
| Statement | Deflection at A due to load at B = Deflection at B due to load at A | Work done by one load system through displacements of another = vice versa |
| Mathematical form | $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$ | $\sum P_i \delta_i^{(Q)} = \sum Q_j \delta_j^{(P)}$ |
| Applicability | Specific to point loads on beams | Applicable to any elastic structure under any general loading |

**Conclusion:** Betti's theorem is the **generalised** form; Maxwell's is its **single-load specialisation**.

*(3 marks: 2 for table + 1 for conclusion)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Both sub-parts must be answered from the same choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**A simply supported beam of span 1.2 m and rectangular cross-section 30 mm × 60 mm (steel, E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²) is used to verify Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for the deflection at point A (300 mm from left support) due to a load of 80 N applied at point B (900 mm from left support), and the deflection at point B due to the same load applied at point A. Show that the two are equal.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In an experiment, the observed deflections are $\delta_{AB} = 0.052$ mm and $\delta_{BA} = 0.050$ mm. Comment on the verification and calculate the percentage error. State **two** practical reasons why the observed values differ from the theoretical predictions.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Using the standard simply supported beam formula:

**For $x \leq a$:**
$$y \;=\; \frac{Pbx}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)$$

**For $x \geq a$:**
$$y \;=\; \frac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2Lx - a^{2} - x^{2}\bigr)$$

Given: $L = 1200$ mm, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $b_{\text{cs}} \times d = 30 \times 60$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute Second Moment of Area $I$:**

$$I \;=\; \frac{b\,d^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{30 \times 60^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{30 \times 216{,}000}{12} \;=\; 540{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Case 1: Load P = 80 N at A (a = 300 mm from left, b = 900 mm).**

To find deflection at B, $x_B = 900$ mm, which is in the right segment ($x \geq a$). Use right-segment formula with $a = 300$, $x = 900$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{BA} &= \frac{Pa(L-x_B)}{6LEI}\,\bigl(2Lx_B - a^{2} - x_B^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{80 \times 300 \times (1200-900)}{6 \times 1200 \times 2\times10^{5} \times 5.4\times10^{5}} \bigl(2 \times 1200 \times 900 - 300^{2} - 900^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{80 \times 300 \times 300}{7.776 \times 10^{14}} \bigl(2{,}160{,}000 - 90{,}000 - 810{,}000\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{7.776 \times 10^{14}} \times 1{,}260{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 0.01167 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Case 2: Load P = 80 N at B (a = 900 mm from left, b = 300 mm).**

To find deflection at A, $x_A = 300$ mm, which is in the left segment ($x \leq a$). Use left-segment formula with $b = 300$, $x = 300$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{AB} &= \frac{Pb\,x_A}{6LEI}\,\bigl(L^{2} - b^{2} - x_A^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{80 \times 300 \times 300}{6 \times 1200 \times 2\times10^{5} \times 5.4\times10^{5}} \bigl(1200^{2} - 300^{2} - 300^{2}\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{7.776 \times 10^{14}} \bigl(1{,}440{,}000 - 90{,}000 - 90{,}000\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= 0.01167 \times 1{,}260{,}000 / 10^{6} \\[4pt]
&= 0.01167 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA} = 0.01167$ mm. **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem is verified exactly.** *(7 marks: 2 for I, 2.5 for each case, 0.5 for conclusion)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Percentage Error:**

$$\%\text{Error} \;=\; \frac{\vert\delta_{AB} - \delta_{BA}\vert}{\delta_{AB}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{\vert 0.052 - 0.050 \vert}{0.052} \times 100 \;=\; 3.85\%$$

**Step 2 — Verification Status:**

Since the percentage error is **less than 5%**, Maxwell's theorem is **verified** within acceptable experimental tolerance.

**Step 3 — Two Practical Reasons for Discrepancy:**

1. **Support Settlement / Friction at Knife Edges:** Real supports are not perfectly rigid; the beam may experience slight rotation or settlement under load, leading to additional deflections not accounted for in the theoretical formula.

2. **Dial Gauge Mechanical Backlash / Zero Error:** Dial gauges have inherent mechanical friction and zero-setting errors. The Least Count of 0.01 mm introduces a measurement uncertainty that, when the deflections are themselves of the order of 0.05 mm, becomes significant.

*(Other valid reasons: self-weight of beam not considered, point load not applied at exact marked position, beam not perfectly straight, supports not at true knife-edge level, elastic hysteresis.)*

*(7 marks: 2 for error calculation, 2 for verification statement, 3 for reasons)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **experimental procedure** to verify Clerk Maxwell's Law of Reciprocal Deflection in the laboratory. List the apparatus required and state the **precautions** to be taken.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A beam has a stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -4 \\ -4 & 8 \end{pmatrix}$ kN/mm. Determine the flexibility matrix $\mathbf{F}$ and verify that it is symmetric. What does the symmetry of the flexibility matrix imply physically?

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Apparatus Required:**

1. **Simply supported beam** of mild steel (rectangular cross-section)
2. **Two knife-edge supports** (left and right)
3. **Two dial gauges** (Least Count = 0.01 mm)
4. **Loading hanger** with slotted weights (2 kg, 5 kg)
5. **Vernier callipers** to measure beam dimensions (b, d)
6. **Metre scale / measuring tape** to mark points A and B
7. **Spirit level** to check horizontal alignment

**Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Place the beam on the two knife-edge supports, ensuring the overhangs on both sides are equal. Use a spirit level to confirm the beam is horizontal.
2. **Geometry:** Measure the effective span **L** with a metre scale. Measure the beam's width **b** and depth **d** with vernier callipers at three locations; record the average. Compute $I = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$.
3. **Marking:** Mark two interior points **A** and **B** on the beam — say at distances $a$ and $b$ from the left support. (For best results, choose $a \neq b$ but symmetric about midspan.)
4. **Dial Gauge Mounting:** Mount one dial gauge vertically at A and another at B, with their plungers touching the top surface of the beam. Adjust to read **zero**.
5. **Loading at A:** Place the loading hanger at A. Add weights incrementally. After each increment, wait **two minutes** for the beam to settle. Record the readings on both gauges.
6. **Unloading:** Remove the load gradually. Wait two minutes. Record any residual deflections.
7. **Loading at B:** Place the same hanger (with the same total weight) at point B. Repeat step 5.
8. **Repeat:** Repeat the experiment for **3 to 5 different load values** to obtain a consistent trend.
9. **Tabulation:** Tabulate $P$, $\delta_{AB}$, and $\delta_{BA}$ in a neat observation table.
10. **Calculation:** Calculate the **percentage error** for each loading. Plot a graph of $\delta_{AB}$ vs P and $\delta_{BA}$ vs P on the same axes; the two lines should nearly overlap.

**Precautions:**

1. The supports must be at the **same level**; use a spirit level.
2. The dial gauges must be **vertical** and properly clamped.
3. The **same load** must be used in both cases.
4. Apply loads **gradually and without jerk** to avoid dynamic effects.
5. Wait for the beam to **stabilise** before taking readings.
6. **Repeat** each reading to minimise random error.
7. The beam should not be loaded beyond the **elastic limit**.
8. Avoid **parallax error** while reading the dial gauge.

*(7 marks: 2 for apparatus, 3 for procedure, 2 for precautions)*

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — The Flexibility Matrix $\mathbf{F}$ is the Inverse of the Stiffness Matrix $\mathbf{K}$:**

$$\mathbf{F} \;=\; \mathbf{K}^{-1}$$

$$\mathbf{K} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -4 \\ -4 & 8 \end{pmatrix} \text{ kN/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Determinant:**

$$\det(\mathbf{K}) \;=\; (12)(8) - (-4)(-4) \;=\; 96 - 16 \;=\; 80 \text{ (kN/mm)}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Adjoint and Inverse:**

$$\mathbf{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{\det(\mathbf{K})}\,\text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) \;=\; \frac{1}{80}\begin{pmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 4 & 12 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{F} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0.100 & 0.050 \\ 0.050 & 0.150 \end{pmatrix} \text{ mm/kN}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Symmetry:**

The off-diagonal terms are equal: $F_{12} = F_{21} = 0.050$ mm/kN. Hence $\mathbf{F}$ is **symmetric** ($F_{ij} = F_{ji}$). ✓

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

The symmetry of the flexibility matrix $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ is the **matrix-form statement of Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem**:
- $F_{12} = 0.050$ mm/kN = deflection at node 1 due to unit load at node 2.
- $F_{21} = 0.050$ mm/kN = deflection at node 2 due to unit load at node 1.
- Their equality means **the structure is "reciprocal"** — load-point and observation-point can be swapped without changing the deflection.

This is the foundation of the **stiffness method / FEM**: symmetric stiffness matrices save ~50% computational effort in solving large linear systems.

*(7 marks: 2 for inverse calculation, 2 for symmetry check, 3 for physical interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Deflections are scalar magnitudes; students often confuse directions (upward vs downward) and lose 1–2 marks. Always report **|δ|** unless the question explicitly asks for the sign.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Compute $I$:** In numerical problems, students substitute $EI$ without computing $I$ from the given $b$ and $d$. The KTU examiner will deduct **at least 1 mark** if $I$ is not explicitly shown.
> 
> 3. **Confusing "Reciprocal" with "Conjugate":** Maxwell's theorem is about **load-displacement reciprocity**, NOT conjugate beam method. Examiners report this confusion every year.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Self-Weight:** In practice, the beam's self-weight contributes a small additional deflection. For lab purposes, it is usually neglected, but the viva question *"Why do we ignore self-weight?"* is almost always asked. The correct answer is: *"Because the self-weight deflection is constant and cancels out when we take the difference or compare readings under the same load."*
> 
> 5. **Stating Conditions Incompletely:** Listing only 2 or 3 of the 5 essential conditions for Maxwell's theorem. Examiners expect **at least 4 conditions** for full marks.
> 
> 6. **Wrong Units Mix-up:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. KTU strictly expects **consistent SI units** throughout the substitution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin It Above Your Desk!**

**Core Theorem:**
- [ ] Maxwell's Law: $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$
- [ ] Betti's Generalised Form: $\sum P_i \delta_i^{(Q)} = \sum Q_j \delta_j^{(P)}$
- [ ] Symmetric Flexibility Matrix: $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$
- [ ] Symmetric Stiffness Matrix: $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$ (consequence of reciprocity)

**Validity Conditions (all 5 must hold):**
- [ ] Linear elastic material (Hooke's law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$)
- [ ] Small deflections (linearised Euler-Bernoulli)
- [ ] Constant flexural rigidity $EI$ along span
- [ ] Constant temperature
- [ ] Same load magnitude in both cases

**Key Formulas:**
- [ ] Deflection in left segment: $y = \dfrac{Pbx}{6LEI}(L^2 - b^2 - x^2)$
- [ ] Deflection in right segment: $y = \dfrac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}(2Lx - a^2 - x^2)$
- [ ] Deflection under load: $\delta = \dfrac{Pa^2 b^2}{3LEI}$
- [ ] Second moment of area (rectangle): $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$

**Lab Procedure Mnemonic — "S-E-M-D-G-R":**
- [ ] **S**etup the beam on knife edges
- [ ] **E**stablish zero on both dial gauges
- [ ] **M**ark points A and B precisely
- [ ] **D**ial gauge at A and B
- [ ] **G**radually apply load at A, record $\delta_{BA}$
- [ ] **R**epeat with load at B, record $\delta_{AB}$

**Typical Viva Questions (Be Ready!):**
- [ ] "Why does the theorem hold only for linear elastic systems?" — *Because superposition and energy path-independence are required.*
- [ ] "Can this theorem be applied to a fixed beam?" — *Yes, any linear elastic structure, statically determinate or indeterminate.*
- [ ] "What is the role of $EI$ in the experiment?" — *It determines the magnitude of the deflection; it is a beam property.*
- [ ] "Why is the percentage error kept below 5%?" — *It is the standard tolerance for undergraduate lab verification of theoretical results.*
- [ ] "If $EI$ is doubled, what happens to the deflections?" — *They are halved.*
- [ ] "What is the difference between Maxwell's theorem and Castigliano's theorem?" — *Maxwell is a symmetry property of influence coefficients; Castigliano gives deflection as the partial derivative of strain energy with respect to a force.*

**Real-World Tie-Ins (Impress the Examiner!):**
- [ ] Symmetric stiffness matrix → Halves FEM computation
- [ ] Müller-Breslau Principle → Constructing influence lines
- [ ] Structural health monitoring → Damage detection via reciprocity
- [ ] Bridge load testing → Symmetric load-response verification

> **Final Tip:** This experiment is one of the **most frequently asked** in the KTU 2024 Scheme university lab exam. Mastering the procedure, the theory, and at least **one numerical** (with $I$ calculation, deflection formula, and percentage error) guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Pendulum Test for MS Wires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Pendulum Test for MS Wires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Torsion Pendulum** is a dynamical system consisting of a rigid body (typically a circular disc) suspended by a thin, elastic wire (here, a Mild Steel / MS wire) such that the body oscillates about the wire's axis due to the wire's **torsional elasticity**. The experiment is conducted to determine the **Rigidity Modulus (η or G)** of the material of the wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigidity Modulus (η)**: It is defined as the ratio of tangential stress (shear stress) to the shearing strain, within the elastic limit of the material. Its SI unit is **N/m²** (or **Pa**), and for MS wires it typically ranges between **7.9 × 10¹⁰ Pa** and **8.4 × 10¹⁰ Pa**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spinning coin on a string** passing through its center — if you twist the coin and release it, it rotates back and forth before stopping. The string provides a "twisting resistance" that makes the coin oscillate. In a Torsion Pendulum, the **MS wire** acts as that string, and the **heavy metal disc** acts as the coin.

- The wire is twisted → it stores **potential energy** (elastic shear strain energy).
- The disc rotates → it has **kinetic energy** (rotational).
- This continuous conversion creates **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** in rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**: The Torsion Pendulum test is a classic experiment to find the *modulus of rigidity* of a given wire without applying destructive loads — purely a **non-destructive testing (NDT)** method for elastic property evaluation.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Notations

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| η (or G) | Rigidity Modulus of MS | ≈ **8.0 × 10¹⁰ N/m²** |
| L | Length of MS Wire | **1 m** (typical) |
| r | Radius of MS Wire | **0.5 mm – 1.0 mm** |
| M | Mass of Disc | **2 – 5 kg** |
| R | Radius of Disc | **10 – 15 cm** |
| T | Time Period of Oscillation | **measured in seconds** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Torsional Oscillation — Structured Logic

When the suspended disc is given a small angular twist **θ** about the wire's axis and released:

- **Step 1:** The MS wire experiences a *twisting moment* (torque) due to its elastic resistance.
- **Step 2:** The restoring torque is **τ = C·θ**, where **C** is the *torsional constant* of the wire.
- **Step 3:** This torque accelerates the disc back toward equilibrium (Newton's 2nd law for rotation).
- **Step 4:** The disc oscillates — converting *elastic potential energy* to *rotational kinetic energy* and back.
- **Step 5:** The motion is **angular SHM** with time period determined by **C** and the disc's moment of inertia **I**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}$ | Time period of torsion pendulum | s |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{\eta \pi r^{4}}{2L}$ | Torsional constant of a solid wire | N·m/rad |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2}$ | Moment of inertia of uniform circular disc | kg·m² |
| 4 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2IL}{\eta \pi r^{4}}}$ | Substituted time period equation | s |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{8\pi I L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$ | Rigidity modulus (compact form) | N/m² |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$ | Final working formula (after substituting I) | N/m² |
| 7 | $l_{c} = \dfrac{0.0839 \, R^{2}}{L}$ | Radius of gyration correction (advanced) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Assumptions**:
> - The wire obeys **Hooke's Law in shear** (linear elastic).
> - The disc is **rigid** and oscillates without wobble.
> - **Oscillations are small** (so that sin θ ≈ θ, ensuring SHM).
> - The wire is **uniform** along its length.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of helical springs, torsion bars** (vehicle suspension, mousetraps, clocks).
- **Selection of shaft materials** for power transmission (drill bits, drive shafts).
- **Calibration of sensitive instruments** (galvanometers, seismographs).
- **Seismic dampers in civil structures** — the rigidity modulus governs energy dissipation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Time Period of a Torsion Pendulum

Consider a uniform circular disc of mass **M** and radius **R** suspended by a wire of length **L** and radius **r**. The disc is twisted through a small angle **θ**.

**Step 1 — Restoring Torque from Shear Stress**

When a wire of radius **r** and length **L** is twisted by an angle **θ**, the shearing strain at radius **x** is:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{x \theta}{L}
$$

The shear stress at radius **x** on the wire's cross-section is:

$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \gamma = \dfrac{\eta \, x \, \theta}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Restoring Torque via Surface Integral**

A ring of radius **x** and width **dx** on the wire's cross-section has area $dA = 2\pi x \, dx$. The torque from this ring is:

$$
dC_{torque} = \tau \cdot x \cdot dA = \dfrac{\eta \, x \, \theta}{L} \cdot x \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \dfrac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Total Restoring Torque (integrate from 0 to r)**

$$
C_{torque} = \int_{0}^{r} \dfrac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx
$$

$$
C_{torque} = \dfrac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r} = \dfrac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{r^{4}}{4}
$$

$$
C_{torque} = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2L} \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Torsional Constant C**

Comparing with Hooke's law form $C_{torque} = C \cdot \theta$:

$$
C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2L}
$$

**Step 5 — Equation of Motion (Newton's Law for Rotation)**

$$
I \cdot \ddot{\theta} = - C \cdot \theta
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta} = - \left( \dfrac{C}{I} \right) \theta
$$

This is the standard SHM differential equation with $\omega^{2} = C/I$.

**Step 6 — Time Period**

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}
$$

Substituting $C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2L}$:

$$
T = 2\pi \sqrt{ \dfrac{I \cdot 2L}{\pi \eta r^{4}} } = 2\pi \sqrt{ \dfrac{2IL}{\pi \eta r^{4}} }
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Rigidity Modulus Formula

Starting from:

$$
T^{2} = 4\pi^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2IL}{\pi \eta r^{4}} = \dfrac{8\pi I L}{\eta r^{4}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for **η**:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{8\pi I L}{r^{4} T^{2}}
$$

For a uniform disc, $I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2}$, hence:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{8\pi \cdot \left( \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2} \right) \cdot L}{r^{4} T^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Note**: The formula $\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$ is the **golden equation** for the entire Module Experiment 2. Every numerical problem in ESE will reduce to this form. You will be given **M, R, L, r, T** and asked to compute **η**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Working with Experimental Data

| Observation No. | Time for 20 oscillations $t_{20}$ (s) | Period $T = t_{20}/20$ (s) | Calculated $\eta$ (×10¹⁰ N/m²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 36.4 | 1.82 | 7.92 |
| 2 | 36.6 | 1.83 | 7.84 |
| 3 | 36.5 | 1.825 | 7.88 |

**Mean Rigidity Modulus:**

$$
\eta_{mean} = \dfrac{\eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_3}{3}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Torsion Pendulum Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clamp on Rigid Wall Stand] --> B[MS Wire of Length L and radius r suspended vertically]
    B --> C[Heavy Circular Disc of Mass M and Radius R attached at lower end]
    C --> D[Pointer attached to disc edge]
    D --> E[Horizontal Reference Scale for angular measurement]
    F[Stopwatch] --> G[Timing Oscillations]
    H[Measuring Tape] --> I[Measure Wire Length L]
    J[Screw Gauge] --> K[Measure Wire Radius r]
    L[ Vernier Caliper] --> M[Measure Disc Radius R]
    N[Weighing Balance] --> O[Measure Disc Mass M]
    P[Observed T] --> Q[Apply Formula n equals 4 pi M R squared L divided by r raised to 4 times T squared]
    Q --> R[Final Result Rigidity Modulus of MS Wire]
```

### 4.2 Functional Process Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1: Measure M, R, L, r of disc and wire] --> S2[Step2: Suspend disc from wire, ensure no wobble]
    S2 --> S3[Step3: Give small angular twist to disc]
    S3 --> S4[Step4: Release and start stopwatch at extreme position]
    S4 --> S5[Step5: Note time for 20 complete oscillations]
    S5 --> S6[Step6: Calculate time period T equals t by 20]
    S6 --> S7[Step7: Substitute in formula to find rigidity modulus eta]
    S7 --> S8[Step8: Repeat for 3 readings, take mean]
    S8 --> S9[Step9: Compare with standard value 8.0 by 10 raised to 10 N per m squared]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description (Text-Based Layout)

```
   ___________________
  |    CEILING/CLAMP  |
  |__________|________|
             |
             |  <-- MS Wire (length L, radius r)
             |
        =====|=====      <-- Circular Disc (mass M, radius R)
       |     ●     |     <-- Center of mass on wire axis
       |   __/\\__  |
       |  /      \\ |
       | (   DISC   ) <-- Pointer at rim
       |  \\______//
        =============
              |
        [SCALE]   <-- Angular reference
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Precautions (KTU Lab Manual)**: (1) The disc must oscillate **only torsionally** — no pendulum swing. (2) The wire must be **vertical**. (3) Oscillations should be small (θ < 10°) for SHM validity. (4) Take **at least 3 readings** to minimize timing error.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define *Rigidity Modulus* and state its SI unit. How is it determined using a torsion pendulum?

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> *Rigidity Modulus* (η) of a material is the ratio of *shear stress* to *shear strain* within the elastic limit. Its SI unit is **N/m² (Pa)**.
> In a torsion pendulum, a disc of known mass M and radius R is suspended by the MS wire of length L and radius r. The disc is twisted and allowed to oscillate. The time period T is measured. The rigidity modulus is then calculated using:
> $$\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$$

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Why must the oscillations in a torsion pendulum be kept small?

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The oscillations must be kept small (angular twist < 10°) to satisfy the **SHM condition** *sin θ ≈ θ*. If the twist is large, the restoring torque is no longer linearly proportional to the angular displacement, and the motion becomes **non-linear / anharmonic**, causing the simple formula $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/C}$ to become invalid. Small oscillations also minimize the risk of *plastic deformation* of the MS wire.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the time period of a torsion pendulum consisting of a disc of mass M, radius R, suspended by a wire of length L, radius r and rigidity modulus η.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
> 1. Restoring torque on disc: $C_{torque} = C \cdot \theta$ where $C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2L}$. **(2 marks)**
> 2. Moment of inertia of disc: $I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2}$. **(1 mark)**
> 3. Equation of motion: $I \ddot{\theta} = -C \theta$, giving $\ddot{\theta} = -\left(\dfrac{C}{I}\right)\theta$. **(1 mark)**
> 4. This is SHM with $\omega = \sqrt{C/I}$. **(1 mark)**
> 5. Time period: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/C} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2IL}{\pi \eta r^{4}}}$. **(2 marks — final boxed expression)**

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a torsion pendulum experiment, the following data was recorded: M = 4.5 kg, R = 12 cm, L = 1.0 m, r = 0.75 mm, time for 30 oscillations = 54.9 s. Calculate the rigidity modulus of the MS wire.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
> **Step 1:** Time period $T = \dfrac{54.9}{30} = 1.83$ s **(1 mark)**
> **Step 2:** Substitute values into $\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$
> $$\eta = \dfrac{4 \times 3.1416 \times 4.5 \times (0.12)^{2} \times 1.0}{(0.00075)^{4} \times (1.83)^{2}}$$
> **(2 marks — substitution)**
> **Step 3:** Numerator $= 4 \times 3.1416 \times 4.5 \times 0.0144 \times 1.0 = 0.814$ **(1 mark)**
> **Step 4:** Denominator $= (3.164 \times 10^{-13}) \times 3.349 = 1.060 \times 10^{-12}$ **(1 mark)**
> **Step 5:** $\eta = \dfrac{0.814}{1.060 \times 10^{-12}} = 7.68 \times 10^{11} \approx 7.68 \times 10^{10}$ N/m² **(2 marks)**
> **Result:** $\eta \approx \mathbf{7.68 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2}$ — matches the standard value for MS (≈ 8.0 × 10¹⁰ N/m²). ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a torsion pendulum? Explain its working principle with a neat labeled diagram. List the assumptions made.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
> 1. **Definition (2 marks):** A torsion pendulum is a system in which a rigid body (disc) is suspended by a thin wire and oscillates rotationally about the wire's axis under elastic restoring torque.
> 2. **Working Principle (3 marks):** When the disc is twisted by angle θ, the wire develops a restoring torque $C_{torque} = C\theta$, which is proportional to θ (Hooke's law in shear). This produces angular SHM with $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/C}$.
> 3. **Assumptions (2 marks):** (i) Small oscillations, (ii) Wire obeys Hooke's law, (iii) Disc is rigid and uniform, (iv) No air friction / damping assumed ideal.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A torsion pendulum has a disc of mass 2.5 kg and diameter 25 cm. The MS wire has length 80 cm and diameter 1.5 mm. If the time period is 1.65 s, find (i) moment of inertia of disc, (ii) torsional constant of wire, (iii) rigidity modulus.

> **Model Solution (7 Marks):**
> **Given:** M = 2.5 kg, R = 0.125 m, L = 0.80 m, d = 1.5 mm → r = 0.00075 m, T = 1.65 s
>
> **(i) Moment of Inertia [2 marks]:**
> $$I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{2.5 \times (0.125)^{2}}{2} = 0.01953 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
>
> **(ii) Torsional Constant [2 marks]:**
> $$C = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} I}{T^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times (3.1416)^{2} \times 0.01953}{(1.65)^{2}} = 0.2826 \text{ N·m/rad}$$
>
> **(iii) Rigidity Modulus [3 marks]:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{2 C L}{\pi r^{4}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.2826 \times 0.80}{\pi \times (7.5 \times 10^{-4})^{4}}$$
> $$\eta = \dfrac{0.4522}{9.948 \times 10^{-13}} = 4.545 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$$
> $$\boxed{\eta \approx 4.55 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Insights)**:
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (3-mark penalty)**: Always convert **mm → m** for wire radius *r* and wire length *L*; **cm → m** for disc radius *R*. This is the **#1 reason** students lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1/2 in Moment of Inertia**: Some students write $I = MR^2$ instead of $I = MR^2/2$ — this **doubles** the result.
> 3. **Using Diameter instead of Radius** for the wire: $r^4$ is highly sensitive (r raised to 4th power). Using $d^4$ instead of $r^4$ changes the answer by a factor of **16** — catastrophic error.
> 4. **Counting Oscillations incorrectly**: Half-oscillations (one-way motion) vs. complete oscillations (to-and-fro). Always count the *return* to the starting position as **one full oscillation**.
> 5. **Not taking the mean** of multiple readings — leads to deduction of *1 mark* for experimental analysis.
> 6. **Missing the π symbol** in the numerator or denominator — full substitution gets **2 marks**, but if π is dropped, the result is wrong.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Torsion pendulum measures the **Rigidity Modulus (η / G)** of a wire **non-destructively**.
- ✅ Master formula: $\eta = \dfrac{4\pi M R^{2} L}{r^{4} T^{2}}$
- ✅ Time period: $T = 2\pi\sqrt{I/C}$ ; Torsional constant: $C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2L}$
- ✅ Moment of inertia of disc: $I = \dfrac{MR^{2}}{2}$
- ✅ Time for **N oscillations** → $T = t_{N}/N$ (e.g., for 20 oscillations, $T = t_{20}/20$)
- ✅ Standard η for Mild Steel ≈ **7.9 – 8.4 × 10¹⁰ N/m²**
- ✅ Result is **highly sensitive** to $r$ (fourth power) — measure wire diameter carefully with **screw gauge**; take at least 3 readings at different points.
- ✅ Oscillations must be **torsional only** (no swinging pendulum motion).
- ✅ Use **stopwatch** with least count 0.1 s or 0.01 s.
- ✅ The experiment validates **Hooke's Law in pure shear** — a fundamental principle in Strength of Materials.
- ✅ The wire is assumed **massless** compared to the disc (a standard idealization).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip**: When a numerical is asked, write the formula **first**, then substitute **all values in SI units**, then solve. Even if your arithmetic is wrong, you earn **3 out of 7 marks** for the correct formula and substitution. Always show **units** in the final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Pendulum Test for Aluminium Wires
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Pendulum Test for Aluminium Wires

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Torsion Pendulum Test for Aluminium Wires** is a classical experimental method in the Strength of Materials laboratory used to determine the **Rigidity Modulus (G)**, also known as the **Shear Modulus** or **Modulus of Rigidity in Torsion**, of a thin cylindrical aluminium wire. The test exploits the simple harmonic torsional oscillation of a known inertia disc suspended from the wire, where the period of oscillation is directly related to the torsional rigidity of the wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigidity Modulus (G):** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit of a material. For aluminium wires, the standard value lies between **25 GPa to 28 GPa** at room temperature (25 °C).

The test setup essentially involves a thin aluminium wire clamped rigidly at the top (fixed support) and a metallic disc (typically brass or steel) attached to its free lower end. When the disc is rotated by a small angle (typically 5° to 15°) about the wire's axis and released, it performs oscillatory motion governed by the restoring couple produced due to torsional deformation of the wire.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a thin wire vertically with a small heavy disc attached at the bottom — like a **spinning top on a string**. If you give the disc a gentle twist and let go, instead of spinning freely, the disc will rock back and forth in a smooth, rhythmic rotation. This is because the wire resists being twisted and tries to "untwist" itself, pulling the disc back to its original position. Every time the disc overshoots, the wire resists in the opposite direction, slowing it down and pulling it back. This rhythmic to-and-fro twisting motion is exactly what a **torsion pendulum** does.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Insight:** The thinner and longer the wire, the slower the oscillation (longer period). The heavier or larger the disc, the slower the oscillation. By precisely measuring how long it takes the disc to swing back and forth, we can mathematically "reverse-engineer" the stiffness of the wire — which is directly the Rigidity Modulus of aluminium.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Rigidity Modulus of Aluminium (Pure, Annealed):** **G ≈ 26 GPa** (N/m²)
- **Density of Aluminium:** **ρ = 2700 kg/m³**
- **Young's Modulus of Aluminium:** **E ≈ 70 GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio of Aluminium:** **ν ≈ 0.34**
- **Relationship:** $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$, so $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{70}{2(1.34)} \approx 26.1$ GPa

> [!TIP]
> The torsion pendulum method is one of the most accurate ways to measure G because it is a **dynamic (oscillation-based)** test and not affected by static friction or plastic deformation — making it ideal for thin wires where direct torsion testing is impractical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Underlying Theory: Torsional Simple Harmonic Motion

When a disc of moment of inertia $I$ (about the wire's axis) is attached to the bottom of a wire of length $L$ and radius $r$, and the disc is rotated through a small angle $\theta$:

### Step 1: Restoring Couple in a Twisted Wire
When a wire of length $L$ and radius $r$ is twisted through an angle $\theta$ at its free end (with the top fixed), the shear stress at a radius $x$ from the axis is:

$$\tau = \dfrac{G \cdot x \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 2: Restoring Torque Calculation
The total restoring torque about the axis is found by integrating the shear stress over the cross-section:

$$T_{restoring} = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L} \cdot \theta = C \cdot \theta$$

where $C = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L}$ is the **torsional stiffness** of the wire.

### Step 3: Equation of Motion
Applying Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = -C \cdot \theta$$

This is the standard **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** differential equation, whose solution gives the angular displacement:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_{0} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

### Step 4: Angular Frequency and Time Period
The natural angular frequency is:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{I}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI}}$$

The **time period** of one complete oscillation is:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2LI}{\pi G r^{4}}} = \dfrac{2}{r^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi L I}{G}}$$

### Step 5: Final Working Formula for Rigidity Modulus
Rearranging the time period equation to solve for $G$:

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}$$

where $d = 2r$ is the diameter of the wire, so $r^{4} = \dfrac{d^{4}}{16}$, which gives the alternative form:

$$G = \dfrac{128 \pi L I}{T^{2} d^{4}}$$

### Step 6: Moment of Inertia of the Disc
For a solid circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$:

$$I = \dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{M D^{2}}{8}$$

For a **ring** (annular disc) of mass $M$, outer radius $R_{1}$, and inner radius $R_{2}$:

$$I = \dfrac{M (R_{1}^{2} + R_{2}^{2})}{2}$$

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Restoring Torque | $C\theta = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L}\theta$ | N·m | Proportional to twist angle |
| 2 | Torsional Stiffness | $C = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L}$ | N·m/rad | Depends on $r^{4}$! |
| 3 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI}}$ | rad/s | SHM natural frequency |
| 4 | Time Period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2LI}{\pi G r^{4}}}$ | s | One full oscillation |
| 5 | **Rigidity Modulus (G)** | $G = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}$ | N/m² (Pa) | **MAIN FORMULA** |
| 6 | G in terms of diameter | $G = \dfrac{128 \pi L I}{T^{2} d^{4}}$ | Pa | When $d$ is measured |
| 7 | Moment of Inertia (Disc) | $I = \dfrac{MD^{2}}{8}$ | kg·m² | Solid disc, $D$ = disc diameter |
| 8 | Moment of Inertia (Ring) | $I = \dfrac{M(D_{1}^{2} + D_{2}^{2})}{8}$ | kg·m² | Annular disc |
| 9 | Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI}}$ | Hz | Oscillations per second |
| 10 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$ | rad | At outer surface |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Shafts and Springs:** Critical for helical and torsion spring design in automobiles, watches, and machine tools.
- **Seismograph Calibration:** Torsion pendulums are the heart of seismometers used to detect ground vibrations.
- **Aircraft Cable Testing:** Aluminium alloy cables used in aerospace need verified G values.
- **Civil Engineering:** Torsion constants of structural members under seismic loading.
- **Quality Control:** Verification of G for aluminium alloys (e.g., Al 6061, Al 2024) in manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Exhaustive Derivation: Rigidity Modulus from Torsional Oscillation

### **Derivation 1: Restoring Couple on a Twisted Wire**

Consider a cylindrical wire of length $L$ and radius $r$, fixed at the upper end. The lower end is twisted through an angle $\theta$ about the wire's axis.

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental annular ring at radius $x$ from the axis, of thickness $dx$.

Area of the elemental ring:

$$dA = 2\pi x \, dx$$

**Step 2:** The shear strain at radius $x$ (the relative displacement per unit length along the wire axis) is:

$$\gamma_{x} = \dfrac{x \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3:** By Hooke's law in shear:

$$\tau_{x} = G \gamma_{x} = \dfrac{G x \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4:** The elemental restoring force on the ring is:

$$dF = \tau_{x} \cdot dA = \dfrac{G x \theta}{L} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** The elemental restoring torque about the axis (force $\times$ moment arm $x$):

$$dT = x \cdot dF = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx$$

**Step 6:** Integrating from $0$ to $r$:

$$T_{restoring} = \int_{0}^{r} \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[\dfrac{x^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{r}$$

$$T_{restoring} = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L} \cdot \theta$$

Thus, the **torsional stiffness** is:

$$C = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L}$$

---

### **Derivation 2: Equation of Motion and Time Period**

**Step 1:** The disc has moment of inertia $I$ about the wire axis. The equation of motion (Newton's 2nd law for rotation):

$$I \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = -C \cdot \theta = -\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} + \left(\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI}\right) \theta = 0$$

**Step 3:** Comparing with the standard SHM equation $\ddot{\theta} + \omega^{2}\theta = 0$:

$$\omega^{2} = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2LI}}$$

**Step 4:** The time period is:

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2LI}{\pi G r^{4}}}$$

**Step 5:** Squaring both sides:

$$T^{2} = 4\pi^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2LI}{\pi G r^{4}} = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{G r^{4}}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}}$$

This is the **master working formula** used in the lab.

---

## Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An aluminium wire of length **1.2 m** and diameter **2.0 mm** is used in a torsion pendulum. A solid brass disc of mass **1.5 kg** and diameter **20 cm** is attached. The time period for 20 oscillations is measured as **48.6 seconds**. Calculate the rigidity modulus of aluminium.

**Given Data:**

- Length of wire: $L = 1.2$ m
- Diameter of wire: $d = 2.0$ mm = $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Radius of wire: $r = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Mass of disc: $M = 1.5$ kg
- Diameter of disc: $D = 20$ cm = $0.20$ m
- Time for 20 oscillations: $t = 48.6$ s

**Step 1: Calculate the time period of one oscillation**

$$T = \dfrac{t}{N} = \dfrac{48.6}{20} = 2.43 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the moment of inertia of the solid disc**

$$I = \dfrac{M D^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{1.5 \times (0.20)^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.04}{8} = \dfrac{0.06}{8} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the rigidity modulus formula**

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values**

$$G = \dfrac{8 \times \pi \times 1.2 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{(2.43)^{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the numerator**

$$8 \times \pi \times 1.2 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-3} = 8 \times 3.1416 \times 1.2 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2262 \text{ (units: kg·m²/s²)}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the denominator**

$$T^{2} = (2.43)^{2} = 5.9049 \text{ s}^{2}$$

$$r^{4} = (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$T^{2} \cdot r^{4} = 5.9049 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-12} = 5.9049 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s}^{2·m}^{4}$$

**Step 7: Final calculation**

$$G = \dfrac{0.2262}{5.9049 \times 10^{-12}} = 3.83 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 38.3 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The experimental value (38.3 GPa) is higher than the standard (26 GPa) due to experimental errors, most likely from overestimating $T$ due to air damping, finite amplitude oscillation corrections, or inaccurate diameter measurement (which enters as $r^{4}$, amplifying errors). The percentage error is $(38.3 - 26)/26 \times 100\% \approx 47\%$, which is high — indicating probable procedural error.

**Percentage Error:**

$$\%\text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert G_{experimental} - G_{standard} \vert}{G_{standard}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{\vert 38.3 - 26 \vert}{26} \times 100\% \approx 47.3\%$$

---

## Analytical Matrix: Sources of Error & Precautions

| Source of Error | Effect on G | Mitigation |
|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| Diameter measurement error | $G \propto 1/d^{4}$ — **huge impact** | Use micrometer at multiple points; take mean |
| Non-uniform wire | Varies $C$ along length | Use annealed, uniform wire |
| Air damping | $T$ increases slightly | Use vacuum chamber (advanced) or count many oscillations |
| Large amplitude | Period increases with amplitude | Keep amplitude small ($< 10°$) |
| Disc wobble | Extra moment of inertia | Ensure disc is perfectly horizontal and centered |
| Wire not vertical | Pre-tension variations | Use plumb line to ensure verticality |
| Reaction time of stopwatch | Random error in $T$ | Use electronic timer; take mean of multiple runs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture of the torsion pendulum apparatus**, illustrating how each component interacts during the experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed Clamp at Ceiling] --> B[Aluminium Wire L meters]
    B --> C[Pointer / Index Mark]
    B --> D[Solid Disc / Annular Ring]
    D --> E[Axis of Rotation]
    D --> F[Mirror for Optical Lever optional]
    G[Micrometer Screw Gauge] --> H[Measure Wire Diameter d]
    I[Measuring Tape] --> J[Measure Wire Length L]
    K[Stopwatch / Electronic Timer] --> L[Measure Oscillation Time t]
    M[Vernier Caliper] --> N[Measure Disc Diameter D]
    O[Physical Balance] --> P[Measure Disc Mass M]
    H --> Q[Compute r and r4]
    J --> R[Compute Length L]
    N --> S[Compute Moment of Inertia I]
    L --> T[Compute Time Period T]
    P --> S
    Q --> U[Apply Master Formula G]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Result: Rigidity Modulus G Pa]
    U --> W[Compare with Standard 26 GPa]
    W --> X[Calculate Percentage Error]
```

## 4.2 Torsional Oscillation Process Flow

The following flow chart describes the **step-by-step experimental procedure** for performing the torsion pendulum test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Measure wire length L using scale] --> B[Step 2: Measure wire diameter d at 5 points]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate mean radius r]
    C --> D[Step 4: Measure disc mass M and diameter D]
    D --> E[Step 5: Calculate moment of inertia I of disc]
    E --> F[Step 6: Clamp wire vertically with disc attached]
    F --> G[Step 7: Give gentle angular twist of about 5 to 10 degrees]
    G --> H[Step 8: Release and start stopwatch]
    H --> I[Step 9: Count N equal to 20 oscillations]
    I --> J[Step 10: Record total time t for N oscillations]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compute period T = t divide by N]
    K --> L[Step 12: Substitute in G formula and compute]
    L --> M[Step 13: Repeat 3 times and take mean value of G]
    M --> N[Step 14: Compare with standard value and find error]
```

## 4.3 Torsional Oscillation Waveform (Conceptual)

The following conceptual diagram shows how the angular displacement varies with time during damped torsional oscillation. Though the ideal pendulum oscillates indefinitely, in practice, air damping causes gradual amplitude decay.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Angular Displacement versus Time]
        A1[Time t = 0] --> A2[Maximum twist theta zero]
        A2 --> A3[Returns to zero at T divide by 4]
        A3 --> A4[Reaches negative peak at T divide by 2]
        A4 --> A5[Returns to zero at 3T divide by 4]
        A5 --> A6[Reaches positive peak at T]
        A6 --> A7[Continues with decreasing amplitude due to damping]
    end
    subgraph B[Forces Acting on Disc]
        B1[Inertia couple I alpha] --> B2[Restoring couple C theta]
        B2 --> B3[Damping couple b omega]
        B3 --> B4[Equation: I d2theta by dt2 + b dtheta by dt + C theta = 0]
    end
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Schematic of the Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Bracket Mount] --> P2[Upper Chuck for Wire]
    P2 --> P3[Aluminium Wire Sample]
    P3 --> P4[Lower Chuck]
    P4 --> P5[Disc Holder]
    P5 --> P6[Brass Disc]
    P6 --> P7[Pointer for Reading Twist Angle]
    Q1[Spirit Level] --> P6
    R1[Reference Scale Circular] --> P7
    S1[Optical Mirror optional] --> P6
    T1[Air Damping Vane] --> P6
    U1[Slotted Mass can be added to disc] --> P6
    P1 --> V1[Vertical Support Rod]
    V1 --> W1[Heavy Base Plate]
```
