<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define rigidity modulus. How is it experimentally determined using a torsion pendulum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **rigidity modulus (G)**, also called the **shear modulus** or **modulus of rigidity in torsion**, is defined as the ratio of **shear stress (τ)** to **shear strain (γ)** within the elastic limit of a material. Mathematically:

$$G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

It has units of **N/m²** or **Pa**, and for most metals lies in the range of **25 to 110 GPa**.

In a **torsion pendulum experiment**, a wire of the test material (aluminium) is fixed at the top with a disc of known moment of inertia $I$ suspended at the bottom. The disc is twisted slightly and released; the resulting torsional oscillations have a time period $T$ given by:

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2LI}{\pi G r^{4}}}$$

Rearranging, the rigidity modulus is:

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}$$

By measuring $L$, $r$, $I$, and $T$ experimentally, $G$ is calculated. **(3 marks complete)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the relationship between Young's modulus (E), rigidity modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν). Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship between the three elastic constants is:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Significance:**

- It shows that the three constants are **not independent** — knowing any two determines the third.
- For isotropic materials like aluminium, all three constants are related and must satisfy thermodynamic constraints: $-1 \lt \nu \lt 0.5$ and $E, G \gt 0$.
- For aluminium: $E \approx 70$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.34$, giving $G = E / [2(1+\nu)] = 70 / 2.68 \approx 26.1$ GPa.
- The relationship is useful for verifying experimental values of any one modulus by cross-checking with the other two. **(3 marks complete)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Set]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the rigidity modulus of a material of a wire using a torsion pendulum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminium wire of length 1.5 m and diameter 1.5 mm is used in a torsion pendulum with a disc of mass 2 kg and diameter 25 cm. If the time for 25 oscillations is 60 seconds, determine the rigidity modulus and compare it with the standard value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a): Derivation (7 marks)**

**Given:** Wire of length $L$, radius $r$, disc of moment of inertia $I$.

**Step 1:** When the wire is twisted by angle $\theta$, the shear strain at radius $x$ is:

$$\gamma_{x} = \dfrac{x \theta}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Shear stress by Hooke's law: $\tau_{x} = G \gamma_{x} = \dfrac{G x \theta}{L}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Restoring force on element of area $dA = 2\pi x \, dx$:

$$dF = \tau_{x} \, dA = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{2} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Restoring torque: $dT = x \, dF = \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Integrating over the cross-section:

$$T_{restoring} = \int_{0}^{r} \dfrac{2\pi G \theta}{L} x^{3} \, dx = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L} \cdot \theta$$

Thus, the torsional stiffness: $C = \dfrac{\pi G r^{4}}{2L}$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 6:** Equation of SHM: $I \ddot{\theta} = -C \theta \Rightarrow \ddot{\theta} + \omega^{2} \theta = 0$ where $\omega = \sqrt{C/I}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Time period: $T = 2\pi / \omega = 2\pi \sqrt{2LI / (\pi G r^{4})}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 8:** Final expression: $\boxed{G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}}$ **(1 mark — boxed answer mandatory for full credit)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b): Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5$ m
- $d = 1.5$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.75 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $M = 2$ kg, $D = 0.25$ m
- $N = 25$ oscillations, $t = 60$ s

**Step 1: Time period**

$$T = \dfrac{t}{N} = \dfrac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ s} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Moment of inertia of solid disc**

$$I = \dfrac{MD^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{2 \times (0.25)^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0625}{8} = \dfrac{0.125}{8} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg·m}^{2} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $r^{4}$**

$$r^{4} = (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 0.3164 \times 10^{-12} = 3.164 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^{4} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $T^{2}$**

$$T^{2} = (2.4)^{2} = 5.76 \text{ s}^{2} \quad \text{...(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute in G formula**

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}} = \dfrac{8 \times 3.1416 \times 1.5 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-2}}{5.76 \times 3.164 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$G = \dfrac{0.5890}{1.8225 \times 10^{-12}} = 3.232 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 32.32 \text{ GPa} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 6: Comparison with standard**

Standard value: $G_{std} = 26$ GPa. Percentage error:

$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 32.32 - 26 \vert}{26} \times 100\% = 24.3\% \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Conclusion:** The experimental value is higher than the standard due to errors in diameter measurement (which has a fourth-power effect) and air damping. The deviation is acceptable within ±25% for a basic lab setup. **(0.5 mark for conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Set]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a torsion pendulum. State the formula used to determine the rigidity modulus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A torsion pendulum with an aluminium wire of length 1.0 m and diameter 2 mm has a disc of mass 1.2 kg and diameter 18 cm. The time for 30 complete oscillations is observed to be 70.5 seconds. Calculate: (i) the moment of inertia of the disc, (ii) the time period, and (iii) the rigidity modulus of aluminium. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a): Descriptive (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A torsion pendulum consists of:

1. A **rigid ceiling clamp** (or wall-mounted bracket) to fix the top of the wire.
2. An **aluminium wire** of length ~1 m and diameter ~1–2 mm clamped between two chucks.
3. A **circular disc** (brass or steel) suspended at the lower end of the wire, with a pointer to indicate rotation.
4. A **graduated circular scale** behind the disc to measure angular twist.
5. A **stopwatch** for timing oscillations.
6. A **micrometer screw gauge** for wire diameter and **vernier caliper** for disc diameter.
7. A **spirit level** to ensure the disc is perfectly horizontal.

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 for visual reference.)

**Working (3 marks):**

- The wire is fixed at the top, disc suspended at the bottom.
- The disc is rotated gently through a small angle (5°–10°) in the horizontal plane, twisting the wire.
- On release, the disc executes **torsional simple harmonic motion** — rotating back and forth about the wire's axis.
- The restoring torque is provided by the elastic shear resistance of the wire.
- The time $t$ for $N$ oscillations is measured using a stopwatch; the time period $T = t/N$.
- The rigidity modulus is calculated from the formula:

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}}$$

**Formula and significance (1 mark):** The formula gives G in Pa (N/m²). For aluminium, the expected value is around **26 GPa**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b): Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 1.0$ m
- $d = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m $\Rightarrow r = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $M = 1.2$ kg
- $D = 0.18$ m
- $N = 30$ oscillations
- $t = 70.5$ s

**(i) Moment of Inertia of disc (2 marks):**

$$I = \dfrac{M D^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (0.18)^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.0324}{8} = \dfrac{0.03888}{8} = 4.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Time Period (1.5 marks):**

$$T = \dfrac{t}{N} = \dfrac{70.5}{30} = 2.35 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Rigidity Modulus (3.5 marks):**

Compute $r^{4} = (1 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 1 \times 10^{-12}$ m⁴
Compute $T^{2} = (2.35)^{2} = 5.5225$ s²

$$G = \dfrac{8 \pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}} = \dfrac{8 \times 3.1416 \times 1.0 \times 4.86 \times 10^{-3}}{5.5225 \times 1 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$G = \dfrac{0.1222}{5.5225 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.213 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 22.13 \text{ GPa}$$

**Result:** $G \approx 22.13$ GPa, which is within ~15% of the standard 26 GPa — a reasonable experimental result. **(0.5 mark for final statement)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $r^{4}$ factor** — Students often use $r^{2}$ or $d$ instead of $r^{4}$ in the denominator. This is the #1 reason for massive errors. *Always double-check the power.*
>
> 2. **Mixing up moment of inertia formulas** — For a **solid disc**: $I = MD^{2}/8$. For a **ring**: $I = M(D_1^{2} + D_2^{2})/8$. For a **thin rod about center**: $I = ML^{2}/12$. **Read the question carefully!**
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Length must be in **meters**, diameter in **meters**, mass in **kg**. Converting to cm and forgetting leads to errors of $10^{4}$ to $10^{8}$!
>
> 4. **Wrong period calculation** — Some students use the time of **one oscillation** as the period, but the experiment measures the time for $N$ oscillations. Period $T = t_{total} / N$.
>
> 5. **No percentage error calculation** — KTU awards full credit only when experimental value is compared with the standard value and % error is computed.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units of G** — Always state $G$ in **N/m² (Pa)** or **GPa**. Naked numbers lose marks.
>
> 7. **Missing the "justify" or "compare" part** — When asked to "compare with standard value", the percentage error and a brief comment on possible sources of error are mandatory.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Torsion Pendulum Test for Aluminium Wires**

- **Principle:** Torsional oscillations of a disc suspended by a wire follow SHM with period $T = 2\pi\sqrt{2LI/(\pi G r^{4})}$.

- **Master Formula (MUST memorize):**

$$G = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{T^{2} r^{4}} = \dfrac{128 \pi L I}{T^{2} d^{4}}$$

- **For solid disc:** $I = MD^{2}/8$; **For ring:** $I = M(D_1^{2}+D_2^{2})/8$.

- **Torsional stiffness:** $C = \pi G r^{4}/(2L)$ — note the $r^{4}$ dependence, which means diameter errors are amplified **four-fold**.

- **Standard Rigidity Modulus of Aluminium:** $\approx 26$ GPa (annealed, pure); alloy values range from **25 to 28 GPa**.

- **Procedure Order:** Measure $L$ → Measure $d$ at multiple points → Calculate $I$ → Twist & release → Time $N$ oscillations → Compute $T$ → Apply formula → Compare with standard.

- **Key Precautions:**
  * Use thin, uniform, defect-free aluminium wire.
  * Wire must be **vertical** (use plumb line).
  * Twist angle must be **small** ($< 10°$).
  * Count **at least 20–30 oscillations** to minimize timing error.
  * Repeat the experiment **3 times** and take the mean.

- **Sources of Error:** Diameter measurement (most critical — $r^{4}$ effect), air damping, amplitude correction, finite timing accuracy, non-uniformity of wire.

- **Real-World Uses:** Torsion spring design, seismograph calibration, aircraft cable testing, shaft design in turbines and automobiles.

- **KTU 2024 Examiner's Hot Tip:** Always state the formula, derive step-by-step in long-answer derivations, use proper SI units, and conclude with percentage error. A 14-mark derivation question will typically award 1–2 marks for the **boxed final formula** and 0.5–1 mark for **units and percentage error** — do not skip these.

- **Cross-Verification Relationship:** $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ can be used to verify the experimental $G$ value if $E$ and $\nu$ are known from other tests (e.g., tensile test, ultrasonic test).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Pendulum Test for Brass Wires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Pendulum Test for Brass Wires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Torsion Pendulum Test** is a classical experimental procedure in **Strength of Materials** used to determine the **Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus)** — denoted by the symbol **G** (or sometimes **η** or **C**) — of a thin cylindrical wire, specifically **brass wire** in the KTU syllabus context.

A torsion pendulum consists of a **cylindrical wire** of the test material (brass) rigidly clamped at its upper end to a fixed, vibration-free support, while a **heavy circular disc** (the inertia member) is rigidly attached to its lower end. When the disc is given a small angular twist about the vertical axis (the wire's longitudinal axis) and released, the restoring **torsional couple** developed in the wire due to its shear elasticity causes the disc to execute **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** in the angular domain — this oscillatory motion is called **torsional oscillation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The student is expected to determine the **rigidity modulus of the brass wire** by measuring the **time period of oscillation** for **at least two different inertia discs** and to verify the result graphically by plotting **T² versus I** (where I = moment of inertia of the disc, T = time period).

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Think of a torsion pendulum like this: imagine hanging a **heavy pizza pan** (the disc) using a single **thin metal wire** (brass) from a ceiling hook. If you gently twist the pan in the horizontal plane (say, by rotating it by 10–15 degrees using your fingers) and let go:

- The wire resists your twist like a twisted rubber band — it wants to "untwist" back.
- The pan, due to its rotational inertia, overshoots its original position, twists the wire the other way, and the cycle repeats.
- This back-and-forth rotational motion is **angular SHM**, just like how a pendulum swings back and forth in linear SHM.

The **stiffer** the wire (higher G), the **faster** it oscillates (shorter time period). The **heavier or larger** the disc (higher I), the **slower** it oscillates (longer time period). By carefully measuring these oscillations, we can mathematically extract the **hidden stiffness property** of the wire — its **rigidity modulus**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The experiment relies on the following measurable quantities, each with **SI units**:

- **Length of the wire (L)** — meters (**m**), typically **1 m** for KTU lab setups.
- **Radius of the wire (r)** — meters (**m**), measured using a **screw gauge** (typically **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** for brass).
- **Mass of the disc (M)** — kilograms (**kg**), measured using an **electronic balance**.
- **Diameter of the disc (D)** — meters (**m**), measured using a **vernier caliper**.
- **Time period (T)** — seconds (**s**), measured using a **digital stopwatch** over 20 oscillations.
- **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — Pascals (**Pa**) or **N/m²**. Standard value for brass ≈ **3.5 × 10¹⁰ Pa to 4.5 × 10¹⁰ Pa**.
- **Moment of Inertia of disc (I)** — kg·m².

## 1.4 The Torsion Equation — The Heart of the Experiment

When a wire of length **L** and radius **r** is twisted by an angle **θ**, the **restoring couple** is:

$$C_{restoring} = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \cdot \theta$$

Where **η** (eta) is the **modulus of rigidity** of the wire material (brass). The quantity:

$$C = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}$$

is called the **torsional rigidity** (or **moment of torsion**) of the wire. Notice that it is **extremely sensitive** to the wire's radius — it depends on **r⁴**. A small error in measuring **r** causes a **large error** in **η**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Establish the Restoring Couple

When a wire is fixed at one end and twisted at the other end through a small angle **θ**, the wire develops **shear strain** throughout its length. By **Hooke's Law in shear**:

$$\text{Shear Stress} = \eta \times \text{Shear Strain}$$

The shear strain on the outer surface at radius **r** of the wire is **rθ/L**. Integrating the shear stress over the entire cross-sectional area gives the total **restoring torque**:

$$\boxed{\tau = -C \theta = -\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \cdot \theta}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the couple is **opposite** to the direction of twist (restoring).

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation

For the disc of moment of inertia **I** rotating about the wire axis:

$$\tau = I \alpha = I \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$$

Substituting the restoring couple:

$$I \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\left(\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}\right) \theta$$

This is the **standard SHM differential equation** with:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}$$

### Step 3: Identify Angular Frequency and Time Period

The angular frequency of oscillation:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}}$$

The time period:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2LI}{\pi \eta r^4}}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Rigidity Modulus

Squaring both sides:

$$T^2 = \frac{8\pi L I}{\eta r^4}$$

Therefore, the **rigidity modulus** is:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}}$$

This is the **master formula** of the experiment.

### Step 5: Moment of Inertia of the Disc

For a uniform circular disc of mass **M** and diameter **D** rotating about its geometric (diameter) axis through its center:

$$\boxed{I = \frac{MD^2}{8}}$$

If the disc rotates about an axis **perpendicular to its plane through center** (more common in some setups):

$$I = \frac{MD^2}{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Most KTU torsion pendulum setups use the disc oscillating in its **own plane**, with the wire along the central perpendicular axis. Therefore, the rotation axis is **perpendicular to the disc plane**, and **I = MD²/8** is typically applied. **Always verify** with your lab manual.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table summarizes **every formula** required for solving KTU university exam problems on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Restoring couple | $\tau = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \cdot \theta$ | N·m | Hooke's law in shear |
| 2 | Torsional rigidity | $C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}$ | N·m/rad | Wire stiffness constant |
| 3 | Angular frequency | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}}$ | rad/s | SHM relation |
| 4 | Time period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2LI}{\pi \eta r^4}}$ | s | Core experimental formula |
| 5 | **Rigidity modulus** | $\eta = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}$ | Pa = N/m² | **Primary result** |
| 6 | Moment of inertia (disc, axis ⊥ to plane) | $I = \dfrac{MD^2}{8}$ | kg·m² | Standard KTU disc |
| 7 | Moment of inertia (disc, axis in plane) | $I = \dfrac{MD^2}{4}$ | kg·m² | Alternative geometry |
| 8 | Linear T² vs I graph slope | $\text{slope} = \dfrac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4}$ | s²/(kg·m²) | Graphical verification |
| 9 | Rigidity modulus from slope | $\eta = \dfrac{8\pi L}{r^4 \cdot \text{slope}}$ | Pa | Used with graph |
| 10 | Period from graph intercept | Intercept → 0 | s² | Theory confirms zero intercept |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **modulus of rigidity** is one of the most critical mechanical properties of materials and finds direct application in:

- **Design of helical springs** (automotive suspensions, watch mechanisms).
- **Torsion bars** in vehicle suspension systems and aircraft landing gear.
- **Shafts and axles** in power transmission systems — angle of twist under torque.
- **Musical instrument strings** (piano, guitar wire tuning involves shear modulus).
- **Earthquake-resistant building design** — columns undergo torsional shear during quakes.
- **Microscale MEMS devices** — micro-cantilevers and torsional mirrors.

## 2.4 Why the Graphical Method (T² vs I)?

Using the formula $\eta = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}$, we can rewrite:

$$T^2 = \left(\frac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4}\right) I$$

This is in the **y = mx** form, where **T²** is the y-variable, **I** is the x-variable, and the slope **m = 8πL / (η r⁴)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages of graphical method over single calculation:**
> 1. **Minimizes random errors** by averaging multiple data points.
> 2. **Detects systematic errors** (e.g., if the graph does not pass through origin).
> 3. **Validates the theoretical model** — linearity confirms the SHM assumption.
> 4. Reduces reliance on **stopwatch reaction-time error** in any single measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Rigidity Modulus from Torsional Oscillation

### Derivation 3.1.1 — From Shear Strain to Restoring Couple

Consider a wire of length **L** and radius **r** clamped at the upper end. The lower end is twisted by an angle **θ**.

A thin cylindrical shell of radius **x** and thickness **dx** on the cross-section experiences shear strain:

$$\gamma = \frac{x \theta}{L}$$

By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$\text{Shear Stress at radius } x: \quad \tau_s = \eta \gamma = \frac{\eta x \theta}{L}$$

Force on the elementary ring of area **dA = 2π x dx**:

$$dF = \tau_s \cdot dA = \frac{\eta x \theta}{L} \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = \frac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} x^2 \, dx$$

The torque (moment of force about the axis) on this ring is:

$$d\tau_{ring} = x \cdot dF = \frac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} x^3 \, dx$$

Integrating from **x = 0** to **x = r**:

$$\tau_{total} = \int_0^r \frac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} x^3 \, dx = \frac{2\pi \eta \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{r^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \theta}$$

### Derivation 3.1.2 — From Newton's Law to SHM Equation

For the disc (moment of inertia **I**) attached at the bottom:

$$\tau_{net} = I \alpha$$

The restoring couple opposes motion, so by Newton's second law in rotation:

$$I \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L} \theta$$

Dividing throughout by **I**:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\left(\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}\right) \theta$$

Define:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}$$

Then:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \omega^2 \theta = 0$$

This is the **canonical SHM differential equation**.

### Derivation 3.1.3 — Solution and Time Period

The general solution is:

$$\theta(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

The angular frequency:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}}$$

The time period:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2LI}{\pi \eta r^4}}$$

### Derivation 3.1.4 — Final Closed-Form Expression for η

Squaring the time-period equation:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 \cdot 2LI}{\pi \eta r^4} = \frac{8\pi L I}{\eta r^4}$$

Solving for **η**:

$$\eta = \frac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}$$

Substituting **I = MD²/8**:

$$\eta = \frac{8\pi L \cdot \frac{MD^2}{8}}{r^4 T^2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2}}$$

This is the **most-used working formula** for KTU lab calculations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style Solution

### Problem Statement

A brass wire of length **1.0 m** and radius **0.5 mm** is used in a torsion pendulum. A circular disc of mass **0.5 kg** and diameter **12 cm** is attached. The time period for 20 oscillations is measured as **24.6 seconds**. Calculate the **rigidity modulus** of brass.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Given:**
- $L = 1.0$ m
- $r = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $M = 0.5$ kg
- $D = 12$ cm $= 0.12$ m
- 20T $= 24.6$ s, so $T = 1.23$ s

**Step 1: Compute the moment of inertia**

$$I = \frac{MD^2}{8} = \frac{0.5 \times (0.12)^2}{8} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.0144}{8} = \frac{0.0072}{8}$$

$$I = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the rigidity modulus formula**

$$\eta = \frac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2}$$

Compute $r^4$:

$$r^4 = (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4$$

Compute $T^2$:

$$T^2 = (1.23)^2 = 1.5129 \text{ s}^2$$

**Step 3: Numerator evaluation**

$$\pi L M D^2 = 3.14159 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.0144 = 0.022619 \text{ (kg·m³)}$$

**Step 4: Denominator evaluation**

$$r^4 T^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.5129 = 9.456 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4 \cdot \text{s}^2$$

**Step 5: Final calculation**

$$\eta = \frac{0.022619}{9.456 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.392 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 2.39 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 23.9 \text{ GPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with standard value:** The accepted value of rigidity modulus for brass is **3.5–4.5 × 10¹⁰ Pa (35–45 GPa)**. Our calculated value is somewhat higher, indicating a possible systematic error (e.g., over-estimation of I, under-estimation of T, or non-ideal wire clamping). Students should always compare with standard reference and compute **percentage error**.

### Percentage Error Calculation

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \eta_{exp} - \eta_{std} \vert}{\eta_{std}} \times 100$$

Using $\eta_{std} = 4.0 \times 10^{10}$ Pa:

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 2.39 \times 10^{11} - 4.0 \times 10^{10} \vert}{4.0 \times 10^{10}} \times 100 \approx 497\%$$

This indicates the experimental value is highly off, signaling an **error in measurement or formula selection**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Errors on η

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because **η ∝ 1/r⁴**, the radius measurement is the **most critical** parameter. The table below shows how a small percentage error in each measured quantity propagates to **η**.

| Measured Quantity | Relation to η | % Error in η per 1% Error in Quantity | Sensitivity Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radius **r** | $\eta \propto 1/r^4$ | **4%** | **1 (highest)** |
| Time period **T** | $\eta \propto 1/T^2$ | **2%** | **2** |
| Length **L** | $\eta \propto L$ | **1%** | **3** |
| Disc diameter **D** | $\eta \propto D^2$ | **2%** | **2 (tied)** |
| Disc mass **M** | $\eta \propto M$ | **1%** | **3 (tied)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always measure the wire radius **multiple times** along its length and **at different orientations** using the screw gauge. **Reject anomalous readings** and use the **mean**. Even a **2% error in r** leads to **8% error in η**!

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Rigidity Modulus from Graph (T² vs I)

For multiple discs of different masses/diameters, plot **T² (y-axis)** versus **I (x-axis)**.

The relation is:

$$T^2 = \frac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4} \cdot I$$

- **Slope** of best-fit line: $m = \dfrac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4}$
- **Intercept** of best-fit line: theoretically **zero**.

Solving for **η** from the slope:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{8\pi L}{r^4 \cdot m}}$$

The **intercept** being close to **zero** confirms that the **empty wire (without disc) has no oscillation period**, which is physically consistent.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of the torsion pendulum apparatus, identifying the role of each component in the experimental measurement chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ceiling Mount Rigid Support] --> nodeB[Brass Wire Clamp Top]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Brass Wire Length L Radius r]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Brass Wire Clamp Bottom]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Inertia Disc Mounted Hub]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Circular Inertia Disc Mass M Diameter D]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Angular Pointer Indicator]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Angular Scale Reference Mark]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Digital Stopwatch External]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Stopwatch Activation on Release]
    nodeJ --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeK[Time for N Oscillations Recorded]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Time Period T Computed]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Vernier Caliper Diameter D]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Electronic Balance Mass M]
    nodeC --> nodeO[Screw Gauge Radius r]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Master Formula eta equals 8 pi L I by r4 T2]
    nodeL --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Final Result Rigidity Modulus of Brass]
```

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure — Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Measure Wire Diameter at 5 Positions Using Screw Gauge] --> stepB[Step 2 Measure Wire Length L Between Clamps]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Measure Disc Mass M on Electronic Balance]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Measure Disc Diameter D Using Vernier Caliper]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Mount Disc on Brass Wire Hub]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Twist Disc by 30 to 45 Degrees Carefully]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Release Disc Gently Ensure No Lateral Swing]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Start Stopwatch at Zero Crossing]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9 Count 20 Full Oscillations Stop Stopwatch]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10 Record Total Time t]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 11 Calculate Period T equals t by 20]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 12 Compute Moment of I equals MD2 by 8]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 13 Apply Master Formula eta equals 8 pi L I by r4 T2]
    stepM --> stepN[Step 14 Repeat with Different Discs for Graphical Method]
    stepN --> stepO[Step 15 Plot T squared vs I Graph Determine Slope]
    stepO --> stepP[Step 16 Compute Rigidity Modulus from Slope]
    stepP --> stepQ[Step 17 Calculate Percentage Error vs Standard Value]
```

## 4.3 Torsional Oscillation — Conceptual Energy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    energyA[Disc Rotated by theta Maximum Angular Displacement] --> energyB[All Energy is Potential Energy in Torsion]
    energyB --> energyC[Wire Restoring Couple Acts on Disc]
    energyC --> energyD[Disc Accelerates Towards Equilibrium theta equals zero]
    energyD --> energyE[Maximum Angular Velocity All Kinetic Energy]
    energyE --> energyF[Disc Overshoots Due to Inertia Twists Wire Other Way]
    energyF --> energyG[Kinetic Converts Back to Potential]
    energyG --> energyH[One Complete Cycle Complete Time T]
    energyH --> energyA
```

## 4.4 Component Summary Table

| Component | Function | Measurement Instrument | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brass Wire | Specimen under test | Screw gauge (diameter) | 0.5–1.5 mm diameter |
| Top Clamp | Rigid support, vibration isolation | Visual inspection | Firm grip essential |
| Bottom Clamp/Hub | Transfers wire twist to disc | Visual inspection | Aligned with wire axis |
| Inertia Disc | Provides rotational inertia | Vernier caliper + Balance | 100–500 g, 10–15 cm |
| Angular Pointer | Marks angular position | Visual reference | Aligned with scale |
| Stopwatch | Time measurement | Digital stopwatch | ±0.01 s least count |
| Screw Gauge | Wire diameter | Screw gauge | ±0.01 mm least count |
| Vernier Caliper | Disc diameter | Vernier caliper | ±0.01 cm least count |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the modulus of rigidity and explain its significance in the torsion pendulum experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **modulus of rigidity** (also called **shear modulus**), denoted by **η** or **G**, is defined as the ratio of **shear stress** to **shear strain** within the elastic limit of a material:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Shear Stress}}{\text{Shear Strain}}$$

Its SI unit is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**.

In the torsion pendulum experiment, the modulus of rigidity of the **brass wire** is the unknown quantity to be determined. When the wire is twisted, it develops shear strain, and the resulting shear stress produces a restoring couple that drives the disc in **simple harmonic motion**. By measuring the time period of this oscillation, we can compute **η** using the relation:

$$\eta = \frac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}$$

**Significance:** The rigidity modulus is essential in designing components subjected to **torsional loads** — such as drive shafts, axles, helical springs, and torsion bars — where angular deformation must remain within safe limits.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Why is a thin wire preferred for the torsion pendulum experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thin wire** is preferred for the following reasons:

1. **Large measurable time period:** A thin wire has **low torsional rigidity** ($C = \pi \eta r^4 / 2L$ depends on **r⁴**). With a smaller $r$, the time period **T** becomes large enough to be measured accurately with a stopwatch.

2. **Easier to twist:** A thin wire can be twisted by a small angle manually (e.g., 30–45°) without requiring large force.

3. **Larger amplitude of oscillation:** Lower stiffness means the disc oscillates with a noticeable angular amplitude, making it easier to count oscillations visually.

4. **Homogeneous deformation:** In a thin wire, the shear strain is more uniform along the cross-section, and **Hooke's Law** is well-satisfied within the small deformation regime.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **time period of oscillation** of a torsion pendulum and hence obtain the formula for the **rigidity modulus of the wire material**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a torsion pendulum experiment, a brass wire of length **1.2 m** and diameter **0.8 mm** is used with a disc of mass **0.4 kg** and diameter **10 cm**. If the time period for 20 oscillations is **28.0 seconds**, calculate the rigidity modulus and the percentage error (standard value of η for brass = 4.0 × 10¹⁰ N/m²). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Derivation:**

Consider a wire of length **L**, radius **r**, fixed at the upper end. A disc of moment of inertia **I** is attached at the lower end.

When the disc is rotated through a small angle **θ**, the wire develops a **restoring couple**:

$$\tau = -C\theta = -\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}\theta$$

The equation of motion for the disc is:

$$I \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \left(\frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}\right)\theta = 0$$

This is the SHM equation with:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{\pi \eta r^4}{2LI}$$

Therefore, the **time period** is:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{2LI}{\pi \eta r^4}}$$

Squaring and solving for **η**:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}}$$

For a uniform disc of mass **M** and diameter **D** (axis perpendicular to disc plane):

$$I = \frac{MD^2}{8}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Given:**
- $L = 1.2$ m
- Wire diameter $= 0.8$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $M = 0.4$ kg
- $D = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m
- 20T $= 28.0$ s $\Rightarrow T = 1.4$ s
- $\eta_{std} = 4.0 \times 10^{10}$ N/m²

**Step 1: Moment of inertia**

$$I = \frac{MD^2}{8} = \frac{0.4 \times (0.10)^2}{8} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.01}{8} = \frac{0.004}{8} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute $r^4$**

$$r^4 = (4 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 256 \times 10^{-16} = 2.56 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3: Compute $T^2$**

$$T^2 = (1.4)^2 = 1.96 \text{ s}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator $\pi L M D^2$**

$$\pi L M D^2 = 3.14159 \times 1.2 \times 0.4 \times 0.01 = 0.015079 \text{ kg·m}^3$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator $r^4 T^2$**

$$r^4 T^2 = 2.56 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.96 = 5.0176 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 6: Final calculation**

$$\eta = \frac{0.015079}{5.0176 \times 10^{-14}} = 3.005 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{exp} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The experimental value is **higher** than the standard, indicating possible sources of error.

**Step 7: Percentage error**

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \eta_{exp} - \eta_{std} \vert}{\eta_{std}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 3.0 \times 10^{11} - 4.0 \times 10^{10} \vert}{4.0 \times 10^{10}} \times 100 = \frac{2.6 \times 10^{11}}{4.0 \times 10^{10}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{ Error} \approx 650\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Using **D** (diameter) instead of **R = D/2** in the formula. Always check whether your formula needs **r** or **R**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm with cm and m causes catastrophic errors. **Convert everything to SI units (m, kg, s) FIRST** before any calculation.
> 3. **Squaring the radius:** Students often write **r²** instead of **r⁴** — this is the most frequent formula error and leads to a value **orders of magnitude** off.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Always remember that $\eta$ must be **positive**; if you get a negative value, check your formula application.
> 5. **Plot confusion:** When drawing T² vs I, students sometimes plot T vs I, leading to wrong slope interpretation.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labeled diagram**, describe the **experimental setup** and procedure to determine the rigidity modulus of a brass wire using a torsion pendulum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **graphical method** of determining the rigidity modulus. In an experiment, the following observations were recorded for two different discs. Calculate η using the slope method and compare with the calculated value.

| Disc | Mass M (kg) | Diameter D (cm) | Time for 20 osc t (s) |
|------|-------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0.30 | 8.0 | 22.4 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 12.0 | 30.0 |

Given: $L = 1.0$ m, wire diameter = 0.6 mm.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Torsion pendulum stand with rigid clamp
2. Brass wire (specimen)
3. Two inertia discs of different masses/diameters
4. Vernier calipers
5. Screw gauge
6. Electronic balance
7. Digital stopwatch
8. Metre scale

**Setup Description:**
- The brass wire is clamped at the top to a **rigid ceiling-mounted support** using a firm grip to prevent any vertical or lateral motion.
- The wire hangs vertically with a length **L** (typically 1 m) between the upper clamp and the lower disc-holding chuck.
- A **circular metal disc** (brass or aluminum) is attached to the lower end via a chuck that ensures the disc's center lies exactly on the **wire's longitudinal axis**.
- An **angular pointer** is fixed to the disc, and a **reference scale** is positioned behind it to measure angular displacement.
- The disc must be able to rotate **freely in the horizontal plane** without wobbling.

**Procedure:**
1. **Measure wire diameter** at 5 different positions along its length using a screw gauge. Calculate the **mean radius r**.
2. **Measure wire length L** between the two clamps using a metre scale.
3. **Measure disc mass M** on an electronic balance and **diameter D** using a vernier caliper (at 3 different orientations).
4. **Mount the disc** carefully and ensure no pre-tension in the wire.
5. **Twist the disc** gently by 30°–45° and release it without imparting any lateral push.
6. Using a stopwatch, **measure the time for 20 complete oscillations** (t₂₀).
7. Calculate the **time period** $T = t_{20}/20$.
8. **Compute** $\eta = \dfrac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2}$.
9. **Repeat** the experiment with a second disc of different mass/diameter.
10. **Plot** T² vs I graph and determine η from the slope for verification.

**Precautions:**
- The top clamp must be **rigidly fixed** to avoid spurious oscillations.
- The disc should oscillate in its own plane — **no lateral or vertical motion**.
- Count oscillations from a clearly defined reference point.
- Take **multiple readings** and use the mean.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Graphical Method Explanation:**

The relation $T^2 = \dfrac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4} I$ is of the form **y = mx**, where:
- $y = T^2$
- $x = I$
- Slope $m = \dfrac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4}$
- Intercept = 0 (theoretically)

By plotting T² vs I and finding the slope, we compute:

$$\eta = \frac{8\pi L}{r^4 \cdot m}$$

**Calculation from given data:**

**Wire:** $L = 1.0$ m, diameter = 0.6 mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m

**Disc 1:** $M = 0.30$ kg, $D = 8.0$ cm $= 0.08$ m, $T_1 = 22.4/20 = 1.12$ s

$$I_1 = \frac{M D^2}{8} = \frac{0.30 \times (0.08)^2}{8} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.0064}{8} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$T_1^2 = (1.12)^2 = 1.2544 \text{ s}^2$$

**Disc 2:** $M = 0.50$ kg, $D = 12.0$ cm $= 0.12$ m, $T_2 = 30.0/20 = 1.50$ s

$$I_2 = \frac{M D^2}{8} = \frac{0.50 \times (0.12)^2}{8} = \frac{0.50 \times 0.0144}{8} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$T_2^2 = (1.50)^2 = 2.25 \text{ s}^2$$

**Slope of T² vs I:**

$$m = \frac{T_2^2 - T_1^2}{I_2 - I_1} = \frac{2.25 - 1.2544}{9.0 \times 10^{-4} - 2.4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.9956}{6.6 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$m = 1508.5 \text{ s}^2 / (\text{kg·m}^2)$$

**Compute $r^4$:**

$$r^4 = (3 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 81 \times 10^{-16} = 8.1 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^4$$

**Rigidity Modulus from slope:**

$$\eta = \frac{8\pi L}{r^4 \cdot m} = \frac{8 \times 3.14159 \times 1.0}{8.1 \times 10^{-15} \times 1508.5}$$

$$\eta = \frac{25.133}{1.222 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.057 \times 10^{12} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 2.06 \times 10^{12} \text{ N/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is significantly higher than the standard value of brass (≈ 4 × 10¹⁰ N/m²). The discrepancy suggests **measurement errors** — most likely in the wire diameter measurement, since **η ∝ 1/r⁴** amplifies any radius error drastically.

**Direct calculation for comparison (Disc 1):**

$$\eta_1 = \frac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2} = \frac{3.14159 \times 1.0 \times 0.30 \times 0.0064}{8.1 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.2544}$$

$$\eta_1 = \frac{0.006032}{1.016 \times 10^{-14}} = 5.94 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Direct calculation (Disc 2):**

$$\eta_2 = \frac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2} = \frac{3.14159 \times 1.0 \times 0.50 \times 0.0144}{8.1 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.25}$$

$$\eta_2 = \frac{0.022619}{1.823 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.241 \times 10^{12} \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Discussion:** Both direct methods and the graphical method yield values **larger than the standard**, indicating the **wire radius is overestimated** in the measurement. To improve accuracy, the screw gauge should be used with greater care and multiple readings averaged.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- **Master Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{8\pi L I}{r^4 T^2}$ where $I = \dfrac{MD^2}{8}$ for a disc.

- **Working formula for KTU lab:** $\eta = \dfrac{\pi L M D^2}{r^4 T^2}$.

- **Physical meaning:** Modulus of rigidity = ratio of shear stress to shear strain within elastic limit.

- **Torsional rigidity of wire:** $C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^4}{2L}$ — depends on **r⁴**.

- **Sensitivity ranking:** Radius (×4) > Time period (×2) = Diameter (×2) > Length (×1) = Mass (×1).

- **Most critical measurement:** **Wire radius r** — use screw gauge at 5+ positions.

- **Graphical method:** Plot T² (y-axis) vs I (x-axis); slope = $\dfrac{8\pi L}{\eta r^4}$.

- **Time period measurement:** Count **at least 20 oscillations** and divide by 20 for accuracy.

- **Standard value of η for brass:** Approximately **3.5 × 10¹⁰ to 4.5 × 10¹⁰ N/m²**.

- **Precautions:** (1) Rigid clamping of top end. (2) Disc must oscillate **only in its own plane** — no vertical or lateral motion. (3) Avoid parallax in stopwatch reading. (4) Maintain small amplitude (< 45°) for SHM validity.

- **Sources of error:** (1) Non-uniform wire diameter. (2) Reaction-time error in stopwatch. (3) Disc not centered on wire axis. (4) Clamp not perfectly rigid. (5) Air currents causing damping.

- **Linear SHM assumption:** Valid only for **small angular amplitudes** (< 10° for high accuracy, < 45° acceptable).

- **Physical analogy:** Torsion pendulum is the **rotational analog** of a linear simple pendulum.

- **Damping:** Real pendulums experience **air damping**, slightly increasing the time period compared to the undamped theoretical value. This is a source of systematic error.

- **Application areas:** Spring design, drive shafts, torsional vibration analysis, MEMS devices, musical instrument strings, earthquake engineering.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Tension Test on Mild Steel (MS) Rod

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> The **Tension Test on a Mild Steel (MS) Rod** is a standardized destructive mechanical test conducted on a standardized cylindrical or flat test specimen subjected to a gradually increasing uniaxial tensile load until failure. The test is performed on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** to determine fundamental mechanical properties of mild steel including **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**, **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$)**, **Breaking Strength ($\sigma_b$)**, **Percentage Elongation ($\% \in$)**, and **Percentage Reduction in Area ($\% \psi$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a chewing gum strip from both ends. Initially, the gum stretches easily. As you increase the pull, it resists more, then it becomes very hard to stretch further, and finally, it snaps at the thinnest point. A **mild steel rod behaves very similarly**, but with four distinct stages instead of two:

1. **Easy stretch** → Elastic region (the rod returns to original size if released).
2. **Sudden easy stretch without extra force** → Yielding (material "gives up" temporarily).
3. **Hard but possible stretch until necking** → Plastic/strain hardening region.
4. **Snap!** → Fracture at the necked region.

By recording the load applied and the corresponding elongation at every instant, engineers build the famous **Stress–Strain Curve** of mild steel — a "fingerprint" of the material used worldwide in design of bridges, buildings, pressure vessels, and machinery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mild Steel?**
> Mild steel (low-carbon steel with carbon content $\approx 0.15\% - 0.30\%$) is chosen as a standard because it exhibits **clearly distinct yield point and plastic deformation** — making it the canonical example of a **ductile material** taught across all engineering disciplines.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Standard specimen:** Circular cross-section (per **IS 1608 / ASTM E8**).
- **Gauge length ($L_0$):** $5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ (proportional specimen) where $A_0$ is original cross-sectional area.
- **Loading rate:** Slow, quasi-static ($\approx 1$–$10 \text{ MPa/s}$).
- **Standard temperature:** $27 \pm 10 \text{ °C}$ (ambient).
- **Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

## 2. Specimen, Apparatus & Preparation (Prelab Context)

### 2.1 Standard Tensile Specimen (IS 1608)

A typical MS round specimen consists of:

- **Grip ends (shoulders):** Threaded or shouldered for UTM jaws.
- **Gauge length ($L_0$):** Central parallel portion over which elongation is measured.
- **Reduced parallel section:** Ensures failure occurs here.

For a rod of nominal diameter $d_0 = 20 \text{ mm}$:
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### 2.2 Apparatus Required

| S.No | Apparatus | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** | Applies tensile load and measures force |
| 2 | **Extensometer** | Measures small elongation in elastic region |
| 3 | **Vernier caliper (L.C. 0.02 mm)** | Measures diameter before and after test |
| 4 | **Dividers & ruler** | Measures final gauge length after fracture |
| 5 | **Centre punch / marking tool** | Marks gauge length on specimen |

> [!NOTE]
> A **UTM** is called "universal" because it can perform tension, compression, bending, and shear tests by changing fixtures. For tension, the maximum rated load is typically $400 \text{ kN}$, $600 \text{ kN}$, or $1000 \text{ kN}$ in KTU-affiliated college labs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Theoretical Framework of the Tension Test

The tension test is governed by **uniaxial stress–strain relationships** derived from classical mechanics of materials. The core assumption is that, during loading, the stress state on any cross-section of the gauge length is uniform and uniaxial.

### 1.1 Engineering Stress and Engineering Strain

For a rod under axial load $P$ with original cross-sectional area $A_0$ and original gauge length $L_0$:

$$\sigma_e = \frac{P}{A_0} \quad (\text{Engineering Stress, in MPa or N/mm}^2)$$

$$\epsilon_e = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \quad (\text{Engineering Strain, dimensionless})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Engineering" stress and not "True" stress?**
> Engineering stress uses the **original** area $A_0$ throughout. It assumes the cross-section does not change — which is wrong after necking. **True stress** uses instantaneous area $A_i$, and true strain uses incremental elongation. For KTU exams, **engineering stress–strain values suffice** unless explicitly asked.

### 1.2 Hooke's Law (Linear Elastic Region)

In the initial linear portion of the curve:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

where $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity. For mild steel, $E \approx 200 - 210 \text{ GPa}$.

### 1.3 Distinctive Phases of Mild Steel Stress–Strain Curve

A typical mild steel stress–strain diagram shows the following labeled points:

| Point | Name | Characteristic |
|-------|------|----------------|
| **A** | Proportional Limit | Stress is proportional to strain (Hooke's law strictly valid) |
| **B** | Elastic Limit | Maximum stress the material can withstand without permanent deformation |
| **C** | **Upper Yield Point** ($\sigma_{uy}$) | Stress at which noticeable plastic deformation begins |
| **D** | **Lower Yield Point** ($\sigma_{ly}$) | Stress at which deformation continues at nearly constant load |
| **E** | Strain Hardening Region | Material gains strength due to dislocation pile-up |
| **F** | **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** | Maximum engineering stress the material can sustain |
| **G** | Necking Region | Local cross-sectional reduction begins |
| **H** | **Fracture / Breaking Point** | Final rupture of the specimen |

> [!NOTE]
> The **yield plateau** (region between C and D) is the signature feature of low-carbon steels. It corresponds to **Lüders band propagation** along the gauge length, visible as surface markings called **Lüders lines** during the test.

### 1.4 Ductility Metrics (Post-Fracture Measurements)

After fracture, two key ductility parameters are computed:

$$\% \text{ Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction in Area} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

where $L_f$ = final gauge length (measured by carefully fitting the two broken halves end-to-end) and $A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$ = final cross-sectional area at the fracture neck.

## 2. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are 80% of the exam marks.**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | SI / KTU Unit |
|------|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Original cross-sectional area | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 2 | Engineering stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2 = \text{MPa}$ |
| 3 | Engineering strain | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless (or %) |
| 4 | Young's modulus (from linear region) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 5 | Yield stress | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{P_y}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 6 | Ultimate tensile stress (UTS) | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 7 | Breaking stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_f}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 8 | Percentage elongation | $\% \in = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Percentage reduction in area | $\% \psi = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Resilience modulus (area under elastic portion) | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | $\text{MJ/m}^3$ |
| 11 | Toughness modulus (total area under stress–strain curve) | $U_t = \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \, d\epsilon$ | $\text{MJ/m}^3$ |
| 12 | Proportional gauge length (IS 1608) | $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ | $\text{mm}$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yield stress** → Used to determine **factor of safety** in structural design (e.g., allowable stress in steel beams, columns).
- **UTS** → Used in **cable, wire rope, and bolt design** where plastic deformation is acceptable but failure is not.
- **% Elongation** → Indicates **formability** — critical for sheet metal, forging, and rolling operations.
- **% Reduction in area** → Indicates **toughness and ductility** — critical in pressure vessel and earthquake-resistant design.

# Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Numerical Solution

## 1. Standard Test Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Order)

The following step-by-step procedure is what KTU examiners expect in the **"Procedure"** section of your lab record:

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation**
- Machine a mild steel rod to a standard cylindrical shape (shoulder + reduced parallel + threaded ends).
- Measure the **original diameter $d_0$** at three locations along the gauge length using a vernier caliper. Average the readings to reduce error.
- Mark a precise **gauge length $L_0$** (e.g., 50 mm or 80 mm) using a centre punch and a steel scale.

**Step 2 — Initial Measurements**
- Record initial gauge length $L_0$ (mm), initial diameter $d_0$ (mm).
- Compute $A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$.

**Step 3 — Mounting the Specimen**
- Fix the specimen between the upper and lower grips of the UTM. Align the axis of the specimen with the loading axis to avoid bending.
- Attach an **extensometer** to the gauge length (if required for modulus measurement).

**Step 4 — Pre-Loading Check**
- Apply a small pre-load to remove slack and ensure the specimen is seated properly. Set dial gauges / digital readouts to zero.

**Step 5 — Apply Load Incrementally**
- Increase the load **gradually and continuously** at a slow rate.
- Record load $P$ and corresponding elongation $\Delta L$ at regular intervals (typically every 2 kN or 5 kN).
- Note the load at which the **yield point** is observed (sudden elongation dial jump / visible Lüders bands).
- Continue loading through strain hardening until **maximum load** is reached.
- Note $P_{max}$ (peak load on the dial gauge).
- Continue until the specimen **fractures** at the neck.

**Step 6 — Post-Fracture Measurements**
- Carefully remove the broken halves.
- Measure **final gauge length $L_f$** by fitting both pieces end-to-end along the original axis.
- Measure **final diameter $d_f$ at the neck** (smallest diameter at fracture).

**Step 7 — Plot and Compute**
- Plot **Load vs. Elongation** curve.
- Convert to **Stress–Strain curve** using formulas above.
- Compute all mechanical properties from peak/final values.

## 2. Exhaustive Sample Calculation (Canonical KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> The following numerical is the **standard template** used in KTU labs and model papers. Memorize its structure.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Observation)

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Initial diameter, $d_0$ | $20.00 \text{ mm}$ |
| Initial gauge length, $L_0$ | $50.00 \text{ mm}$ |
| Load at upper yield point, $P_{uy}$ | $78.50 \text{ kN}$ |
| Load at lower yield point, $P_{ly}$ | $73.30 \text{ kN}$ |
| Maximum load (at necking), $P_{max}$ | $117.75 \text{ kN}$ |
| Load at fracture, $P_f$ | $94.20 \text{ kN}$ |
| Final gauge length, $L_f$ | $67.50 \text{ mm}$ |
| Final diameter at neck, $d_f$ | $14.20 \text{ mm}$ |
| Elongation at proportional limit, $\Delta L$ | $0.05 \text{ mm}$ |
| Corresponding load at proportional limit, $P_{prop}$ | $25.20 \text{ kN}$ |

### Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (20.00)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi \approx 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Final Cross-Sectional Area at Neck

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (14.20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 201.64}{4} = 50.41\pi \approx 158.366 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Young's Modulus (E)

The Young's Modulus is determined from the linear (proportional) region:

$$\sigma_{prop} = \frac{P_{prop}}{A_0} = \frac{25200 \text{ N}}{314.159 \text{ mm}^2} \approx 80.21 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\epsilon_{prop} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.05}{50.00} = 0.001$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma_{prop}}{\epsilon_{prop}} = \frac{80.21}{0.001} = 80210 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 80.21 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For mild steel the **expected value** of E is $\approx 200 \text{ GPa}$. The lower value in this sample simply reflects the chosen $\Delta L$. In a real KTU exam, if your computed $E$ is far from $200 \text{ GPa}$, recheck the data and conversion units.

### Step 4 — Upper and Lower Yield Stress

$$\sigma_{uy} = \frac{P_{uy}}{A_0} = \frac{78500}{314.159} \approx 249.86 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 249.86 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{ly} = \frac{P_{ly}}{A_0} = \frac{73300}{314.159} \approx 233.30 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)

$$\sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{117750}{314.159} \approx 374.81 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 6 — Breaking Stress

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P_f}{A_0} = \frac{94200}{314.159} \approx 299.85 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 7 — Percentage Elongation

$$\% \in = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{67.50 - 50.00}{50.00} \times 100 = \frac{17.50}{50.00} \times 100 = 35.00\%$$

### Step 8 — Percentage Reduction in Area

$$\% \psi = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.159 - 158.366}{314.159} \times 100$$

$$\% \psi = \frac{155.793}{314.159} \times 100 \approx 49.59\%$$

### Step 9 — Resilience Modulus

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(233.30)^2}{2 \times 80210} = \frac{54428.89}{160420} \approx 0.3393 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.3393 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

### Step 10 — Tabulated Result

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | $\approx 80.21$ | GPa |
| Upper Yield Stress | $\sigma_{uy}$ | $249.86$ | MPa |
| Lower Yield Stress | $\sigma_{ly}$ | $233.30$ | MPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | $374.81$ | MPa |
| Breaking Stress | $\sigma_b$ | $299.85$ | MPa |
| Percentage Elongation | $\% \in$ | $35.00$ | % |
| Percentage Reduction in Area | $\% \psi$ | $49.59$ | % |
| Resilience Modulus | $U_r$ | $0.339$ | MJ/m³ |

## 3. Observation Table Format (For Lab Record)

| S.No | Load $P$ (kN) | Elongation $\Delta L$ (mm) | Stress $\sigma$ (MPa) | Strain $\epsilon$ |
|------|---------------|----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000000 |
| 2 | 10 | 0.020 | 31.83 | 0.000400 |
| 3 | 20 | 0.040 | 63.66 | 0.000800 |
| 4 | 25.2 | 0.050 | 80.21 | 0.001000 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| n | 78.50 (yield) | 0.20 | 249.86 | 0.004000 |
| n+1 | 117.75 (max) | — | 374.81 | — |
| final | 94.20 (fracture) | — | 299.85 | $L_f = 67.50 \text{ mm}$ |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Standard Tensile Test Specimen Geometry (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threaded Upper Grip End] --> B[Shoulder Fillet]
    B --> C[Reduced Parallel Section - Gauge Length L0]
    C --> D[Shoulder Fillet]
    D --> E[Threaded Lower Grip End]
    F[Centre Punch Marks at L0 = 5.65 sqrt A0] -.-> C
    G[Extensometer Clamp Points] -.-> C
    H[Final Fracture Neck - Measured d_f] -.-> C
```

## 2. UTM Functional Architecture & Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[Power Supply and Motor] --> HG[Hydraulic Pump]
    HG --> CV[Control Valves]
    CV --> PISTON[Hydraulic Piston in Cylinder]
    PISTON --> MG[Movable Crosshead]
    MG --> LG[Lower Grip Assembly]
    SPEC[Mild Steel Specimen] --> LG
    SPEC --> UG[Upper Grip Assembly]
    UG --> LC[Load Cell - Measures P]
    LC --> DIAL[Dial Gauge / Digital Indicator]
    EX[Extensometer] --> SPEC
    EX --> EXD[Elongation Indicator]
    DIAL --> READ[Recorded Load Data P kN]
    EXD --> READ2[Recorded Elongation dL mm]
    READ --> PLOT[Plot Load vs Elongation]
    READ2 --> PLOT
    PLOT --> CONV[Convert to Stress vs Strain]
    CONV --> ANAL[Compute E sigma_y UTS and ductility]
```

## 3. Stress Strain Curve of Mild Steel (Annotated Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Origin] -->|Linear Elastic| P1[Point A - Proportional Limit]
    P1 --> P2[Point B - Elastic Limit]
    P2 --> P3[Point C - Upper Yield Point sigma_uy]
    P3 --> P4[Point D - Lower Yield Point sigma_ly]
    P4 --> P5[Point E - Strain Hardening Region]
    P5 --> P6[Point F - UTS sigma_u - Peak Load]
    P6 --> P7[Point G - Necking Begins]
    P7 --> P8[Point H - Fracture sigma_b]
    P8 --> P9[Rod Ruptures into Two Pieces]
    P1 -.Hooke Region.-> P3
    P3 -.Yield Plateau.-> P4
    P4 -.Plastic Deformation.-> P6
    P6 -.Localized Necking.-> P8
```

## 4. Fracture Surface Classification (Ductile vs Brittle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FRAC[MS Rod Fracture Cross Section] --> CUP[Cup and Cone Fracture - Ductile]
    FRAC --> BR[Brittle Fracture - Granular]
    CUP --> NECK[Central Flat Region - Crack Initiation]
    CUP --> SHEAR[Outer Shear Lip - 45 deg slip]
    BR --> CLEAV[Crystalline Cleavage Planes]
    style CUP fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#1f7a1f
    style BR fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a83232
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mild steel typically exhibits a **cup-and-cone fracture**, which is the classic signature of ductile failure. This is a viva favorite — be ready to describe why the cup-and-cone forms (internal void coalescence in the center, followed by 45° shear lip formation at the periphery).

## 5. Logical Flow of the Tension Test Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 - Measure d0 and L0] --> S2[Step 2 - Compute A0]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Mount Specimen in UTM]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - Apply Load Incrementally]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 - Record P and dL]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 - Identify Yield Load]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 - Identify Maximum Load]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 - Continue to Fracture]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 - Measure Lf and df]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 - Compute Properties]
    S10 --> R1[Yield Stress sigma_y]
    S10 --> R2[UTS sigma_u]
    S10 --> R3[Breaking Stress sigma_b]
    S10 --> R4[Percent Elongation]
    S10 --> R5[Percent Reduction in Area]
    S10 --> R6[Young Modulus E]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **Engineering Stress** and **True Stress**. Why is true stress always greater than engineering stress beyond the ultimate point?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Engineering Stress | True Stress |
|--------|--------------------|-------------|
| Definition | $\sigma_e = P / A_0$ | $\sigma_t = P / A_i$ |
| Area used | Original area $A_0$ | Instantaneous area $A_i$ |
| Curve shape | Reaches peak and then falls | Continues to rise until fracture |
| Use | Design and tabulated data | Detailed metal forming analysis |

**Why True > Engineering beyond UTS:** Beyond the ultimate point, the specimen undergoes **necking** and the actual cross-sectional area $A_i$ continuously decreases. Since the denominator shrinks while the numerator $P$ does not fall as fast, the **true stress increases monotonically** up to fracture, whereas engineering stress appears to fall.

(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation)

---

### Q2. Explain the significance of the **yield point** in mild steel and why it is used as the design criterion for ductile materials.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The yield point marks the stress at which the material begins to undergo **significant permanent (plastic) deformation** without any increase in load. For mild steel, this is visible as a **horizontal plateau (C–D region)** in the stress–strain curve.

**Significance:**
1. It represents the **maximum safe stress** a ductile material can sustain in service without permanent deformation.
2. It is used as the **basis for allowable stress design** (e.g., $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$).
3. Beyond this point, structures would suffer **visible sagging, creep, or misalignment** — unacceptable in civil engineering.
4. Codes like **IS 800 (General Construction in Steel)** and **IS 456** use $\sigma_y$ as the reference strength for limit-state design.

(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark plateau explanation, 1 mark design relevance)

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern, Module Experiment 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **standard tensile test specimen** as per IS 1608. List the apparatus required and the procedure to be followed during the test. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A mild steel specimen of diameter $25 \text{ mm}$ and gauge length $62.5 \text{ mm}$ is tested in a UTM. The following observations were recorded:

- Load at proportional limit: $P_p = 31.5 \text{ kN}$ with elongation $\Delta L_p = 0.031 \text{ mm}$
- Load at upper yield: $P_{uy} = 122.5 \text{ kN}$
- Load at lower yield: $P_{ly} = 115.6 \text{ kN}$
- Maximum load: $P_{max} = 184.0 \text{ kN}$
- Load at fracture: $P_f = 147.2 \text{ kN}$
- Final gauge length: $L_f = 84.5 \text{ mm}$
- Final diameter at neck: $d_f = 17.5 \text{ mm}$

Calculate: (i) Young's Modulus, (ii) Upper and Lower Yield Stress, (iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iv) Breaking Stress, (v) Percentage Elongation, (vi) Percentage Reduction in Area. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Specimen, Apparatus & Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Specimen Sketch (as per IS 1608):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. The specimen has:

- **Threaded end** for UTM grip (length $\approx 30 \text{ mm}$ each)
- **Shoulder radius** for smooth load transfer
- **Reduced parallel gauge section** of length $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ (here, for $d_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 5.65 \times 25 / 2 \approx 70 \text{ mm}$)
- Total specimen length typically $L_{total} \approx 200$–$300 \text{ mm}$

**Apparatus:**
1. UTM (400 kN/600 kN capacity)
2. Extensometer
3. Vernier caliper
4. Steel scale and divider
5. Centre punch

**Procedure (concise 7-point version):**
1. Measure $d_0$ and $L_0$ using vernier caliper and steel scale.
2. Mount specimen in UTM grips and align axially.
3. Apply small pre-load; zero the dial gauges.
4. Increase load gradually and record $P$ vs. $\Delta L$.
5. Note the load at which yield plateau occurs.
6. Continue loading through strain hardening to maximum load.
7. Note $P_{max}$, continue to fracture, then measure $L_f$ and $d_f$ on broken halves.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Initial Area**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (25)^2}{4} = 490.874 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Final Area**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (17.5)^2}{4} = 240.528 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Young's Modulus**

$$\sigma_p = \frac{P_p}{A_0} = \frac{31500}{490.874} = 64.17 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\epsilon_p = \frac{\Delta L_p}{L_0} = \frac{0.031}{62.5} = 4.96 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E = \frac{64.17}{4.96 \times 10^{-4}} = 129375 \text{ MPa} \approx 129.4 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Upper Yield Stress**

$$\sigma_{uy} = \frac{122500}{490.874} = 249.55 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Lower Yield Stress**

$$\sigma_{ly} = \frac{115600}{490.874} = 235.50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Ultimate Tensile Strength**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{184000}{490.874} = 374.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Breaking Stress**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{147200}{490.874} = 299.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8 — Percentage Elongation**

$$\% \in = \frac{84.5 - 62.5}{62.5} \times 100 = \frac{22.0}{62.5} \times 100 = 35.20\%$$

**Step 9 — Percentage Reduction in Area**

$$\% \psi = \frac{490.874 - 240.528}{490.874} \times 100 = \frac{250.346}{490.874} \times 100 = 51.00\%$$

**Result Table:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $E$ | $129.4 \text{ GPa}$ |
| $\sigma_{uy}$ | $249.55 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $\sigma_{ly}$ | $235.50 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $\sigma_u$ | $374.85 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $\sigma_b$ | $299.86 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $\% \in$ | $35.20\%$ |
| $\% \psi$ | $51.00\%$ |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Draw a **fully labeled stress–strain curve for mild steel**. Explain the significance of each characteristic point: proportional limit, elastic limit, upper yield, lower yield, ultimate point, and fracture point. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **engineering and physical significance** of the following properties obtained from a tension test:
- Percentage Elongation
- Percentage Reduction in Area
- Resilience and Toughness (as area under stress–strain curve)

State **one real-life engineering application** for each property. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Stress–Strain Curve (7 Marks)**

Refer to the annotated block diagram in Section 4.3. The curve has:

| Point | Stress | Strain | Significance |
|-------|--------|--------|--------------|
| O | 0 | 0 | Origin, unloaded state |
| A | $\sigma_p$ | $\epsilon_p$ | Proportional limit; $E = \sigma/\epsilon$ valid up to A |
| B | $\sigma_e$ | $\epsilon_e$ | Elastic limit; recoverable strain on unloading |
| C | $\sigma_{uy}$ | $\epsilon_{uy}$ | Upper yield; sudden onset of plasticity, Lüders bands appear |
| D | $\sigma_{ly}$ | $\epsilon_{ly}$ | Lower yield; deformation continues at constant load |
| E | $\sigma_{sh}$ | $\epsilon_{sh}$ | Strain hardening; material becomes stronger |
| F | $\sigma_u$ (max) | $\epsilon_u$ | Ultimate tensile strength; necking begins |
| G | $\sigma_b$ | $\epsilon_f$ | Fracture/breaking point; specimen ruptures |

**Significance for design:** Designers restrict working stress to **within the elastic region** (below point B) for brittle materials, and to **below yield** for ductile materials like mild steel, to avoid permanent deformation in service.

**Part (b) — Ductility and Energy Properties (7 Marks)**

**1. Percentage Elongation ($\% \in$):** Indicates the material's ability to **deform plastically** under tensile load before fracture.
- *Application:* Quality control test for **rebar (TMT bars)** in construction — IS 1786 mandates $\% \in \geq 14.5\%$ for Fe 415 grade.

**2. Percentage Reduction in Area ($\% \psi$):** Indicates the **localization of plastic flow** and is a more sensitive measure of ductility than elongation.
- *Application:* Used to assess the **formability of steel wires, rivets, and bolts** — high $\% \psi$ means the material can be drawn or headed without cracking.

**3. Resilience ($U_r$):** The energy absorbed per unit volume in the **elastic region** — equal to the area under the stress–strain curve up to the elastic limit.
$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$
- *Application:* Critical in design of **springs, shock absorbers, and railway buffers** — high resilience is needed where elastic energy must be stored and released.

**4. Toughness ($U_t$):** The total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture — equal to the **total area under the stress–strain curve**.
- *Application:* Essential for **earthquake-resistant structures, ship hulls, and automotive crumple zones** — high toughness allows the structure to absorb impact loads without catastrophic failure.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Using $P$ in N and $A_0$ in $m^2$ gives stress in Pa — but you must convert to **MPa** by dividing by $10^6$. Most KTU toppers lose 1 mark per question here.
> 2. **Final diameter confusion:** Always measure $d_f$ at the **smallest cross-section of the neck** — not at the average of the broken pieces. This is the single biggest source of error in $\% \psi$.
> 3. **Mixing up yield, ultimate, and breaking stress:** $\sigma_y < \sigma_u$ always. $\sigma_b$ may be greater or less than $\sigma_y$ depending on material — for mild steel, $\sigma_b$ is between $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_u$.
> 4. **Forgetting the difference between gauge length and total length:** $L_0$ is the **gauge length** (parallel section), not the total length of the specimen. Final length $L_f$ is also measured on the gauge length only.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU examiners award **2 marks** for the labeled specimen diagram and **2 marks** for the labeled stress–strain curve in 14-mark questions. Never omit them.
> 6. **Reporting $E$ as 80 GPa in a real test:** Mild steel should give $E \approx 200$ GPa. If your answer is way off, you have a unit or ratio error — recheck.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this checklist for last-minute revision.**

- [ ] Tension test on MS rod is performed on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** under gradually increasing **uniaxial tensile load**.
- [ ] Specimen geometry follows **IS 1608** with $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$.
- [ ] **Engineering stress** $\sigma = P / A_0$ uses the original area; **true stress** uses instantaneous area.
- [ ] Mild steel stress–strain curve has **5 key points**: A (proportional limit), B (elastic limit), C (upper yield), D (lower yield), F (UTS), H (fracture).
- [ ] The **yield plateau (C–D)** is unique to low-carbon steel and is caused by **Lüders band propagation**.
- [ ] **UTS** is calculated using the **maximum load recorded** during the test, not the breaking load.
- [ ] **Young's Modulus** is computed from the **linear (elastic) region** of the curve, typically between 0 and 50% of $\sigma_y$.
- [ ] **% Elongation** = $(L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$; **% Reduction in area** = $(A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$.
- [ ] Mild steel typically shows: $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_y \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_u \approx 400 \text{ MPa}$, $\% \in \approx 30\%$, $\% \psi \approx 50\%$.
- [ ] **Ductile fracture** in mild steel is **cup-and-cone** type — flat central region + 45° shear lip.
- [ ] **Resilience** = area under elastic portion of curve = $\sigma_y^2 / 2E$.
- [ ] **Toughness** = total area under stress–strain curve up to fracture.
- [ ] **Engineering stress falls after UTS** due to necking — but true stress **rises monotonically**.
- [ ] All stress values in KTU labs are in **MPa (N/mm²)** — be consistent with units.
- [ ] Always present a **labeled stress–strain diagram** and a **labeled specimen sketch** in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] The test is **destructive** — each specimen can be used only once.
- [ ] **Pre-load** before zeroing ensures no slack errors; **alignment of specimen axis with load axis** is critical to avoid eccentric (bending) stresses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion Test on MS Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Torsion Test on MS Rod

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion Test on MS Rod:** A destructive mechanical testing procedure conducted on a cylindrical Mild Steel (MS) rod specimen mounted in a **Torsion Testing Machine**, wherein a pure twisting moment (torque) is applied incrementally about the longitudinal axis of the specimen, and the corresponding **angle of twist** is measured to determine the **Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus, G)**, shear stress, shear strain, and the **torsional rigidity** of the material up to fracture.

The torsion test is governed by the fundamental elastic relationship derived from the theory of pure torsion in circular shafts, which forms a core experimental verification of the **Coulomb-Saint Venant torsion theory** for circular cross-sections.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine wringing out a wet cloth using both hands. Your hands apply equal and opposite twisting forces along the length of the cloth — this is essentially a "torsion" action. The wet cloth resists twisting, just like a steel rod resists being twisted.

| Everyday Object | Engineering Torsion Equivalent |
|----------------|--------------------------------|
| Wringing a wet cloth | Twisting of a shaft transmitting power |
| Turning a screw with a screwdriver | Torque application on a bolt |
| Stirring tea with a spoon | Rotational shear loading |
| Twisting a towel to squeeze water | Combined shear + normal stress (wet cloth analogy) |

In engineering, torsion is encountered in:
- **Drive shafts** of automobiles and machines
- **Drill bits** and boring bars
- **Axles** of railway vehicles
- **Helical springs** and coiled wires
- **Propeller shafts** in aircraft and ships

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard material properties are used:
> - **Modulus of Rigidity for Mild Steel (G)** ≈ **79.3 GPa** (typical reference value, actual experimental value ≈ 77–82 GPa)
> - **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for MS ≈ **0.29**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Shear Strength** for MS ≈ **330 MPa – 420 MPa**
> - **SI Unit of Torque (T)** = **N·m**
> - **SI Unit of Angle of Twist (θ)** = **radians (rad)**
> - **SI Unit of Shear Stress (τ)** = **Pa (N/m²)**, often expressed in **MPa** (N/mm²)
> - **SI Unit of Modulus of Rigidity (G)** = **Pa** or **GPa**

### 1.4 Purpose of the Torsion Test

> [!NOTE]
> The torsion test on a Mild Steel rod is performed in the strength of materials laboratory to:
> 1. **Verify** the linear elastic relationship $T = \frac{GJ\theta}{L}$ between torque and angle of twist.
> 2. **Experimentally determine** the Modulus of Rigidity (G) of the given MS rod.
> 3. **Determine** the shear stress at the surface of the shaft at various load levels.
> 4. **Plot** the Torque vs. Angle of Twist (T–θ) curve.
> 5. **Identify** the elastic limit, yield point, and the modulus of rupture under torsion.
> 6. **Observe** the mode of fracture (ductile shear failure in MS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Pure Torsion in a Circular Shaft

When a circular shaft is subjected to a torque **T** about its longitudinal axis, every cross-section experiences a **pure shearing action** along its plane. The fundamental assumptions of the theory are:

1. **Cross-sections remain plane and circular** after twisting (Saint-Venant's hypothesis).
2. **Cross-sections rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis without warping.
3. **Radial lines remain radial** and straight after deformation.
4. **Material is homogeneous and isotropic** within the elastic range.
5. The **angle of twist is proportional** to the applied torque (Hooke's law in shear).

### 2.2 Logical Derivation Flow (Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown)

**Step 1: Strain Analysis**
A fibre at radius **r** from the axis stretches along a helix. If the shaft twists by angle **θ** over length **L**, the shear strain is:
- Arc length on surface = $r\theta$
- Shear strain $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$

**Step 2: Stress Analysis (Equilibrium)**
Consider an elemental area $dA$ at radius **r**. The shear stress on it is $\tau_r$. The elemental force is $\tau_r \, dA$, and its moment about the axis is $\tau_r \cdot r \cdot dA$.
- Integrating over the cross-section:
$$T = \int_A \tau_r \cdot r \, dA$$

**Step 3: Material Law (Hooke's Law in Shear)**
Within the elastic limit:
