$$\tau_r = G \cdot \gamma = \frac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4: Final Torsion Equation**
Combining the above:
$$T = \int_A \frac{G \cdot r^2 \cdot \theta}{L} \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA = \frac{G J \theta}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER EQUATION:** $T = \dfrac{G J \theta}{L}$
> This is the most heavily tested equation in the KTU exam for the torsion experiment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are tabulated below. Note: Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of vertical pipes for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Applied Torque | $T = F \times r_{arm}$ | N·m | From torsion machine lever arm |
| 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | mm⁴ | $d$ = diameter of rod |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | mm⁴ | $D$ = outer, $d$ = inner diameter |
| 4 | Maximum Shear Stress (at surface) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | $r = d/2$ for solid shaft |
| 5 | Shear Strain at Surface | $\gamma = \dfrac{r \theta}{L} = \dfrac{d \theta}{2L}$ | rad (dimensionless) | L = gauge length |
| 6 | Modulus of Rigidity (Experimental) | $G = \dfrac{T L}{J \theta}$ | MPa or GPa | Slope of $T$ vs $\theta$ gives $GJ/L$ |
| 7 | Torsional Rigidity | $GJ$ | N·m² | Resistance to twisting |
| 8 | Strain Energy Stored | $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ | N·m or Joules | Elastic energy in twisted shaft |
| 9 | Power Transmitted by Shaft | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Watts (W) | $N$ = rpm |
| 10 | Angle of Twist per unit length | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | rad/mm | Slope of twist distribution |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this experiment matter?**
> 1. **Automotive Drive Shafts:** A car drive shaft transmits engine torque to the differential. The designer needs $G$ to compute the angle of twist and prevent vibration.
> 2. **Aircraft Propeller Shafts:** Lightweight and high-strength hollow shafts are designed using $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$ — the torsion test validates the selected alloy.
> 3. **Turbine Shafts in Power Plants:** Operate at high rpm and torque; failure leads to catastrophic consequences.
> 4. **Helical and Torsion Springs:** Spring design relies on $G$ value.
> 5. **Drill Rods and Boring Bars:** Operate under pure torsion in machining operations.

### 2.5 Failure Modes in Torsion of MS Rod

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Ductile (Mild Steel):** Fails along a **helical (45° spiral) plane** because principal tensile stress in pure torsion is at 45° to the shaft axis (maximum normal stress theory / Coulomb's criterion).
> - **Brittle (Cast Iron):** Fails along a **transverse plane** perpendicular to the axis (maximum tensile stress criterion).
> - MS Rod → **Cup-and-cone / fibrous shear surface** characteristic of ductile materials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Torsion Equation

We now rigorously derive $T = \dfrac{G J \theta}{L}$ from first principles.

**Starting Conditions:**
A solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ and length $L$ is fixed at one end. A torque $T$ is applied at the free end, causing it to rotate by angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal axis.

**Step 1: Geometry of Deformation**

Consider a point P on the surface of the shaft at radius $r = d/2$ from the axis. After twisting by angle $\theta$ over the length $L$, the point P moves to a new position P'.

The arc length swept by P on the cylindrical surface is:
$$s = r \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2: Definition of Shear Strain**

The shear strain $\gamma$ at radius $r$ is the angular deformation of an originally right-angled element of material on the surface, given by the arc length divided by the longitudinal length:
$$\gamma = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 3: Shear Stress from Hooke's Law in Shear**

In the linear elastic range, the shear stress is proportional to the shear strain through the Modulus of Rigidity $G$:
$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium of the Cross-Section**

The internal resisting torque $T$ on any cross-section equals the moment of the elemental shear forces about the axis:
$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA$$

Substituting the expression for $\tau$:
$$T = \int_A G \cdot \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L} \cdot r \, dA$$

Rearranging:
$$T = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

**Step 5: Polar Moment of Inertia**

The integral $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **polar moment of inertia** $J$ of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis.

For a **solid circular cross-section** of diameter $d$:
$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{d/2} r^2 \cdot r \, dr \, d\phi \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{d/2} r^3 \, dr \\
&= 2\pi \cdot \left[\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^{d/2} \\
&= 2\pi \cdot \frac{(d/2)^4}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \, d^4}{32}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Final Master Equation**

Substituting $J$ back:
$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

This is the **Master Torsion Equation** — the most important equation for this experiment.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

Since shear stress varies linearly from zero at the centre to a maximum at the outer surface:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J} = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^3}$$

**Numerical example derivation:** If $T = 50$ N·m, $d = 20$ mm:
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{16 \times 50 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}}{\pi \times (20)^3 \text{ mm}^3} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{\pi \times 8000} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{25{,}132.74} \\
&\approx 31.83 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 31.83 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Modulus of Rigidity (G) from Experimental Data

Rearranging the master equation:
$$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta}$$

In the experiment, we apply incremental torque $T_i$ and measure corresponding angle of twist $\theta_i$. Plotting $T$ on the y-axis vs $\theta$ on the x-axis gives a **straight line** through the origin in the elastic region.

The slope of this line is:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$

Therefore:
$$G = \frac{\text{Slope} \times L}{J}$$

### 3.4 Typical Calculation (Worked KTU-Style Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Question Style:** A mild steel rod of diameter 25 mm and gauge length 200 mm is tested in torsion. The torque-angle readings are tabulated. Compute $G$ and maximum shear stress.

| S.No | Torque $T$ (N·m) | Angle $\theta$ (degrees) | Angle $\theta$ (radians) |
|------|------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | 10 | 0.32 | 0.00559 |
| 2 | 20 | 0.65 | 0.01134 |
| 3 | 30 | 0.98 | 0.01710 |
| 4 | 40 | 1.30 | 0.02269 |
| 5 | 50 | 1.63 | 0.02845 |

**Given:** $d = 25$ mm, $L = 200$ mm.

**Step 1: Compute J:**
$$J = \frac{\pi \times (25)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 390625}{32} = 38{,}349.52 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Compute slope of $T$ vs $\theta$ (in radians):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope} &= \frac{50 - 0}{0.02845 - 0} \\
&= 1757.5 \text{ N·m/rad} \\
&= 1.7575 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute G:**
$$\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{\text{Slope} \times L}{J} \\
&= \frac{1.7575 \times 10^6 \times 200}{38{,}349.52} \\
&= \frac{3.515 \times 10^8}{38{,}349.52} \\
&= 9166.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 9.17 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear stress at $T = 50$ N·m:**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 50 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (25)^3} = 16.30 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** If the value of $G$ comes out around 9–11 GPa in calculation from raw readings, the calculation chain is correct, though the literature value is 79 GPa. The discrepancy is because **torsion testing machines are often scaled or use lever-arm factors** that must be applied as a **machine constant**. Always check the lever ratio in the apparatus manual.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Experimental Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen: MS Rod] --> B[Measure Diameter d and Length L]
    B --> C[Fix Specimen in Torsion Machine]
    C --> D[Set Initial Zero on Angle Dial]
    D --> E[Apply Incremental Torque T via Lever Arm]
    E --> F[Record Angle of Twist theta in Degrees]
    F --> G{Is theta in Linear Range?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Increase Torque Increment and Repeat]
    H --> E
    G -->|No / Fracture| I[Plot T vs theta Graph]
    I --> J[Compute Slope of Linear Region]
    J --> K[Calculate G from Slope x L / J]
    K --> L[Compute tau_max at Final Torque]
    L --> M[Tabulate Results and Verify with G approx 79 GPa]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Conceptual Stress Distribution in Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cross-section of MS Rod] --> B[Centre r=0]
    A --> C[Mid Radius r=d divide 4]
    A --> D[Surface r=d divide 2]
    B --> E[tau equals 0 at centre]
    D --> F[tau equals tau_max at surface]
    C --> G[tau varies linearly with r]
    E --> H[Linear stress distribution: tau proportional to r]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Result: Maximum shear stress occurs on outer surface]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Torsion Test Procedure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Stage: MS Rod Specimen and Machine] --> P2[Pre-Test Measurement: d, L, gauge marks]
    P2 --> P3[Loading Stage: Apply torque in steps]
    P3 --> P4[Measurement Stage: Record T and theta]
    P4 --> P5[Post-Processing: Convert degrees to radians]
    P5 --> P6[Analysis: Plot T-theta graph]
    P6 --> P7[Computation: G, tau_max, torsional rigidity]
    P7 --> P8[Output: Verify result and report]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Failure Mode Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Type] --> B[Ductile: MS Rod]
    A --> C[Brittle: Cast Iron]
    B --> D[Failure plane at 45 degrees helix]
    C --> E[Failure plane perpendicular to axis]
    D --> F[Cause: Maximum shear stress criterion]
    E --> G[Cause: Maximum normal stress criterion]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Modulus of Rigidity. What are its units, and how is it experimentally determined using a torsion test on an MS rod?

**Model Answer:**

The **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to shear strain ($\gamma$) within the elastic limit of a material:
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**Units:** $G$ is measured in $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) or GPa. For mild steel, the standard value is approximately **79.3 GPa**.

**Experimental Determination:**
1. Conduct a torsion test on the MS rod in a torsion testing machine.
2. Record incremental torque ($T$) and corresponding angle of twist ($\theta$).
3. Convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians.
4. Compute polar moment of inertia $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$.
5. Plot $T$ vs $\theta$ graph and determine the slope of the linear region.
6. Apply the formula:
$$G = \frac{\text{Slope} \times L}{J}$$

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion for a circular shaft.

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental assumptions of the pure torsion theory (Saint-Venant's theory) for a circular shaft are:

1. The **material is homogeneous and isotropic** within the elastic range.
2. **Cross-sections remain plane and circular** both before and after twisting (no warping).
3. **Cross-sections rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis.
4. **Radial lines remain radial and straight** with no change in length.
5. The **angle of twist is proportional** to the applied torque (Hooke's law in shear is valid).
6. The **shaft is subjected to pure torque** with no bending, axial, or transverse loading.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the torsion equation $T = \dfrac{GJ\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, clearly stating the assumptions used.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A solid MS shaft of diameter **40 mm** and length **1.5 m** is subjected to a torque of **200 N·m**. Calculate: (i) Maximum shear stress, (ii) Angle of twist in degrees, and (iii) Modulus of rigidity if the angle of twist observed is **2.5°**. Take $G = 80$ GPa for verification.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
*(Write the full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps:)*

1. Geometry: Shear strain at radius $r$ is $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$.
2. Hooke's Law in shear: $\tau = G\gamma = \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L}$.
3. Equilibrium: $T = \displaystyle\int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA = \dfrac{GJ\theta}{L}$.
4. For solid shaft: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$.

**Assumptions:** (state the 6 assumptions from Q2 above).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 40$ mm, $L = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm, $T = 200$ N·m $= 200 \times 10^3$ N·mm, $\theta = 2.5° = \dfrac{2.5 \times \pi}{180}$ rad.

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia**
$$J = \frac{\pi \times (40)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2{,}560{,}000}{32} = 251{,}327.4 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \\
&= \frac{16 \times 200 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (40)^3} \\
&= \frac{3.2 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 64{,}000} \\
&= \frac{3.2 \times 10^6}{201{,}061.93} \\
&\approx 15.92 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 15.92 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Angle of twist in degrees (theoretical)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{rad} &= \frac{T L}{G J} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times 1500}{80 \times 10^3 \times 251{,}327.4} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.0106 \times 10^{10}} \\
&\approx 0.01492 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_{deg} &= 0.01492 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.855°
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Modulus of rigidity from experiment**
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{exp} &= 2.5° = 2.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.04363 \text{ rad} \\
G_{exp} &= \frac{T L}{J \theta_{exp}} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times 1500}{251{,}327.4 \times 0.04363} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10{,}966.4} \\
&\approx 27{,}356 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 27.36 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #1:** Many students forget to convert **degrees to radians** before applying $G = \dfrac{TL}{J\theta}$. This single error can cost **3 marks**. Always check units.

---

> **Question B** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure of conducting a torsion test on a Mild Steel rod specimen in the laboratory. List the observations and the formula used to compute the Modulus of Rigidity.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** In a torsion test on a hollow MS shaft of outer diameter **50 mm** and inner diameter **30 mm**, the torque applied is **500 N·m** over a gauge length of **600 mm**. If the angle of twist measured is **1.2°**, calculate: (i) Polar moment of inertia, (ii) Maximum shear stress, (iii) Modulus of rigidity, and (iv) Strain energy stored in the shaft.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure:**

1. **Preparation:** Measure the diameter $d$ of the MS rod specimen using a vernier caliper at multiple points and take the average. Mark a gauge length $L$ (typically 100–200 mm) on the rod.
2. **Mounting:** Fix one end of the specimen rigidly in the **fixed chuck** of the torsion testing machine. The other end is clamped in the **rotating chuck**.
3. **Zero Setting:** Set the torque and angle dials to zero. Ensure the lever arm is horizontal.
4. **Loading:** Apply torque in equal increments (e.g., 10 N·m steps) by adding weights at the lever-arm pan or by rotating the handwheel.
5. **Observation:** For each torque increment, record the corresponding angle of twist from the protractor dial in degrees.
6. **Continue** until the rod fractures.
7. **Post-Test:** Measure the final gauge length and observe the fracture surface (helical at 45° for MS).
8. **Plot** the $T$ vs $\theta$ graph.
9. **Calculate** $G$ using the formula:
$$G = \frac{T L}{J \theta}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 50$ mm, $d = 30$ mm, $T = 500$ N·m $= 500 \times 10^3$ N·mm, $L = 600$ mm, $\theta = 1.2° = 0.02094$ rad.

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia (hollow)**
$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times [(50)^4 - (30)^4]}{32} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times [6{,}250{,}000 - 810{,}000]}{32} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 5{,}440{,}000}{32} \\
&= 534{,}070.75 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress (at outer surface)**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} \\
&= \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 25}{534{,}070.75} \\
&= \frac{12.5 \times 10^6}{534{,}070.75} \\
&\approx 23.41 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 23.41 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Modulus of rigidity**
$$\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{T L}{J \theta} \\
&= \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 600}{534{,}070.75 \times 0.02094} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{11{,}183.0} \\
&\approx 26{,}826 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 26.83 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Strain energy stored**
$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J} \\
&= \frac{(500 \times 10^3)^2 \times 600}{2 \times 26{,}826 \times 534{,}070.75} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{11} \times 600}{2.864 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{14}}{2.864 \times 10^{10}} \\
&\approx 5237 \text{ N·mm} = 5.237 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #2:**
> - For **hollow shafts**, many students incorrectly use $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ (the solid shaft formula). This leads to a 50–60% error in the answer. **Always check whether the shaft is solid or hollow.**
> - When the computed $G$ is much lower than the standard 79 GPa, students panic. **Do not**. Torsion testing machines often have **lever-arm mechanical advantage** (e.g., 1:10 or 1:5 ratios). Apply the **machine constant** as specified in your lab manual.
> - **Units consistency:** $T$ in N·mm, $L$ in mm, $J$ in mm⁴, $\theta$ in radians → $G$ in N/mm² = MPa.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Torsion Test Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting the radian conversion** for $\theta$ (Most common: −3 marks).
> 2. **Using the wrong $J$ formula** — hollow vs. solid confusion (−3 to −4 marks).
> 3. **Not mentioning the six assumptions** in derivation questions (−2 marks).
> 4. **Confusing shear stress with normal stress** — in torsion, the principal stresses are at 45°, not 0° or 90° (−2 marks).
> 5. **Neglecting the machine constant / lever ratio** in experimental $G$ calculation (−2 to −3 marks).
> 6. **Forgetting the "modulus of rigidity = slope × L / J"** concept and applying other wrong formulas (−3 marks).
> 7. **Improper units in the final answer** — always express in MPa or GPa (−1 mark).
> 8. **Not stating the failure mode** (helical at 45° for ductile MS) (−1 mark).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Torsion Test on MS Rod:**

- [x] **Torsion = Twisting** of a circular shaft about its longitudinal axis due to a torque $T$.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $T = \dfrac{GJ\theta}{L}$
- [x] **Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid):** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$
- [x] **Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow):** $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$
- [x] **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ (solid) — occurs on the outer surface.
- [x] **Shear Strain at Surface:** $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L} = \dfrac{d\theta}{2L}$
- [x] **Experimental G:** $G = \dfrac{\text{Slope of } T\text{-}\theta \text{ graph} \times L}{J}$
- [x] **Standard value of G for MS:** ≈ **79.3 GPa** (or 80 GPa for textbook problems).
- [x] **Always convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians** before calculation: $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.
- [x] **Failure mode of MS Rod:** Ductile shear failure along a **45° helical plane** (along principal tensile stress direction).
- [x] **Failure mode of Cast Iron:** Brittle fracture along a **transverse plane**.
- [x] **Strain Energy:** $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$ (used in shock loading and resilience problems).
- [x] **Torsional Rigidity** $= GJ$ — resistance of shaft to twisting.
- [x] **Six assumptions** of pure torsion theory must be memorised (homogeneous, plane sections, rigid disc, radial lines straight, Hooke's law in shear, pure torque).
- [x] **Couple of St. Venant's Principle:** Stress distribution becomes uniform away from points of load application.
- [x] **Apparatus used:** Torsion Testing Machine (with fixed chuck, rotating chuck, lever arm, protractor dial, weights).
- [x] **Graph plot:** $T$ (y-axis) vs $\theta$ (x-axis) → straight line in elastic region → slope = $GJ/L$.
- [x] **Apparatus constant** (lever ratio) must be applied to the dial reading before computing torque.
- [x] **Angle of twist is cumulative** — each new reading is added to the previous (not replaced).
- [x] **Polar moment of inertia $J$** has units of **mm⁴** (or m⁴ in SI) and is the **torsional analog of $I$** (area moment of inertia) used in bending.
- [x] **Application areas:** drive shafts, propeller shafts, drill bits, axles, helical springs.
- [x] **KTU Mark Distribution Hint:** In a 14-mark question, expect **(a) 7 marks for derivation + theory** and **(b) 7 marks for numerical calculation**.
- [x] **Quick numerical sanity check:** For $d = 25$ mm, $T = 50$ N·m → $\tau_{max} \approx 16$ MPa. Memorise this baseline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact Test Using Izod Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Impact Test Using Izod Apparatus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Izod Impact Test** is a standardized, high-strain-rate destructive testing procedure used to determine the **toughness** (ability to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing) of a material under a suddenly applied dynamic load. The test is conducted on a **pendulum-type impact testing machine**, where a notched specimen is held rigidly as a **vertical cantilever (fixed-free beam)** and is struck on the **notched side** by a single swing of the pendulum hammer released from a known initial elevation. The energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen, measured by the difference in the pendulum's potential energy before and after impact, is reported as the **Izod Impact Strength**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"The Izod impact test measures the energy required to fracture a notched specimen held as a cantilever beam, struck by a single blow of a pendulum hammer at the notch."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are holding a piece of chalk (a brittle material) sticking out horizontally from a wall (like a cantilever). If you tap the free end gently with a hammer, it snaps cleanly with very little effort. Now imagine doing the same with a thick plastic ruler — you would have to swing much harder before it finally breaks, and it would bend and deform a lot before snapping. The Izod test formalizes exactly this "tap-and-snap" idea, but measures **how much energy the ruler or chalk absorbed** before failure. The notch is like a pre-made weak point — it forces the crack to start at a known, controlled location, just like tearing a perforated edge on a snack packet is easier than tearing the middle. > [!NOTE] > - **Brittle materials** (e.g., glass, cast iron) absorb **little** energy and fail suddenly. > - **Ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel, aluminium) absorb **a lot** of energy and bend/deform before breaking. > - The energy absorbed = **toughness** of the material. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (KTU/IS 1757 / ASTM E23) | |---|---| | **Izod specimen cross-section** | **10 mm × 10 mm** | | **Specimen length** | **75 mm** | | **Notch type** | **V-notch, 45° included angle** | | **Notch depth** | **2 mm** | | **Notch root radius** | **0.25 mm** | | **Striking velocity** | ~ **3.5 to 5.5 m/s** (depending on capacity) | | **Standard test temperature** | **20 °C (room temperature)** | | **Report unit (energy)** | **Joules (J)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the Izod configuration, the **notch faces the striker** and the specimen is held as a **vertical cantilever** (top fixed, bottom free). This is the **key distinction from the Charpy test**, where the specimen is held as a **simply supported horizontal beam** with the notch on the **tension (opposite) side**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Notch? When a real engineering component (a shaft, a gear tooth, a connecting rod) is loaded dynamically, microscopic surface flaws or geometrical discontinuities act as **stress concentrators**. To make the test reproducible and to simulate a worst-case scenario, a **standardized sharp V-notch** is introduced. The notch: 1. **Localizes the fracture** to a known cross-section. 2. **Tri-axially constrains** the material at the notch root, shifting the failure mode toward brittle fracture. 3. Creates a **standardized stress concentration factor (Kt ≈ 2 to 3)** for a 45° V-notch of 0.25 mm root radius. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A notched specimen typically absorbs **less** energy than an un-notched one. This is why the Izod value is *not* an absolute material property but a **comparative index** for notch sensitivity and toughness ranking. ### 2.2 Working Principle — The Pendulum Energy Method The Izod testing machine is essentially a **gravitational potential energy measuring device**. **Step-by-step operational logic:** - The pendulum hammer of weight **W** (in Newtons) is raised and latched at an initial height **h₁** above the lowest point of its swing. - The **initial potential energy** stored = $W \cdot h_1$. - The pendulum is released, swings down, strikes the specimen, and fractures it. - The pendulum continues swinging upward on the other side (or the same side, depending on design) to a final height **h₂**. - The **residual potential energy** = $W \cdot h_2$. - The **energy absorbed in fracturing** the specimen is the difference: $$E_{absorbed} = W \cdot h_1 - W \cdot h_2 = W \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$$ The machine's graduated dial is **directly calibrated** to read this energy difference in **Joules (J)**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the ONLY equations you need for the entire lab exam.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Initial PE of pendulum | $E_1 = W \cdot h_1$ | Joules (J) | $W$ = weight of pendulum in N, $h_1$ = initial height in m | | 2 | Final PE of pendulum | $E_2 = W \cdot h_2$ | Joules (J) | $h_2$ = final (swing) height in m | | 3 | **Energy absorbed (Impact Energy)** | $E = E_1 - E_2 = W(h_1 - h_2)$ | **Joules (J)** | **This is what the dial reads.** | | 4 | Initial height (geometry) | $h_1 = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | m | $L$ = pendulum length, $\alpha$ = initial lift angle | | 5 | Final height (geometry) | $h_2 = L(1 - \cos\beta)$ | m | $\beta$ = final swing angle | | 6 | Energy absorbed (angle form) | $E = W L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ | Joules (J) | Substitute $h$ in geometric form | | 7 | **Izod Impact Strength (when area given)** | $I_s = \dfrac{E}{A_{notch}}$ | $\text{J/m}^2$ | $A_{notch}$ = cross-section at notch root = $b \times d_{remaining}$ | | 8 | Cross-section at notch | $A_{notch} = b \times (D - n)$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | $b$ = width, $D$ = total depth, $n$ = notch depth | | 9 | Friction/windage correction | $E_{corrected} = E_{dial} - E_{friction}$ | J | Subtracted to account for air drag and bearing friction | | 10 | Strain rate (typical) | $\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 10^{2} \text{ to } 10^{3} \ \text{s}^{-1}$ | s⁻¹ | Impact tests are at high strain rate | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Alert:** For standard 10×10 mm specimens, KTU examiners usually accept the **dial reading (J) directly as the Izod value** in lab reports. The "Impact Strength" formula (J/m²) is used only when an **unstandardized specimen** is tested and the area is specified. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | How Izod Test Helps | |---|---| | **Material selection for low-temperature service** (Arctic pipelines, ship hulls) | Determines whether material undergoes **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBTT)**. | | **Automotive crashworthiness** (bumpers, chassis rails) | Ensures materials absorb crash energy in a controlled, ductile manner. | | **Pressure vessel & boiler design** (ASME Sec. II) | Confirms parent metal and weld HAZ have sufficient notch toughness. | | **Quality control in steel mills** | Batch-wise comparison of incoming raw material. | | **Failure analysis** (forensic engineering) | Reproduces brittle fracture to identify causes of in-service failures. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Absorbed in Terms of Pendulum Geometry Let the pendulum hammer have: - Total weight = $W$ (in Newtons) - Distance from pivot to centre of gravity = $L$ (in metres) - Initial lift angle (from vertical) = $\alpha$ - Final swing angle (from vertical) = $\beta$ **Step 1 — Initial height of CG above the lowest point:** When the pendulum is lifted by angle $\alpha$, the centre of gravity rises. The vertical rise $h_1$ is given by the difference between the length $L$ and its vertical projection $L\cos\alpha$: $$h_1 = L - L\cos\alpha = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$$ **Step 2 — Final height of CG above the lowest point:** Similarly, after fracturing the specimen, the pendulum swings up to angle $\beta$: $$h_2 = L(1 - \cos\beta)$$ **Step 3 — Initial stored potential energy:** $$E_1 = W \cdot h_1 = W L (1 - \cos\alpha)$$ **Step 4 — Final residual potential energy:** $$E_2 = W \cdot h_2 = W L (1 - \cos\beta)$$ **Step 5 — Energy absorbed by specimen (by conservation of energy, neglecting friction and air drag):** $$\begin{aligned} E_{absorbed} &= E_1 - E_2 \\ &= WL(1 - \cos\alpha) - WL(1 - \cos\beta) \\ &= WL \cdot \big[ (1 - \cos\alpha) - (1 - \cos\beta) \big] \\ &= WL \cdot (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the master equation. The dial of the impact testing machine is **pre-calibrated** to compute $WL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ and display it directly. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Izod Impact Strength (when specimen area is given) For a non-standard specimen (e.g., research or quality comparison), the impact energy is normalized by the cross-sectional area **at the notch root**. **Step 1 — Original cross-section area:** $$A_{original} = b \times D$$ where $b$ = width (10 mm standard) and $D$ = depth (10 mm standard). **Step 2 — Area remaining at the notch root:** The V-notch of depth $n$ removes material. The remaining ligament area: $$A_{notch} = b \times (D - n)$$ **Step 3 — Impact Strength (specific energy absorption):** $$I_s = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{A_{notch}} = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{b(D - n)}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Calculation) **Problem Statement:** An Izod test was conducted on a mild steel specimen of cross-section **10 mm × 10 mm** with a V-notch of depth **2 mm**. The pendulum weight is **W = 200 N**, the pendulum length **L = 0.8 m**, the initial lift angle **α = 90°**, and the final swing angle after fracture **β = 40°**. Compute: (i) the energy absorbed in Joules, and (ii) the Izod impact strength in J/m². Take $g = 10 \ \text{m/s}^2$. **Step 1 — Energy absorbed (use the master equation):** $$E = W L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E &= 200 \times 0.8 \times (\cos 40° - \cos 90°) \\ &= 200 \times 0.8 \times (0.7660 - 0) \\ &= 160 \times 0.7660 \\ &= 122.56 \ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Area at notch root:** $$A_{notch} = b \times (D - n) = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 10 \times 8 = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$ Convert to $\text{m}^2$: $$A_{notch} = 80 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Izod impact strength:** $$I_s = \frac{E}{A_{notch}} = \frac{122.56}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.532 \times 10^{6} \ \text{J/m}^2$$ **Final Answer (with units):** - Energy absorbed = **122.56 J** - Izod impact strength = **$1.532 \times 10^6 \ \text{J/m}^2$** (or **153.2 J/cm²**) > [!NOTE] > **Mass of hammer (m) check:** $W = mg \Rightarrow m = 200/10 = 20 \ \text{kg}$. The pendulum mass is implicit and not required if $W$ is given directly. ### 3.4 Sources of Error in the Experiment | Source | Effect on Reading | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | **Friction in bearings** | Energy loss not due to fracture → **over-estimation** of absorbed energy | Apply friction correction by free swing test | | **Air drag (windage)** | Slight energy loss | Negligible at low speeds; use enclosed machines | | **Specimen not clamped tightly** | Specimen slips, less energy absorbed | Tighten vice firmly; check for movement | | **Notch root damage/blunting** | Increases apparent toughness | Use fresh, machined specimens | | **Pendulum released manually with push** | Adds unaccounted energy | Release gently from latch without forcing | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Izod Impact Test Setup The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the Izod test — illustrating how the energy flows from the gravitational PE reservoir, through the pendulum hammer, into the specimen, and finally to the fracture event and dial readout. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Hammer Assembly
Weight W, Length L"] nodeB["Latch and Release
Mechanism"] nodeC["Initial Lift Angle
alpha degrees"] nodeD["Initial Potential
Energy E1 = WL of 1 minus cos alpha"] nodeE["Swing Arc
Through Lowest Point"] nodeF["Notched Specimen
Vertical Cantilever
V notch faces striker"] nodeG["Striking Edge
of Hammer"] nodeH["Fracture Event
Energy E absorbed"] nodeI["Final Swing Angle
beta degrees"] nodeJ["Final Potential
Energy E2 = WL of 1 minus cos beta"] nodeK["Graduated Dial
Pointer reads E directly"] nodeL["Friction and
Windage Loss
E friction"] nodeM["Corrected Impact
Energy E dial minus E friction"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM nodeH --> nodeK ``` ### 4.2 Izod vs. Charpy — Functional Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA1["Izod Test"] nodeB1["Specimen held
VERTICALLY"] nodeC1["Cantilever
Fix-FREE"] nodeD1["Notch on
STRIKER side"] nodeE1["Single impact
at notch"] nodeF1["Energy in
JOULES"] nodeA2["Charpy Test"] nodeB2["Specimen held
HORIZONTALLY"] nodeC2["Simply supported
PIN-PIN"] nodeD2["Notch on
TENSION side
opposite to striker"] nodeE2["Single impact
at midspan"] nodeF2["Energy in
JOULES"] nodeA1 --> nodeB1 --> nodeC1 --> nodeD1 --> nodeE1 --> nodeF1 nodeA2 --> nodeB2 --> nodeC2 --> nodeD2 --> nodeE2 --> nodeF2 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of Izod Specimen (Notch Geometry) > **Visual Description (since Mermaid cannot draw physical notch profiles):** > > ``` > ←————————————— 75 mm —————————————→ > ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ ▲ > │ │ │ > │ │ │ > │ │ 10 mm > │ │ │ > │ V │ │ > │ ▲ │ │ > │ │ │ ▼ > │ 2 mm │ > │ (notch) │ > │ │ │ > │ ▼ │ > └──────────────────────────────────┘ > ◄──► > 45° V-notch > root radius 0.25 mm > ``` > > - The **notch opens towards the striker face**. > - The **un-notched face** is the side away from the impact. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the term "Izod Impact Strength" and list the standard specimen dimensions used for an Izod test as per IS 1757.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Izod Impact Strength** is defined as the energy absorbed in fracturing a standard notched specimen, held as a vertical cantilever, when struck by a single blow of a pendulum hammer at the notch. It is expressed in **Joules (J)**. **Standard Izod specimen dimensions (IS 1757):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Length | 75 mm | | Cross-section | 10 mm × 10 mm | | Notch type | 45° V-notch | | Notch depth | 2 mm | | Root radius | 0.25 mm | *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for dimensions, 1 mark for unit / standard reference.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between the Izod and Charpy impact tests in any three aspects.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Izod Test | Charpy Test | |---|---|---| | **Specimen orientation** | Held **vertically** as a cantilever | Held **horizontally** as a simply supported beam | | **Position of notch** | Notch is on the **striker side** (face of impact) | Notch is on the **tension side** (opposite to striker) | | **Type of loading** | **Cantilever** bending (fixed-free) | **Three-point** bending (simply supported) | | **Standard reference** | IS 1757 (Izod) | IS 1757 (Charpy) / ASTM E23 | *(1 mark each for any three distinct differences.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]** **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an **Izod impact testing machine**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An Izod test on a cast iron specimen (10 mm × 10 mm cross-section, 2 mm V-notch) gave a pendulum swing from an initial angle of **90°** to a final angle of **75°**. The pendulum weight is **150 N** and the effective length is **0.85 m**. Calculate: (i) the energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen, and (ii) the Izod impact strength in J/m². **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Construction and Working of the Izod Impact Testing Machine (7 marks)** **Construction:** 1. **Heavy base** (cast iron) with provisions for levelling screws to keep the machine vertical. 2. **Two rigid pillars** supporting a **pivot** at the top. 3. **Pendulum hammer** — a heavy disc of mass $m$ attached to a rigid arm of length $L$, pivoted at the top. 4. **Latching and release mechanism** to hold the pendulum at a preset initial angle and release it without shock. 5. **Striking edge** (knife-edge) on the pendulum, hardened to resist deformation. 6. **Specimen vice (clamping jaws)** mounted on the base, positioned so that the specimen is held **vertically** with the notch at the level of the striking edge. 7. **Graduated dial** (0–300 J typical) with a **pointer/drag indicator** that records the **final swing angle $\beta$**; the dial is calibrated to read energy directly. 8. **Friction ring / brake** to stop the pendulum after one swing. **Working:** 1. The notched specimen is clamped **vertically** in the vice with the **notch facing the striker**. 2. The pendulum is raised to the latched position at the initial angle $\alpha$ (typically 90° for a 300 J machine). 3. The pointer is set to the maximum energy position on the dial. 4. The pendulum is **released without imparting extra force**. 5. The pendulum swings down, strikes the specimen at the notch, and **fractures** it. 6. The pendulum continues swinging up to a final angle $\beta$ on the other side. 7. The **drag pointer** holds the maximum position reached, indicating the energy absorbed (Joules). 8. The specimen is removed, and the procedure is repeated for additional samples. **Key Energy Equation:** $$E = W L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$ where $W$ = weight of pendulum, $L$ = distance from pivot to CG, $\alpha$ = initial angle, $\beta$ = final angle. *(Distribution: 3 marks construction list, 2 marks sketch description, 2 marks working sequence.)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given data:** - $b = 10 \ \text{mm}$, $D = 10 \ \text{mm}$, $n = 2 \ \text{mm}$ - $\alpha = 90°$, $\beta = 75°$ - $W = 150 \ \text{N}$, $L = 0.85 \ \text{m}$ **Step 1 — Energy absorbed:** $$E = W L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E &= 150 \times 0.85 \times (\cos 75° - \cos 90°) \\ &= 127.5 \times (0.2588 - 0) \\ &= 127.5 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 33.0 \ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area at notch root:** $$A_{notch} = b \times (D - n) = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$ Convert to $\text{m}^2$: $$A_{notch} = 80 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Izod impact strength:** $$I_s = \frac{E}{A_{notch}} = \frac{33.0}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.125 \times 10^{5} \ \text{J/m}^2$$ **Final Results:** - (i) Energy absorbed = **33.0 J** - (ii) Izod impact strength = **$4.125 \times 10^5 \ \text{J/m}^2$** (or **41.25 J/cm²**) *(Distribution: 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks area conversion, 1 mark final unit.)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain the **significance of the notch** in an Izod impact test specimen. How does the notch orientation and geometry affect the measured impact energy? **(7 marks)** **(b)** In an Izod test, the pendulum of weight **180 N** and effective length **0.9 m** is released from **120°** initial angle. After fracturing the specimen, the pendulum rises to **30°** on the other side. Neglecting friction, calculate: (i) the initial and final potential energies of the pendulum, and (ii) the energy absorbed. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Significance of the Notch (7 marks)** 1. **Stress concentration:** The sharp V-notch (0.25 mm root radius) creates a local stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 2$–$3$. This forces the crack to initiate at a known, reproducible location — the notch root. 2. **Tri-axial stress state:** The notch root constrains plastic flow in all three directions, promoting brittle fracture even in ductile materials. This makes the test **conservative** (worst-case simulation). 3. **Standardization:** By fixing notch depth (2 mm), angle (45°), and root radius (0.25 mm), test results from different laboratories can be **directly compared** for the same material. 4. **Simulation of real defects:** Real engineering components have scratches, machining marks, and micro-cracks that act as stress raisers. The notch **simulates** these in a controlled manner. 5. **Effect of orientation and geometry on impact energy:** - A **deeper notch** → less remaining ligament → **lower** energy absorption. - A **sharper notch** (smaller root radius) → higher stress concentration → **lower** energy (more brittle failure). - A **blunted notch** → less stress concentration → **higher** energy (more ductile behaviour). - The **notch facing the striker** (Izod configuration) puts the notch root in **tension** when the pendulum strikes, ensuring maximum notch sensitivity. *(Distribution: 2 marks stress concentration, 2 marks standardization/simulation, 2 marks effect of geometry, 1 mark conclusion.)* --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given data:** - $W = 180 \ \text{N}$ - $L = 0.9 \ \text{m}$ - $\alpha = 120°$ - $\beta = 30°$ **Step 1 — Initial potential energy:** $$E_1 = W L (1 - \cos\alpha)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_1 &= 180 \times 0.9 \times (1 - \cos 120°) \\ &= 162 \times (1 - (-0.5)) \\ &= 162 \times 1.5 \\ &= 243 \ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Final potential energy:** $$E_2 = W L (1 - \cos\beta)$$ $$\begin{aligned} E_2 &= 180 \times 0.9 \times (1 - \cos 30°) \\ &= 162 \times (1 - 0.8660) \\ &= 162 \times 0.1340 \\ &= 21.71 \ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Energy absorbed:** $$E = E_1 - E_2 = 243 - 21.71 = 221.29 \ \text{J}$$ **Final Results:** - (i) $E_1 = 243 \ \text{J}$, $E_2 = 21.71 \ \text{J}$ - (ii) Energy absorbed = **221.29 J** *(Distribution: 2 marks $E_1$ derivation, 2 marks $E_2$ derivation, 2 marks subtraction and final answer, 1 mark unit.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit errors in angle:** Angles must be in **degrees** when using the calculator's `cos` function directly, OR in **radians** if using radian mode. Mixing them gives catastrophic errors. > 2. **Forgetting the area conversion:** J/m² requires area in **m²**, not mm². The factor $10^{-6}$ is missed by ~40% of students. > 3. **Confusing Izod and Charpy geometry:** In Izod, notch faces striker. In Charpy, notch is opposite. Examiners **specifically** test this distinction. > 4. **Not stating the formula** before substitution: KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the formula is not explicitly written. > 5. **Ignoring friction correction** in the lab report (not in the numerical). The lab report should mention the free-swing friction test. > 6. **Using mass (m) instead of weight (W):** $W = mg$. If mass is given, multiply by $g = 9.81$ (or 10 for KTU) to get weight in Newtons. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Izod test** = vertical cantilever, **notch faces striker**, single pendulum blow. - ✅ **Standard specimen** = 75 mm long, **10 × 10 mm** cross-section, **2 mm deep 45° V-notch**, **0.25 mm root radius**. - ✅ **Master formula:** $E = W L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ → gives energy in **Joules**. - ✅ **Impact strength** (if area given): $I_s = E / [b(D - n)]$ → in **J/m²**. - ✅ **Convert mm² to m²** by multiplying by $10^{-6}$ — the most common error! - ✅ **Dial reading** = direct energy absorbed for standard specimens. - ✅ **Brittle materials** (cast iron, glass) → **low** Izod value; **Ductile materials** (mild steel, Al) → **high** Izod value. - ✅ **Notch function:** stress concentrator + simulation of real defects + standardization. - ✅ **Friction correction** = $E_{corrected} = E_{dial} - E_{friction}$ (from free-swing test). - ✅ **Three differences from Charpy:** orientation (vertical vs horizontal), notch position (striker side vs opposite), support type (cantilever vs simply supported). - ✅ **Energy stored in pendulum** = gravitational PE = $mgh$ or equivalently $WL(1 - \cos\theta)$. - ✅ **High strain rate** ($\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 10^2$–$10^3 \ \text{s}^{-1}$) makes the test **dynamic**, not static. - ✅ **Always write the formula first** in numerical problems, then substitute — KTU marking scheme rewards this. - ✅ **Units to remember:** $W$ in N, $L$ and $h$ in m, $E$ in J, $I_s$ in J/m². - ✅ **Ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** is studied by repeating Izod tests at varied temperatures — a key application.

topics.title: Impact Test Using Charpy Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Impact Test Using Charpy Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact Test (Charpy Method):** A standardized, high-strain-rate pendulum-type destructive test that quantifies the **toughness** of a material by measuring the energy absorbed when a notched specimen is fractured by a single swing of a pendulum hammer under precisely controlled conditions (per **ASTM E23 / IS 1757 / ISO 148-1**).

**Key Terminology Decoded:**

| Term | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|------|---------------------|-------|
| **Toughness** | Ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform **before** fracturing | Joules (J) |
| **Impact Energy (KV)** | Total energy absorbed in breaking the specimen | Joules (J) |
| **Notch Toughness** | Impact energy per unit cross-sectional area at the notch | J/mm² |
| **DBTT** | Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (critical for BCC metals) | °C |
| **Pendulum Capacity** | Maximum energy the hammer can deliver (typically **300 J** or **150 J**) | Joules (J) |
| **Striking Velocity** | Velocity of hammer at moment of impact (typically ~ **5 m/s**) | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> The "KV2" notation refers to a **V-notch of 2 mm depth** with a root radius of **0.25 mm** — this is the standard Charpy specimen designation used in KTU lab records and KTU university exam answer sheets.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The "Snapping a Chocolate Bar" Analogy:**

Imagine holding a Snickers bar. If you *slowly* bend it, it deforms gracefully and bends a lot before breaking — it shows **toughness**. Now imagine *hitting* the same bar sharply on a table edge — it shatters instantly with barely any bending. **The same material behaves completely differently under sudden (impact) loading versus slow (static) loading.** The Charpy test is the engineering laboratory's way of simulating that "hitting" event in a controlled, measurable, and reproducible way.

**Another Analogy — The "Pendulum Energy Bank":**
Think of the raised pendulum as a *fully charged battery* storing gravitational potential energy. When released, this energy discharges into the specimen. A *tough* specimen "drains" a lot of that energy (large swing-angle loss), while a *brittle* specimen breaks instantly and barely saps the swing (small swing-angle loss). The dial directly reads how much energy the specimen stole from the pendulum.

## 1.3 Why This Test Matters (KTU Industrial Relevance)

- **Quality Control** in steel plants (railway rails, ship plates, pressure vessels).
- **Selection of materials** for service in **cold climates** (Arctic pipelines, ship hulls).
- **Failure analysis** of structures that failed catastrophically (e.g., Liberty Ships in WWII).
- **Weld qualification** in fabrication industries.
- **Mandatory test** for materials used in nuclear, defence, and aerospace sectors.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often award extra credit if a student mentions the **"Liberty Ship Failures"** as a historical example of why impact testing became a mandatory code requirement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The "Why" Behind the Test

The Charpy test exploits the principle of **Conservation of Energy** applied to a pendulum system:

1. The pendulum hammer of mass **M** (kg) is latched at an initial angle **α** from the vertical.
2. The center of gravity is at radius **R** (m) from the pivot.
3. At rest position, the center of gravity is at its lowest point — defining zero PE.
4. When raised to angle **α**, the center of gravity is lifted by a height:
   $$ h_i = R \cdot (1 - \cos \alpha) $$
5. Upon release, gravitational PE converts into kinetic energy (KE) at the bottom of the swing.
6. At the instant of impact, KE = M · g · R(1 - cos α).
7. Some of this energy is **siphoned off** by the specimen to break it.
8. The pendulum continues on the other side and rises to a final angle **β** (smaller than α).
9. The energy remaining in the pendulum is M · g · R(1 - cos β).
10. **Energy absorbed by specimen = Initial PE − Final PE** of pendulum.

> [!NOTE]
> The dial of the Charpy machine is pre-calibrated to read the difference **M g R(cos β − cos α)** directly in **Joules**, so the student does not have to compute the swing angles manually.

## 2.2 Logic of the Test — Stepwise "How"

- **Step 1:** The **V-notch** acts as a *stress concentrator* — it forces the crack to initiate from a known, controlled location.
- **Step 2:** The pendulum is raised to a fixed height and **latched** (initial angle α = 140°–160° standard).
- **Step 3:** The specimen is placed **horizontally** as a **simply-supported beam** (span = 40 mm) with the notch facing **away** from the striking edge.
- **Step 4:** Latch is released; hammer swings, strikes the specimen **behind the notch**.
- **Step 5:** The specimen fractures; pendulum continues, dragging a **friction pointer** up the calibrated dial.
- **Step 6:** The maximum height reached (final angle β) corresponds to residual energy; the dial reading = absorbed energy.
- **Step 7:** Energy reading is corrected for **air friction**, **windage**, and **specimen size** (if sub-standard).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE Lab Exam — these formulas are tested every year.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $E = W \cdot R \cdot (\cos \beta - \cos \alpha)$ | Impact energy absorbed (W = weight = Mg) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E = M \cdot g \cdot R \cdot (\cos \beta - \cos \alpha)$ | Same as #1, written in terms of mass | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $U_i = M g R (1 - \cos \alpha)$ | Initial gravitational PE of pendulum | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $U_f = M g R (1 - \cos \beta)$ | Final gravitational PE of pendulum (after break) | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $E = U_i - U_f$ | Energy balance (defining equation) | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{E}{A_n}$ | Impact strength (notch toughness) | J/mm² |
| 7 | $A_n = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ mm}^2$ | Cross-sectional area at V-notch (standard) | mm² |
| 8 | $K = 10 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{B \cdot B'}}$ | Size correction factor for sub-size specimens | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{corr} = E_{obs} \times K$ | Corrected impact energy | Joules (J) |
| 10 | $v = \sqrt{2 g R (1 - \cos \alpha)}$ | Striking velocity of hammer | m/s |

> **Notation key:** $M$ = mass of pendulum (kg), $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $R$ = distance from pivot to CG (m), $\alpha$ = initial angle, $\beta$ = final angle, $W$ = weight (N), $B \times B'$ = actual specimen cross-section dimensions (mm).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding**: Bureau of Indian Standards (IS) mandates Charpy testing for grade-A steel plates.
- **Pipeline Engineering**: API 5L standards for natural gas pipelines require impact tests at sub-zero temperatures.
- **Automotive**: Crash-relevant components (chassis rails, bumper beams) are impact-tested.
- **Nuclear Industry**: Reactor pressure vessel steels undergo Charpy testing across a wide temperature range to establish the **Master Curve** for DBTT.
- **Railway**: Rails must satisfy minimum KV2 values at specified service temperatures to avoid brittle fracture in cold regions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Absorbed by the Specimen

**Given:**
- Pendulum mass = $M$ (kg)
- Distance from pivot to center of gravity = $R$ (m)
- Initial swing angle (from vertical) = $\alpha$
- Final swing angle (from vertical, after fracture) = $\beta$
- Acceleration due to gravity = $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Derivation:**

When the pendulum is latched at angle $\alpha$, the vertical height of its center of gravity above the lowest (rest) position is:

$$
h_i = R - R\cos\alpha = R(1 - \cos\alpha)
$$

The initial potential energy stored in the pendulum is therefore:

$$
U_i = M g h_i = M g R (1 - \cos\alpha)
$$

After the specimen fractures, the pendulum rises to a smaller angle $\beta$ on the opposite side. The vertical lift of the center of gravity above the lowest position is now:

$$
h_f = R(1 - \cos\beta)
$$

The residual (unabsorbed) energy left in the pendulum is:

$$
U_f = M g h_f = M g R (1 - \cos\beta)
$$

By the principle of **conservation of energy** (neglecting air resistance and bearing friction, both of which are accounted for in the machine's calibration chart), the energy absorbed by the specimen is:

$$
E = U_i - U_f
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
E = M g R (1 - \cos\alpha) - M g R (1 - \cos\beta)
$$

Expanding the brackets:

$$
E = M g R - M g R \cos\alpha - M g R + M g R \cos\beta
$$

The $M g R$ terms cancel:

$$
E = M g R \cos\beta - M g R \cos\alpha
$$

Factoring $M g R$:

$$
\boxed{E = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}
$$

Since weight $W = M g$, this is equivalently written as:

$$
\boxed{E = W R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **defining derivation** the KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce on the answer script for the "Theory" section of the lab record.

## 3.2 Standard Specimen Geometry (As per ASTM E23)

| Parameter | Standard V-Notch (KV2) | Standard U-Notch (KU2) |
|-----------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Length $L$ | **55 mm** | 55 mm |
| Width $B$ | **10 mm** | 10 mm |
| Height $H$ | **10 mm** | 10 mm |
| Notch type | V (45° included angle) | U (keyhole) |
| Notch depth | **2 mm** | 2 mm (or 5 mm) |
| Root radius | **0.25 mm** | 1.0 mm |
| Net section at notch | $10 \times 8 = 80$ mm² | varies |
| Span between anvils | **40 mm** | 40 mm |
| Striking edge radius | **2 mm** or **8 mm** | 2 mm or 8 mm |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A Charpy impact test was performed on a standard mild steel specimen. The following data was recorded:
- Mass of pendulum, $M$ = **22.5 kg**
- Effective length (pivot to CG), $R$ = **0.84 m**
- Initial angle of swing, $\alpha$ = **140°**
- Final angle of swing after fracture, $\beta$ = **88°**
- Specimen cross-section at notch = $10 \text{ mm} \times 8 \text{ mm}$

**Calculate:** (i) Impact energy absorbed, (ii) Impact strength (notch toughness).

**Step 1 — Compute the Energy Absorbed $E$:**

$$
E = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

$$
E = 22.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.84 \times (\cos 88° - \cos 140°)
$$

Compute the cosines:

$$
\cos 88° \approx 0.0349
$$

$$
\cos 140° \approx -0.7660
$$

Substituting:

$$
E = 22.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.84 \times (0.0349 - (-0.7660))
$$

$$
E = 22.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.84 \times (0.0349 + 0.7660)
$$

$$
E = 22.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.84 \times 0.8009
$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$
22.5 \times 9.81 = 220.725
$$

$$
220.725 \times 0.84 = 185.409
$$

$$
185.409 \times 0.8009 \approx 148.49 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 148.5 \text{ J}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Impact Strength (Notch Toughness) $I$:**

Area at notch:

$$
A_n = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Notch toughness:

$$
I = \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{148.5}{80} = 1.856 \text{ J/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 1.86 \text{ J/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since $E \approx 148.5$ J, which is **greater than 27 J** (the conventional threshold for "tough" mild steel at room temperature), the material is classified as **ductile** under the given test conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Charpy vs. Izod (Frequently Tested Comparison)

| Feature | Charpy Test | Izod Test |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| Specimen orientation | **Horizontal** (simply supported) | **Vertical** (cantilever) |
| Notch position | At **mid-span**, facing away from striker | At **top**, facing towards striker |
| Striking point | Behind the notch | Above the notch |
| Standard specimen | $10 \times 10 \times 55$ mm, 2 mm V-notch | Same geometry |
| Energy measured | $E$ (Joules) directly on dial | $E$ (Joules) directly on dial |
| Common usage | **Ductile-Brittle Transition** studies | Comparative ranking of plastics |
| Standard | **ASTM E23 / IS 1757** | ASTM D256 (for plastics) |

## 3.5 Size-Correction Sub-Routine (For Sub-Standard Specimens)

If the specimen is non-standard (e.g., $7.5 \times 10$ mm), the size-correction factor is applied:

$$
K = 10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10}{B \cdot B'}}
$$

where $B$ and $B'$ are the **actual** specimen width and thickness in mm.

**Example:** For a $7.5 \times 10$ mm specimen:

$$
K = 10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10}{7.5 \times 10}} = 10 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10}{75}} = 10 \cdot \sqrt{0.1333} \approx 10 \times 0.3651 \approx 3.65
$$

The corrected energy:

$$
E_{corr} = E_{obs} \times 3.65
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This correction is purely empirical and is required when the available material is too thin to machine a full 10×10 mm specimen (common in plate and sheet testing).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Charpy Impact Testing Apparatus (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    baseFrameA[BASE FRAME AND FOUNDATION] --> verticalPillarsA[VERTICAL PILLARS]
    verticalPillarsA --> pivotBearingA[PIVOT BEARING HOUSING]
    pivotBearingA --> pendulumArmA[PENDULUM ARM WITH HAMMER HEAD]
    pendulumArmA --> strikingEdgeA[STRIKING EDGE RADIUS 2 MM]
    pendulumArmA --> cgMarkerA[CENTER OF GRAVITY AT RADIUS R]
    pendulumArmA --> latchA[RELEASE LATCH AND HOOK]
    pendulumArmA --> frictionPointerA[FRICTION POINTER AND DIAL]
    verticalPillarsA --> specimenAnvilA[SPECIMEN ANVILS SPAN 40 MM]
    specimenAnvilA --> specimenHolderA[NOTCHED SPECIMEN 10 BY 10 BY 55 MM]
    frictionPointerA --> energyDialA[CALIBRATED ENERGY DIAL IN JOULES]
    energyDialA --> brakingSystemA[BRAKING AND RESET MECHANISM]
    verticalPillarsA --> safetyShieldA[SAFETY SHIELD AND ENCLOSURE]
```

**Component Roles Decoded:**

- **BASE FRAME**: Heavy cast-iron foundation that absorbs the impact reaction; must be rigidly bolted to a concrete plinth.
- **PIVOT BEARING**: Low-friction precision bearing; ensures the energy loss to friction is minimal and predictable.
- **PENDULUM ARM**: The "lever" that stores and delivers the impact energy. Length chosen so that the center of gravity is at the calibrated radius R.
- **STRIKING EDGE**: Hardened tool-steel edge with a standardized radius (2 mm for sub-size, 8 mm for full-size specimens).
- **FRICTION POINTER**: A loose pointer dragged by a light friction fit — it stays at the highest point reached by the pendulum, indicating the residual energy.
- **ENERGY DIAL**: A graduated scale, calibrated such that the pointer directly indicates the energy *lost* (i.e., absorbed by the specimen) in Joules.
- **BRAKING MECHANISM**: Prevents the pendulum from oscillating freely after the test; allows it to be re-latched.
- **SAFETY SHIELD**: A mandatory polycarbonate enclosure that protects the operator from flying fragments.

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    step1[VERIFY MACHINE CALIBRATION AND ZERO POINTER] --> step2[MEASURE SPECIMEN DIMENSIONS B B PRIME]
    step2 --> step3[INSPECT V NOTCH FOR SYMMETRY AND ROOT RADIUS]
    step3 --> step4[PLACE SPECIMEN HORIZONTALLY ON ANVILS]
    step4 --> step5[CENTER NOTCH MIDWAY BETWEEN ANVILS]
    step5 --> step6[ENSURE NOTCH FACES AWAY FROM STRIKER]
    step6 --> step7[RAISE PENDULUM TO STANDARD ANGLE ALPHA]
    step7 --> step8[RELEASE LATCH AND ALLOW FREE SWING]
    step8 --> step9[PENDULUM STRIKES SPECIMEN AT BOTTOM]
    step9 --> step10[SPECIMEN FRACTURES INTO TWO PIECES]
    step10 --> step11[PENDULUM CONTINUES TO ANGLE BETA]
    step11 --> step12[POINTER LOCKS AT MAXIMUM SWING]
    step12 --> step13[RECORD ENERGY DIAL READING IN JOULES]
    step13 --> step14[COLLECT FRACTURED PIECES FOR FRACTOGRAPHY]
    step14 --> step15[BRAKE PENDULUM AND RESET FOR NEXT TEST]
```

## 4.3 Specimen Geometry — V-Notch (ASTM E23 KV2 Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dimL[LENGTH L EQUALS 55 MM] --> dimH[HEIGHT H EQUALS 10 MM]
    dimH --> dimB[WIDTH B EQUALS 10 MM]
    dimB --> notchD[NOTCH DEPTH D EQUALS 2 MM]
    notchD --> notchA[NOTCH ANGLE 45 DEGREES INCLUDED]
    notchA --> notchR[ROOT RADIUS 0.25 MM]
    notchR --> netA[NET SECTION 8 MM BY 10 MM EQUALS 80 MM SQ]
    netA --> strikeR[STRIKING EDGE RADIUS 2 MM OR 8 MM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note how the **net section** (the un-notched ligament behind the notch) determines the fracture cross-section. The 2 mm V-notch leaves $10 - 2 = 8$ mm of net depth, giving $A_n = 80$ mm² — this is the denominator used in the **notch toughness** formula.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "impact toughness" of a material. State the standard specimen designation for a Charpy V-notch test.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Impact Toughness** is defined as the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation **prior to fracture** under a suddenly applied (high-strain-rate) load. It is measured in **Joules (J)** and is a direct indicator of the material's resistance to brittle failure.
>
> The standard Charpy V-notch specimen is designated **"KV2"**, where:
> - **K** = KERBSCHLAG (German for "impact notch")
> - **V** = V-shaped notch (45° included angle)
> - **2** = 2 mm depth of notch
>
> Standard specimen dimensions per **ASTM E23**: $L = 55$ mm, cross-section $= 10 \times 10$ mm, notch root radius $= 0.25$ mm.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Standard (1.5 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Charpy and Izod impact tests on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Charpy Test | Izod Test |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Specimen orientation** | Horizontal — simply supported between two anvils (span 40 mm) | Vertical — clamped as a cantilever at the bottom |
| **Notch position** | At the centre of the span, **facing away** from the striker | At the top of the specimen, **facing towards** the striker |
| **Striking point** | Behind the notch (on the tension side) | Above the notch (on the tension side) |
| **Usage** | Metals, especially steels; ductile-brittle transition studies | Plastics and polymers (ASTM D256) |

**Marks Distribution:** Three differences × 1 mark = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working of the Charpy impact testing machine**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

The Charpy impact testing machine consists of the following main components:

1. **Heavy base frame** of cast iron, rigidly bolted to a concrete foundation, which absorbs the impact reaction without vibration.
2. **Two vertical pillars** mounted on the base frame, supporting the entire pendulum assembly.
3. **Pendulum assembly** comprising a heavy hammer head mounted on a rigid arm. The pendulum is pivoted on **precision bearings** at the top of the pillars. The mass and effective length of the pendulum are designed such that the center of gravity is at a precisely known distance $R$ from the pivot. Common capacities are 300 J and 150 J.
4. **Striking edge** — a hardened tool-steel edge with a standardized radius of **2 mm** (for sub-size specimens) or **8 mm** (for full-size specimens), bolted to the hammer head.
5. **Specimen anvils** — two rigid supports fixed to the base frame, precisely **40 mm apart**, on which the specimen rests.
6. **Friction pointer and calibrated dial** — a low-friction pointer that remains at the maximum height reached by the pendulum after breaking the specimen; the dial is pre-calibrated to read the **energy absorbed** directly in Joules.
7. **Release latch** — a spring-loaded mechanism that releases the pendulum from the latched position without imparting any additional kinetic energy.
8. **Pendulum-raising handle and braking mechanism** — to raise the pendulum to the initial angle and to arrest its motion after the test.
9. **Safety enclosure** — a transparent polycarbonate shield to protect the operator from flying fragments.

**Working (3 marks):**

The pendulum is raised and latched at an initial angle $\alpha$ (typically 140°), storing a known gravitational potential energy $U_i = M g R (1 - \cos\alpha)$. A standard notched specimen is placed horizontally on the anvils with the notch facing away from the striker. The latch is released; the pendulum swings down, converting its PE into KE. At the lowest point, it strikes the specimen behind the notch. The specimen fractures, absorbing an amount of energy $E$, and the pendulum continues to swing to a lower height corresponding to angle $\beta$ on the opposite side. The residual energy $U_f = M g R (1 - \cos\beta)$ is what remains. By energy conservation, **$E = U_i - U_f = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$**. The friction pointer, driven by the pendulum, slides up the calibrated dial and locks at the maximum height reached, giving a direct reading of $E$ in Joules.

---

**b)** In a Charpy test on a steel specimen, the following data was obtained:
- Mass of pendulum = 18 kg
- Effective length (pivot to CG) = 0.80 m
- Initial angle of swing = 135°
- Final angle of swing = 80°
- Specimen cross-section at notch = 10 mm × 8 mm

Calculate: **(i)** the impact energy absorbed by the specimen, **(ii)** the impact strength (notch toughness), and **(iii)** state whether the material is **ductile or brittle** at the test temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $M = 18$ kg
- $R = 0.80$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\alpha = 135°$
- $\beta = 80°$
- $A_n = 10 \times 8 = 80$ mm²

**(i) Impact Energy $E$:**

$$
E = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

Compute cosines:

$$
\cos 80° \approx 0.1736
$$

$$
\cos 135° \approx -0.7071
$$

Substituting:

$$
E = 18 \times 9.81 \times 0.80 \times (0.1736 - (-0.7071))
$$

$$
E = 18 \times 9.81 \times 0.80 \times (0.1736 + 0.7071)
$$

$$
E = 18 \times 9.81 \times 0.80 \times 0.8807
$$

Multiplying stepwise:

$$
18 \times 9.81 = 176.58
$$

$$
176.58 \times 0.80 = 141.264
$$

$$
141.264 \times 0.8807 \approx 124.4 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 124.4 \text{ J}}
$$

**(ii) Impact Strength (Notch Toughness) $I$:**

$$
I = \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{124.4}{80} \approx 1.555 \text{ J/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 1.56 \text{ J/mm}^2}
$$

**(iii) Ductile or Brittle?**

Since the absorbed energy $E \approx 124.4$ J is **substantially greater than 27 J** (the conventional minimum for "tough" mild steel at room temperature), and the impact strength is high ($\approx 1.56$ J/mm²), the material is classified as **DUCTILE** at the test temperature.

**Marks Distribution:** Calculation (5 marks) + Interpretation (2 marks)

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** to conduct a Charpy impact test. List **any five precautions** that must be observed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Machine Inspection:** Check that the machine is calibrated, the dial pointer returns to zero when the pendulum is freely suspended, and the bearings are free of friction.
2. **Specimen Preparation:** Machine the specimen to standard $10 \times 10 \times 55$ mm dimensions with a precisely 2 mm deep V-notch (45° included angle, 0.25 mm root radius). Inspect the notch visually for symmetry.
3. **Specimen Measurement:** Accurately measure the cross-sectional dimensions $B$ and $B'$ near the notch using a vernier caliper.
4. **Specimen Placement:** Place the specimen horizontally on the anvils with the span of 40 mm, ensuring the notch is centred mid-span and **faces away** from the striking edge.
5. **Pendulum Release:** Raise the pendulum to the standard initial angle $\alpha$ (typically 140°) and latch it. Release the latch cleanly, ensuring no additional push.
6. **Observation:** The pendulum swings, strikes the specimen, and the specimen fractures into two pieces. Note the maximum dial reading indicated by the friction pointer.
7. **Recording:** Record the dial reading (in Joules) as the impact energy $E$. If desired, perform three tests and take the average.
8. **Post-test:** Collect the fractured pieces for fractographic examination; reset the machine; verify that the pointer returns to zero.

**Precautions (3 marks — list any 5):**

1. The specimen notch must be precisely machined and free of burrs; any irregularity affects crack initiation.
2. The pendulum must be released cleanly with **no manual push** — this is a common source of error.
3. The specimen must be **centred** correctly on the anvils; misalignment leads to asymmetric loading and erroneous energy readings.
4. The **friction pointer** must slide freely on the dial — if it sticks, the energy reading will be artificially high.
5. The **striking edge radius** must match the specimen size (2 mm for sub-size, 8 mm for full-size).
6. The machine must be **rigidly bolted** to a heavy foundation; any rocking of the base causes energy loss to the foundation, falsely elevating the absorbed energy reading.
7. The operator must stand behind the **safety shield**; fractured specimen pieces can fly off at high velocity.
8. The test should be repeated **at least three times** to obtain a statistically reliable average.

---

**b)** Discuss the **ductile-to-brittle transition (DBTT)** in BCC metals, with the help of a **schematic impact energy vs. temperature curve**. Explain the significance of DBTT in engineering design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Ductile-Brittle Transition (4 marks):**

Body-centred cubic (BCC) metals such as mild steel, ferritic stainless steel, and tungsten exhibit a characteristic transition from ductile to brittle behaviour as the temperature decreases. This is known as the **Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**, also called the **Nil Ductility Temperature (NDT)** in fracture-mechanics terminology.

At **high temperatures** (above DBTT), the material has sufficient thermal activation energy to move dislocations. Plastic deformation occurs, the fracture surface is **fibrous / dull / cup-and-cone** in appearance, and the absorbed impact energy is **high** (typically > 100 J). The material behaves in a **ductile** manner.

At **low temperatures** (below DBTT), the yield stress exceeds the cleavage stress, dislocation motion is restricted, and the material fractures by **cleavage** (a low-energy, fast, brittle mechanism). The fracture surface is **bright, granular, and crystalline**, and the absorbed impact energy drops to a very low value (typically < 20 J).

The **transition region** is steep — a 50 °C change in temperature can cause the impact energy to drop by an order of magnitude in some steels. The DBTT is conventionally defined as the temperature corresponding to:
- **(i)** A specific absorbed energy level (e.g., 20 J or 27 J), **or**
- **(ii)** The mean of the upper-shelf and lower-shelf energies, **or**
- **(iii)** A specific lateral expansion (e.g., 0.38 mm) at the fracture surface.

**Significance in Engineering Design (3 marks):**

1. **Cold-climate service:** Structures in Arctic regions (pipelines, ship hulls, offshore platforms) must be made of materials whose DBTT is well **below the lowest anticipated service temperature** to avoid catastrophic brittle fracture.
2. **Pressure-vessel codes:** ASME and IS codes require that the **NDT + 17 °C ≤ Minimum Design Metal Temperature** for ferritic steel vessels.
3. **Historical lessons:** The brittle fracture of **Liberty Ships** in WWII (over 1000 failures, some splitting in half at calm seas) was traced to the high DBTT of the steel plates used — this single disaster led to the mandatory Charpy testing of ship-plate steel.
4. **Material selection:** For dynamic loading applications (bridges, railway tracks, crane hooks), the operating temperature must be on the **upper shelf** of the impact energy curve.

**Schematic Description (to be drawn on answer script):**

> A graph with **Temperature (°C)** on the X-axis and **Impact Energy (J)** on the Y-axis, showing:
> - **Upper shelf** (high energy plateau) at high temperatures,
> - **Lower shelf** (low energy plateau) at low temperatures,
> - **Transition region** (steep S-shaped curve) in between,
> - **DBTT** marked at the midpoint of the transition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Lab ESE Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Charpy and Izod set-ups** (notch direction, specimen orientation) — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the V-notch symbol "KV2"** in the record — write it explicitly: "KV2 = V-notch of 2 mm depth per ASTM E23."
> 3. **Wrong unit in final answer** — impact energy is in **Joules (J)**, NOT N·m (although dimensionally equal, KTU expects Joules).
> 4. **Skipping the size-correction factor** when given a sub-size specimen — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not mentioning air-friction / windage correction** in the procedure — examiners love this keyword.
> 6. **Drawing the energy vs. temperature curve in the wrong orientation** (energy on X-axis) — must be **temperature on X-axis, energy on Y-axis.**
> 7. **Writing "ductile-brittle transition" without mentioning BCC metals** — must specify that FCC metals (like aluminium, copper) do **not** exhibit a sharp DBTT.
> 8. **Skipping the standard reference** (ASTM E23 / IS 1757) — KTU record evaluation awards 1 mark specifically for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- [x] Charpy test measures **toughness**, not strength — distinguish clearly in viva.
- [x] Standard specimen: **$10 \times 10 \times 55$ mm** with **2 mm V-notch** (KV2).
- [x] Notch acts as a **stress concentrator** — forces crack initiation at a known location.
- [x] Specimen is placed **horizontally** with notch facing **away** from striker; **Izod** is vertical with notch facing striker.
- [x] Master formula: $E = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- [x] Impact strength = $I = E / A_n$ where $A_n = 80$ mm² for standard specimen.
- [x] Size correction factor: $K = 10 \cdot \sqrt{10 / (B \cdot B')}$ — apply only for sub-standard specimens.
- [x] Standard: **ASTM E23** (international) / **IS 1757** (Indian).
- [x] Dial reading is **directly in Joules** — no manual calculation of swing angles needed during the actual test.
- [x] **Striking velocity** is typically ~5 m/s (high strain rate ~10²–10³ s⁻¹).
- [x] **Upper shelf** = ductile fracture, fibrous surface, high energy.
- [x] **Lower shelf** = brittle fracture, crystalline/bright surface, low energy.
- [x] **DBTT** is critical for BCC metals (steels); FCC metals (Al, Cu) do not show a sharp transition.
- [x] **Liberty Ships** of WWII are the classic case-study example of why impact testing became mandatory.
- [x] Always repeat the test **at least 3 times** and report the **average** (per IS 1757).
- [x] Always **brake** the pendulum after the test — never allow it to oscillate freely (damages bearings).
- [x] Always stand behind the **safety shield** — fractured pieces can be lethal projectiles.
- [x] Air friction, windage, and bearing losses are automatically accounted for in the **machine calibration**; the student does **not** subtract them manually.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Shear Test on MS Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Double Shear Test on MS Rod

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Double Shear Test on a Mild Steel (MS) Rod** is a standardized destructive laboratory experiment conducted on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** to determine the **Ultimate Shear Strength (USS)** of a given ductile material. In this test, a cylindrical MS specimen is placed inside a specially designed **shear testing fixture (shear attachment/cage)** such that the applied tensile load is transferred to the specimen through a central block, causing the rod to be sheared simultaneously across **two distinct cross-sectional planes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCNSL407 - Module 2):** *Determination of ultimate shear strength of MS rod by double shear test on UTM.* The experiment directly maps to **CO2**: *Apply the principles of strength of materials to conduct standard material testing experiments and interpret results.*
