The **Ultimate Shear Strength (τ_u)** is defined as the maximum shear stress the material can withstand just before fracture:

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{2A} = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$

where:
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum (failure) load in **Newton (N)**
- $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ = Cross-sectional area of one shear plane in **mm²**
- $d$ = Diameter of the MS rod in **mm**
- $\tau_u$ = Ultimate shear strength in **N/mm² (MPa)**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are using a **standard paper cutter (guillotine)** to cut a single sheet of paper. The blade presses down on the paper at one location, and the paper is sheared along **one plane** — this is **single shear**.

Now, suppose you take **two sheets of paper stacked together** and use the same cutter. The blade has to cut through **two planes simultaneously** to sever the stack. Even though the blade applies the same force, the effective area resisting the cut is **doubled**, so the stress per plane is **halved** for the same applied force. This is exactly the principle behind **double shear** — the load is resisted by two shear planes working in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Engineering Examples of Double Shear:**
> - **Scissor blades** — each blade creates one shear plane, together forming double shear.
> - **Punching machine** — when a punch shears a metal plate between a die, two shear planes are involved.
> - **Riveted/bolted lap joints** with double shear configuration.
> - **Cutting pliers** used to cut wires.

---

## 1.3 Standard Test Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Specimen (KTU Lab Convention):**
> - **Material:** Mild Steel (MS) Rod
> - **Diameter (d):** Typically **20 mm** (may vary: 16 mm, 25 mm)
> - **Length:** Approximately **50–60 mm** (fits into the shear cage)
> - **Testing Machine:** **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** of capacity 400 kN / 600 kN / 1000 kN
> - **Loading Rate:** Gradual, quasi-static (≈ 1–5 mm/min crosshead speed)
> - **Standard Reference:** **IS 5242: 1979** — *Method of Test for Determining Shear Strength of Metals* (punch type)

---

## 1.4 Why "Mild Steel" Specifically?

**Mild Steel** is chosen as the specimen for KTU lab examinations due to its:

1. **Ductile behavior** — undergoes visible plastic deformation before fracture, producing a clear, observable shear failure surface.
2. **Predictable shear strength** — typical USS for MS ranges between **250 – 350 MPa**.
3. **Availability & economy** — easily available in standardized rod form.
4. **Pedagogical clarity** — the failure plane and mechanism are clean and easy to demonstrate to undergraduate students.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation: Shear Stress

When a force acts **parallel** to a surface (rather than perpendicular, as in tension), the internal resistance developed along that surface is called **shear stress**. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \frac{\text{Shear Force}}{\text{Area of Shear Plane}} = \frac{P}{A}$$

For a cylindrical rod, the area resisting the shear force is the **circular cross-sectional area**:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Double Shear Configuration

### 2.2.1 Single Shear vs Double Shear — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Single Shear | Double Shear |
|---|---|---|
| Number of resisting planes | **1** | **2** |
| Effective resisting area | $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $2A = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$ |
| Shear stress for same load P | $\tau = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | $\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$ |
| Load capacity for same τ | $P$ | $2P$ |
| Fixture design | Simpler (one outer block) | Two outer blocks + one central block |
| Real example | Lap joint with one bolt | Lap joint with two bolts in series (or central-pulled) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Examiner's Focus):** In double shear, the **same applied load P** is distributed over **two shear planes**, so the **shear stress on each plane is halved** compared to single shear. Equivalently, a double-shear joint can carry **twice the load** of a single-shear joint of the same diameter at the same shear stress.

---

## 2.3 Operational Sequence — The "How" of the Test

The experiment follows a strict, sequential procedure:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Measure the **diameter (d)** of the MS rod at three locations using a **vernier caliper** (precision 0.02 mm) or **micrometer** (precision 0.01 mm). Record the **mean diameter**.

2. **Fixture Assembly (Shear Cage / Attachment):**
   - Place the cylindrical MS specimen horizontally inside the **shear testing attachment**.
   - The fixture has a **central pulling block** that grips the rod from above via a slot, and **two outer support blocks** that support the rod from below.
   - The setup is then mounted on the UTM.

3. **Alignment:** Ensure the axis of the specimen is **perfectly vertical (coaxial)** with the UTM loading axis to avoid bending stresses.

4. **Loading:** Apply a **gradually increasing tensile load** at a constant rate. The central block moves upward, shearing the rod at the **two planes** where the central block meets the outer supports.

5. **Observation:** The rod will:
   - Initially resist elastically.
   - Yield (plastic deformation begins — visible as shearing offsets).
   - Finally **fracture along both shear planes** simultaneously, producing a sharp drop in the load indicator.

6. **Recording:** Note the **maximum load ($P_{max}$)** indicated on the UTM dial/gauge at the moment of failure.

7. **Post-Test Inspection:** Examine the **fractured surfaces** — they should appear **smooth, bright, and granular** (typical ductile shear failure), perpendicular to the rod axis.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the entire following table — these are the *only* formulas you will need for any KTU question on this experiment.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area (one plane) | $A$ | $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | mm² |
| 2 | Effective area (double shear) | $A_{eff}$ | $A_{eff} = 2A = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$ | mm² |
| 3 | Shear stress (general) | $\tau$ | $\tau = \frac{P}{A_{eff}}$ | N/mm² = MPa |
| 4 | Ultimate shear strength | $\tau_u$ | $\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{2A} = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ | MPa |
| 5 | Mean diameter | $d$ | $d = \frac{d_1 + d_2 + d_3}{3}$ | mm |
| 6 | Load per shear plane | $P_{plane}$ | $P_{plane} = \frac{P}{2}$ | N |
| 7 | Strain energy at shear (advanced) | $U$ | $U = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V$ | J (not required) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING (KTU 2024):** When writing $\tau = P/A$, you **must** use $A_{eff} = 2A$ for double shear. Using $A$ (single plane area) is the **#1 most common mistake** students make in the exam and results in **double the actual stress** — an instant loss of 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The double shear test data is **fundamental** to the design of:

- **Riveted and bolted joints** in steel structures (bridges, transmission towers).
- **Pin connections** in machinery, cranes, and linkages.
- **Shear connectors** in composite steel-concrete construction.
- **Fasteners** in aerospace and automotive industries (ISO 898, ASTM F568M standards).
- **Cutting tools and dies** — the shear strength determines the force required to punch/cut metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb:** For ductile materials (like MS), the **allowable shear stress in design** is taken as **0.4 to 0.6 times the ultimate shear strength**, depending on the factor of safety required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Ultimate Shear Strength Formula

### Step 1: Start from the fundamental definition of shear stress

Shear stress is defined as the **shear force per unit area** acting on a plane parallel to the force:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_{resisting}}$$

*Here, P is the applied shear load and $A_{resisting}$ is the area of the plane resisting the shear.*

### Step 2: Identify the resisting area for a cylindrical rod

A cylindrical rod of diameter *d*, when cut perpendicular to its axis, exposes a **circular cross-section**. The area of this circle is:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

*This is the area of a single shear plane.*

### Step 3: Apply the "double shear" condition

In a double shear configuration, the rod is sheared **simultaneously along two parallel planes**. Therefore, the **total effective area resisting the applied load** is:

$$A_{eff} = A + A = 2A = 2 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$$

*The applied load P is shared equally between the two shear planes, so each plane carries P/2.*

### Step 4: Substitute $A_{eff}$ into the shear stress equation

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_{eff}} = \frac{P}{\left(\dfrac{\pi d^2}{2}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the expression

$$\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$$

*This is the general shear stress equation for a double-shear test.*

### Step 6: Introduce the "ultimate" condition

The **ultimate shear strength ($\tau_u$)** is the shear stress corresponding to the **maximum load ($P_{max}$)** the specimen can sustain just before fracture. Substituting $P_{max}$ for P:

$$\boxed{\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}}$$

*This is the final, exam-ready formula. Memorize it.*

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):** In a double shear test on an MS rod of diameter **20 mm**, the specimen failed at a maximum load of **200 kN**. Determine the ultimate shear strength of the material.

### Given Data
- Diameter of rod: $d = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Maximum load at failure: $P_{max} = 200 \text{ kN} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area of the rod.

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A = 100 \times 3.1416 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the effective area for double shear.

$$A_{eff} = 2A = 2 \times 314.16 = 628.32 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the ultimate shear strength formula.

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{628.32 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$\tau_u = 318.31 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u \approx 318.31 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 (Verification):** Cross-check using the direct formula.

$$\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (20)^2} = \frac{400000}{1256.64} = 318.31 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 (Interpretation):** The ultimate shear strength of the given MS rod is **318.31 MPa**, which falls within the **typical range (250–350 MPa) for mild steel** — confirming the result is physically realistic.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variations of the Same Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners often "twist" the same experiment in different ways. The following matrix shows all common variants and the corresponding formula you must apply.**

| Variant | What is Given | What is Asked | Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** (most common) | $P_{max}$, $d$ | $\tau_u$ | $\tau_u = \dfrac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ |
| **Type 2** | $\tau_u$, $d$ | $P_{max}$ (load capacity) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\tau_u \cdot \pi d^2}{2}$ |
| **Type 3** | $P_{max}$, $\tau_u$ | Required diameter $d$ | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{max}}{\pi \tau_u}}$ |
| **Type 4** | $P_{max}$, $d$, factor of safety (FOS) | Safe load $P_{safe}$ | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{FOS}$ |
| **Type 5** | Diameter, allowable stress $\tau_{all}$ | Maximum allowable load | $P_{all} = \dfrac{\tau_{all} \cdot \pi d^2}{2}$ |
| **Type 6** (comparison) | Same $P_{max}$ but single shear | $\tau_u$ (single) | $\tau_u^{single} = \dfrac{4 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ (which is **2×** double shear) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Type 3 (Reverse Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A double-shear joint in a steel truss must carry a maximum load of **150 kN**. If the ultimate shear strength of the MS pin is **320 MPa**, find the required diameter of the pin.

### Solution

**Given:** $P_{max} = 150 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$, $\tau_u = 320 \text{ MPa} = 320 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 1:** Start from the ultimate shear strength formula.

$$\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $d^2$.

$$d^2 = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi \tau_u}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$d^2 = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 320} = \frac{300000}{1005.31} = 298.41 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root.

$$d = \sqrt{298.41} = 17.27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Apply engineering rounding (round up for safety).

$$\boxed{d_{adopt} = 18 \text{ mm (standard size)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Double Shear Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters). No reserved keywords used as standalone nodes. All labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["START EXPERIMENT"] --> B1["MEASURE MS ROD DIAMETER D AT 3 LOCATIONS"]
    B1 --> B2["COMPUTE MEAN DIAMETER D AVG"]
    B2 --> C1["ASSEMBLE SHEAR CAGE FIXTURE"]
    C1 --> C2["INSERT MS ROD INTO FIXTURE"]
    C2 --> C3["MOUNT FIXTURE ON UTM PLATEN"]
    C3 --> D1["ALIGN SPECIMEN AXIS WITH LOAD AXIS"]
    D1 --> E1["APPLY GRADUAL TENSILE LOAD"]
    E1 --> F1["RECORD LOAD VS DEFORMATION"]
    F1 --> G1{"SPECIMEN FAILED?"}
    G1 -- NO --> E1
    G1 -- YES --> H1["RECORD P MAX FAILURE LOAD"]
    H1 --> I1["INSPECT FRACTURE SURFACE"]
    I1 --> J1["COMPUTE AREA A PI D SQUARED BY 4"]
    J1 --> K1["COMPUTE EFFECTIVE AREA 2A"]
    K1 --> L1["CALCULATE TAU U 2 P MAX BY PI D SQUARED"]
    L1 --> M1["REPORT ULTIMATE SHEAR STRENGTH IN MPA"]
    M1 --> N1["END EXPERIMENT"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Shear Cage Fixture — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["UTM UPPER CROSSHEAD"] --> P2["CENTRAL PULLING BLOCK"]
    P2 --> P3["MS ROD SPECIMEN"]
    P3 --> P4["LEFT OUTER SUPPORT BLOCK"]
    P3 --> P5["RIGHT OUTER SUPPORT BLOCK"]
    P4 --> P6["UTM LOWER CROSSHEAD"]
    P5 --> P6
    P2 -.SHEAR PLANE 1.-> P3
    P2 -.SHEAR PLANE 2.-> P3
    P3 --> P7["TWO PARALLEL SHEAR PLANES FORM"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Force Flow and Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["APPLIED LOAD P FROM UTM"] --> F2["DISTRIBUTED EQUALLY"]
    F2 --> F3["PLANE 1 CARRIES P BY 2"]
    F2 --> F4["PLANE 2 CARRIES P BY 2"]
    F3 --> F5["STRESS TAU 1 P BY 2A"]
    F4 --> F6["STRESS TAU 2 P BY 2A"]
    F5 --> F7["TAU 1 EQUALS TAU 2 SYMMETRIC LOADING"]
    F6 --> F7
    F7 --> F8["ULTIMATE STRESS TAU U WHEN P REACHES P MAX"]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description of the Physical Test Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **The shear test fixture (also called the "shear cage" or "shear attachment") consists of three hardened steel blocks:**

```
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │   UTM UPPER CROSSHEAD       │
   └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                  │  (Tensile Load P)
                  ▼
        ┌───────────────────┐
        │  CENTRAL PULLING  │
        │      BLOCK        │
        ├───────────────────┤
        │                   │
   ═════╪═══════════════════╪═════  ← TOP SHEAR PLANE
        │    MS ROD         │
        │   (Specimen)      │
   ═════╪═══════════════════╪═════  ← BOTTOM SHEAR PLANE
        │                   │
        ├───────────────────┤
        │  LOWER SUPPORT    │
        │      BLOCK        │
        └─────────┬─────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │   UTM LOWER CROSSHEAD       │
   └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:** As the upper crosshead moves upward, it pulls the central block. The central block tries to "pull out" the middle section of the rod, while the lower block holds the lower section. The rod fails in shear along the **two interfaces** between the central block and the outer support block.

---

## 4.5 Observation Table (Standard KTU Lab Record Format)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the exact table format expected in your KTU lab record and viva voce.**

| Sl. No. | Observation | Reading 1 | Reading 2 | Reading 3 | Mean |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diameter of MS rod (mm) | — | — | — | $d_{avg}$ |
| 2 | Initial gauge length (mm) | — | — | — | $L_0$ |
| 3 | Maximum load at failure (kN) | — | — | — | $P_{max}$ |
| 4 | Nature of fracture surface | — | — | — | Smooth/Granular |

| Calculation | Value |
|---|---|
| Cross-sectional area $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | — mm² |
| Effective area $A_{eff} = 2A$ | — mm² |
| Ultimate shear strength $\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ | — MPa |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Define ultimate shear strength. With a neat sketch, explain the double shear test on an MS rod.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Ultimate shear strength is the maximum shear stress a material can withstand just before failure in a shear test. Mathematically:
> $$\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$
> where $P_{max}$ is the failure load and $d$ is the diameter of the rod.
>
> **Test Description (2 marks):** In the double shear test, the MS rod is placed in a **shear cage fixture** having a central block and two outer support blocks. The fixture is mounted on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**. As the upper crosshead moves up, the central block pulls the rod while the lower block holds it. The rod fails in shear along **two parallel planes** simultaneously, and the **failure load $P_{max}$** is recorded. The ultimate shear strength is then calculated using the formula above.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between single shear and double shear tests.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Shear Test | Double Shear Test |
|---|---|---|
| Number of shear planes | One | Two |
| Resisting area | $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $2A = \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$ |
| Shear stress formula | $\tau = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | $\tau = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$ |
| Load capacity for same $\tau$ | $P$ | $2P$ |
| Fixture design | Two blocks (one above, one below) | Three blocks (central + two outer) |
| Typical application | Lap joint with one bolt | Central-pulled pin, scissor blades |

> **Key Statement (1 mark):** In a double shear test, the **same load is distributed over two planes**, so the **shear stress is halved** compared to single shear, or equivalently, the joint can carry **twice the load** for the same diameter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Full derivations, diagrams, and step-by-step calculations are mandatory.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of conducting a double shear test on an MS rod using a Universal Testing Machine. List the apparatus and materials required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a double shear test, an MS rod of diameter **25 mm** failed at a maximum load of **300 kN**. Calculate the ultimate shear strength of the material. Also find the load-carrying capacity of the same rod in a **single shear** configuration at the same ultimate shear strength. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Aim (½ mark):** To determine the ultimate shear strength of a given MS rod by conducting a double shear test on a UTM.

**2. Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — 400 kN / 600 kN capacity
- Shear testing attachment (shear cage with central + 2 support blocks)
- Mild Steel rod specimen (diameter ~20–25 mm, length ~60 mm)
- Vernier caliper / Micrometer (precision 0.02 mm / 0.01 mm)

**3. Theory (1 mark):** When the rod is subjected to double shear, it fails along two parallel planes. The ultimate shear strength is given by:
$$\tau_u = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$

**4. Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Measure the diameter of the MS rod at **three different locations** along its length using a vernier caliper. Compute the **mean diameter (d)**.
2. Place the rod inside the **shear cage fixture** such that the central block straddles the middle portion and the two outer support blocks hold the ends.
3. Mount the entire assembly on the UTM, ensuring the specimen axis is **coaxial with the loading axis**.
4. Apply a **gradually increasing tensile load** at a steady rate.
5. Observe the load indicator continuously. The load will rise, then **drop sharply** at the moment of fracture.
6. Note the **maximum load ($P_{max}$)** indicated on the dial/gauge at the point of failure.
7. Inspect the fractured surfaces — they should be **smooth, bright, and perpendicular** to the rod axis (characteristic of ductile shear failure).

**5. Observation & Calculation (1 mark):** Record $P_{max}$ and $d$ in the observation table. Calculate $\tau_u$ using the formula.

**6. Result (½ mark):** The ultimate shear strength of the given MS rod = ___ MPa.

**7. Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the schematic in Section 4.4 of these notes.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Diameter of rod: $d = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Maximum load: $P_{max} = 300 \text{ kN} = 300 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional area (½ mark).**
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 625}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate effective area for double shear (½ mark).**
$$A_{eff} = 2A = 2 \times 490.87 = 981.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate ultimate shear strength (1 mark).**
$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{300 \times 10^3}{981.75} = 305.58 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u \approx 305.58 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate load capacity in single shear at the same $\tau_u$ (1 mark).**

In single shear, the resisting area is $A = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$ (only one plane). Therefore:
$$P_{single} = \tau_u \times A = 305.58 \times 490.87$$

$$P_{single} = 150,000 \text{ N} = 150 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Verification and interpretation (1 mark).**
$$\frac{P_{max}^{double}}{P_{single}} = \frac{300}{150} = 2$$

This confirms that the **double-shear configuration can carry exactly twice the load** of the single-shear configuration for the same diameter and material — a **key design advantage** of double-shear joints.

$$\boxed{P_{single} = 150 \text{ kN}}$$

**Final Answer (½ mark):** $\tau_u = 305.58 \text{ MPa}$; Single-shear load capacity = **150 kN**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** What are the differences between a single shear test and a double shear test? Why is the double shear test preferred in design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-shear pin joint in a structural assembly must transmit a maximum load of **250 kN**. If the ultimate shear strength of the MS pin material is **310 MPa** and a factor of safety of **2.5** is to be applied, determine:
  (i) the required diameter of the pin, and
  (ii) the safe working load on the pin if the diameter is standardized to **25 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** Shear tests are conducted to determine the resistance of a material to shear-type loading. The two standard configurations are single shear and double shear.

**2. Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Shear | Double Shear |
|---|---|---|
| Shear planes | 1 | 2 |
| Resisting area | $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi d^2}{2}$ |
| Shear stress | $\frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | $\frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$ |
| Load for same $\tau$ | $P$ | $2P$ |
| Fixture complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| Failure mechanism | One clean shear | Two simultaneous shears |

**3. Why Double Shear is Preferred (3 marks):**

(i) **Higher load capacity:** For the same pin diameter and material, a double-shear joint can carry **twice the load** of a single-shear joint — leading to more compact and economical designs.

(ii) **Symmetric loading:** The two shear planes are symmetric about the central block, resulting in **balanced force distribution** and **minimal bending moment** on the pin. This reduces stress concentration and improves fatigue life.

(iii) **Reduced pin bending:** In single-shear lap joints, the offset load creates a **bending moment** on the pin, causing combined shear and bending stresses. Double-shear eliminates this eccentricity.

(iv) **Greater design safety:** The redundancy of two shear planes provides a fail-safe mechanism — even if one plane is partially compromised, the other can continue to carry load.

(v) **Compact connection:** Double-shear connections are used in heavy structural applications (bridges, cranes, transmission towers) where space optimization is critical.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Maximum load: $P_{max} = 250 \text{ kN} = 250 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$
- Ultimate shear strength: $\tau_u = 310 \text{ MPa} = 310 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Factor of Safety: $FOS = 2.5$

**Step 1: Calculate allowable shear stress (1 mark).**
$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\tau_u}{FOS} = \frac{310}{2.5} = 124 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

#### Part (b)(i): Required Pin Diameter (3 marks)

**Step 1: Write the design equation (½ mark).**
$$P_{max} = \tau_{allow} \times A_{eff} = \tau_{allow} \times \frac{\pi d^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for $d$ (½ mark).**
$$d^2 = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values (1 mark).**
$$d^2 = \frac{2 \times 250 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 124} = \frac{500000}{389.56} = 1283.55 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate $d$ (1 mark).**
$$d = \sqrt{1283.55} = 35.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Round up to standard size (½ mark).**
$$\boxed{d_{adopt} = 36 \text{ mm (standard)}}$$

---

#### Part (b)(ii): Safe Working Load for Standardized 25 mm Pin (3 marks)

**Given:** Standardized diameter $d = 25 \text{ mm}$, $FOS = 2.5$, $\tau_u = 310$ MPa.

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional area (½ mark).**
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate effective area for double shear (½ mark).**
$$A_{eff} = 2A = 981.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate safe working load (1 mark).**
$$P_{safe} = \frac{\tau_u \times A_{eff}}{FOS} = \frac{310 \times 981.75}{2.5} = \frac{304,341.5}{2.5}$$

$$P_{safe} = 121,736.6 \text{ N} \approx 121.74 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Check against the required 250 kN (1 mark).**
$$P_{safe} = 121.74 \text{ kN} < 250 \text{ kN} \text{ (required)}$$

$$\boxed{P_{safe} = 121.74 \text{ kN — INSUFFICIENT; a larger pin diameter is required.}}$$

**Conclusion (½ mark):** A 25 mm pin is **not safe** for the 250 kN load. The minimum required diameter is **36 mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using A instead of 2A (THE #1 MISTAKE):** Students often write $\tau = \frac{P}{A}$ where $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$, forgetting that double shear has **two** planes. The correct formula is $\tau = \frac{P}{2A}$. **This single error doubles your answer and costs 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kN with mm² without converting to N. Always use **N and mm²** to get **N/mm² = MPa**, or use **kN and m²** to get **kN/m² = kPa**. KTU strictly requires consistent SI units.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\pi$:** Numerical answers without $\pi$ are **wrong**. Always retain $\pi$ symbolically and substitute its value (3.1416) only in the final step.
>
> 4. **Confusing "Failure Load" with "Working Load":** The load obtained from the UTM is the **failure (ultimate) load**, not the working load. To find the working/safe load, divide by the **Factor of Safety**.
>
> 5. **Missing the Diagram:** A 7-mark question on the test procedure **mandates a sketch** of the shear cage fixture. Without it, expect a deduction of **1–2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Reporting USS in the wrong range:** If your calculated $\tau_u$ is, say, 50 MPa or 1500 MPa, it is **definitely wrong** for mild steel (range: 250–350 MPa). Sanity-check your numerical answer.
